Ayahs

الٓر ۚ تِلْكَ ءَايَـٰتُ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ ٱلْمُبِينِ (١)
Aif, Lam, Ra. These are the signs of the Manifest Book. (1)
إِنَّآ أَنزَلْنَـٰهُ قُرْءَٰنًا عَرَبِيًّا لَّعَلَّكُمْ تَعْقِلُونَ (٢)
Indeed We have sent it down as an Arabic Quran so that you may exercise your reason. (2)
نَحْنُ نَقُصُّ عَلَيْكَ أَحْسَنَ ٱلْقَصَصِ بِمَآ أَوْحَيْنَآ إِلَيْكَ هَـٰذَا ٱلْقُرْءَانَ وَإِن كُنتَ مِن قَبْلِهِۦ لَمِنَ ٱلْغَـٰفِلِينَ (٣)
We will recount to you the best of narratives in what We have revealed to you of this Quran, and indeed prior to it you were among those who are unaware [of it]. (3)

Commentary

The Best of Stories is Before You

This Surah also begins with the Disconnected Letters (al-Muqatta'at): Alif-Lam-Ra. These are a sign of the magnificence of the Quran and the composition of these deep and content-rich verses from the simplest components, namely the letters of the alphabet.

Regarding the Disconnected Letters of the Quran, we have already discussed them sufficiently in three instances (at the beginning of Surahs Al-Baqarah, Al-Imran, and Al-A'raf), and there is no need for repetition; we have established their indication of the Quran's greatness.

Perhaps it is for this reason that immediately after mentioning the Disconnected Letters, it refers to the greatness of the Quran, saying: "These are the verses of the Clear Book"—a book that illuminates, distinguishes truth from falsehood, and shows the Straight Path and the way to victory and salvation (تِلْكَ آياتُ الْكِتابِ الْمُبِينِ).

It is noteworthy that in this verse, the distal demonstrative pronoun (tilka - "those") is used, similar to what we saw at the beginning of Surah Al-Baqarah and some other Surahs. We mentioned that such expressions allude to the loftiness of these verses; they are so high and exalted that it is as if they are situated at a distant point, at the peak of the heavens, in the depths of infinite space, requiring great effort and endeavor to reach—unlike mundane matters that one encounters at every step.

(We have similar expressions in Persian literature where, in the presence of a high-ranking person, one says: "That Excellency..." or "That honorable authority...").

Then, it describes the purpose of the revelation of these verses as follows: "Indeed, We have sent it down as an Arabic Quran so that you may understand" (إِنَّا أَنْزَلْناهُ قُرْآناً عَرَبِيًّا لَعَلَّكُمْ تَعْقِلُونَ).

The goal is not merely recitation, chanting, or seeking blessings (tabarruk) by reading its verses; rather, the ultimate goal is understanding—a powerful and rich understanding that invites the entirety of a human's being toward action.

As for the Quran being in Arabic, in addition to the fact that the Arabic language—according to those who study the various languages of the world—is such a vast language that it can serve as the interpreter for the tongue of revelation and convey the concepts and nuances of God's words, it is also certain that Islam dawned from the Arabian Peninsula, from a center of darkness, ignorance, and barbarism. Primarily, it had to gather the people of that region around itself; it had to be so eloquent and clear that it could educate those illiterate individuals far from science and knowledge, transform them under its teaching, and create a core nucleus for the influence of this religion in other regions of the world.

Of course, the Quran in this language is not directly understandable to all people of the world (and it would have been the same in any other language), because we do not have a single global language that everyone understands. However, this does not prevent other people of the world from benefiting from its translations or, even better, by gradually becoming familiar with this language, touching the verses themselves and perceiving the concepts of revelation from within these very words.

In any case, the expression "being Arabic," which is repeated in ten places in the Quran, is a response to those who accused the Prophet of learning these verses from a non-Arab (Ajam) individual, claiming the content of the Quran was an imported thought and did not spring from the essence of revelation.

Furthermore, these successive expressions create a duty for all Muslims: everyone should strive to learn Arabic as a universal second language, as it is the language of revelation and the key to understanding the realities of Islam.

Then He says: "We relate to you the best of stories through what We have revealed to you of this Quran, although before it you were among the unaware" (نَحْنُ نَقُصُّ عَلَيْكَ أَحْسَنَ الْقَصَصِ بِما أَوْحَيْنا إِلَيْكَ هذَا الْقُرْآنَ وَ إِنْ كُنْتَ مِنْ قَبْلِهِ لَمِنَ الْغافِلِينَ).

Some commentators believe that "The Best of Stories" (Ahsan al-Qasas) refers to the Quran in its entirety, considering the phrase "through what We have revealed to you of this Quran" as evidence for this. "Qissah" (story) here does not only mean a tale; rather, etymologically, it means to search for the traces of something. Anything that follows one after another is called "Qissah" by the Arabs. Since words and sentences are expressed successively when explaining a subject, this act is called "Qissah."

In any case, Allah has named the entirety of this Quran—which combines the most beautiful explanation and the most eloquent and articulate words with the highest and deepest meanings, being outwardly beautiful and exceptionally sweet and inwardly very rich in content—as "The Best of Stories."

In several narrations, we see this expression used for the Quran as a whole, even if these narrations were not specifically intended as a commentary on this particular verse (take note).

For example, in a hadith narrated by Ali ibn Ibrahim from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, we read: "And the best of stories is this Quran" (و احسن القصص هذا القرآن) [1]. In the book Rawdat al-Kafi, in a sermon by the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, it is narrated: "Indeed, the best of stories, the most reaching of exhortations, and the most beneficial of reminders is the Book of Allah, Exalted is His mention" (ان احسن القصص و ابلغ الموعظة و انفع التذكر كتاب اللَّه عز ذكره) [2]. However, the connection between the upcoming verses—which detail the life of Yusuf—and the verse under discussion is such that the human mind is more inclined toward the meaning that Allah has named the story of Yusuf "The Best of Stories." Indeed, for many, when studying the opening verses of this Surah, nothing else comes to mind.

But as we have said many times, there is no obstacle to these verses expressing both meanings: the Quran in general is the "Best of Stories," and the story of Yusuf is specifically so.

Why should this story not be the best? For in its thrilling passages, it depicts the most sublime lessons of life.

In this story, we clearly observe the sovereignty of God's Will over all things.

We see with our own eyes the grim fate of the envious and witness their plots coming to naught.

We see the disgrace of immodesty and the grandeur and glory of chastity and piety manifested within its lines.

The scene of a young child's loneliness at the bottom of a well, the days and nights of an innocent prisoner in a dark dungeon, the manifestation of the light of hope from behind the dark curtains of despair, and finally the grandeur and glory of a vast government resulting from awareness and trustworthiness—all march before the human eye in this story.

Moments when the fate of a nation is transformed by a meaningful dream, and the life of a people is saved from destruction through the awareness of a vigilant, divine leader—dozens of other great lessons are reflected in this story. Why should it not be the "Best of Stories"?!

However, the story of Yusuf being the "Best of Stories" is not enough on its own; the important thing is that there must be such merit within us that we can accommodate all these great lessons in our souls.

There are many who still look at the story of Yusuf as a mere interesting love affair, like cattle looking at a lush and flowery garden only as a handful of grass to satisfy hunger.

And there are still many who, by adding false embellishments to this story, try to turn it into a sexual saga. This is due to a lack of merit and capacity in the recipient; otherwise, the original story encompasses all kinds of high human values. In the future, God willing, we will see that one cannot easily pass by the comprehensive and beautiful passages of this story. As the sweet-spoken poet said, sometimes before the attractions of this story, "the scent of the flower intoxicates a person so much that the hem of the garment slips from the hand!"

The Role of Stories in Human Life

Given that a very important part of the Quran is presented in the form of the history of past nations and the stories of predecessors, the question arises for some: why does a book of education and human-building contain so much history and storytelling?

However, attention to a few points clarifies the true reason for this:

  1. History is the laboratory of the various issues of human life. What a person depicts in their mind through rational arguments, they find objectively in the pages of history. Considering that the most reliable knowledge is that which has a sensory aspect, the role of history in showing the realities of life can be well understood.

A person sees with their own eyes in the pages of history the deadly defeat that overtakes a nation due to discord and division, and similarly, the brilliant victory of another nation under the shadow of unity and solidarity.

History, with its silent tongue, recounts the definitive and undeniable results of the schools of thought, methods, and programs of every nation and group.

The stories of the predecessors are a collection of their most valuable experiences, and we know that the product of life is nothing but experience.

History is a mirror that reflects the full stature of human societies: the ugliness, the beauty, the successes, the failures, the victories, the defeats, and the factors behind each of these matters.

For this reason, studying the history of the predecessors lengthens a person's life—exactly by the length of their lives! Because it places the collection of their lifetime experiences at one's disposal.

It is for this reason that Ali عليه السلام, in his historic words within his testament to his noble son, says: "O my son! Even though I have not lived the ages of those before me, I have looked into their deeds, thought about their news, and traveled through their remains until I became like one of them. Rather, it is as if, through what has reached me of their affairs, I have lived with the first of them to the last of them!" [1] Of course, this refers to history that is free from superstitions, fabrications, flattery, and distortions—but unfortunately, such histories are few. The role of the Quran in presenting examples of authentic history should not be overlooked.

A history that is like a clear mirror, not a distorted one! A history that does not merely mention events but also seeks out roots and consequences.

With this in mind, why should the Quran, which is a supreme book of education, not rely on history in its chapters and passages and bring examples and witnesses from the stories of the predecessors?

  1. Beyond this, history and stories have a special attraction, and humans throughout all stages of life—from childhood to old age—are under the influence of this extraordinary attraction.

For this reason, a significant part of world literature and a large portion of the works of writers consist of history and stories.

The best works of great poets and writers, whether Persian-speaking or otherwise, are their stories: Saadi's Gulistan, Ferdowsi's Shahnameh, Nizami's Khamsa, and the attractive works of contemporary writers. Likewise, the thrilling works of Victor Hugo (French), Shakespeare (English), and Goethe (German) are all presented in the form of stories.

Whether a story is in the form of poetry or prose, or in the form of a play or film, it leaves an impact on the reader and viewer that rational arguments are never capable of achieving.

The reason for this may be that a human is sensory before being rational, and is immersed in sensory matters more than they think about intellectual issues.

The various issues of life, the further they move from the field of sense and take on an abstract intellectual aspect, become heavier, more difficult, and harder to digest.

Hence, we see that to establish rational arguments, help is always sought from sensory examples. Sometimes, mentioning a suitable and timely example multiplies the impact of the argument several times over. Therefore, successful scholars are those who have greater mastery over choosing the best examples.

And why should it not be so, when rational arguments are ultimately interpretations of sensory, objective, and empirical matters?

  1. Stories and history are understandable to everyone, unlike rational arguments where not everyone is equal! For this reason, a book that has a general and universal aspect—which everyone from the illiterate, semi-savage desert Arab to the great philosopher and thinker must use—must certainly rely on history, stories, and examples.

The combination of these factors shows that the Quran, in expressing all this history and storytelling, has followed the best path from the perspective of education and training.

Especially considering that the Quran never mentions historical events in a bare and naked fashion; rather, at every step, it draws a conclusion and makes an educational utilization of it, as you will soon see examples of in this very Surah.

[^1]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 2, p. 49.

[^3]: Nahj al-Balagha, Letter 31 to Imam Hasan al-Mujtaba (peace be upon him).

Ayahs

إِذْ قَالَ يُوسُفُ لِأَبِيهِ يَـٰٓأَبَتِ إِنِّى رَأَيْتُ أَحَدَ عَشَرَ كَوْكَبًا وَٱلشَّمْسَ وَٱلْقَمَرَ رَأَيْتُهُمْ لِى سَـٰجِدِينَ (٤)
When Joseph said to his father, ‘Father! I saw eleven planets, and the sun and the moon: I saw them prostrating themselves before me,’ (4)
قَالَ يَـٰبُنَىَّ لَا تَقْصُصْ رُءْيَاكَ عَلَىٰٓ إِخْوَتِكَ فَيَكِيدُوا۟ لَكَ كَيْدًا ۖ إِنَّ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنَ لِلْإِنسَـٰنِ عَدُوٌّ مُّبِينٌ (٥)
he said, ‘My son, do not recount your dream to your brothers, lest they should devise schemes against you. Satan is indeed man’s manifest enemy. (5)
وَكَذَٰلِكَ يَجْتَبِيكَ رَبُّكَ وَيُعَلِّمُكَ مِن تَأْوِيلِ ٱلْأَحَادِيثِ وَيُتِمُّ نِعْمَتَهُۥ عَلَيْكَ وَعَلَىٰٓ ءَالِ يَعْقُوبَ كَمَآ أَتَمَّهَا عَلَىٰٓ أَبَوَيْكَ مِن قَبْلُ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ وَإِسْحَـٰقَ ۚ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ عَلِيمٌ حَكِيمٌ (٦)
That is how your Lord will choose you, and teach you the interpretation of dreams, and complete His blessing upon you and upon the house of Jacob, just as He completed it earlier for your fathers, Abraham and Isaac. Your Lord is indeed all-knowing and all-wise.’ (6)

Commentary

A Glimmer of Hope and the Beginning of Hardships!

The Quran begins the story of Yusuf with his strange and meaningful dream,## Commentary

A Glimmer of Hope and the Beginning of Hardships!

The Quran begins the story of Yusuf with his strange and meaningful dream, as this dream is considered the first chapter of Yusuf's turbulent life.

One morning, with excitement and enthusiasm, he went to his father and unveiled a new event that seemingly wasn't very significant, but in reality, it announced the start of a new chapter in his life.

"Yusuf said to his father: O my father! Last night in a dream I saw eleven stars descending from the sky, and the sun and the moon were accompanying them; they all came to me and prostrated before me" (إِذْ قالَ يُوسُفُ لِأَبِيهِ يا أَبَتِ إِنِّي رَأَيْتُ أَحَدَ عَشَرَ كَوْكَباً وَ الشَّمْسَ وَ الْقَمَرَ رَأَيْتُهُمْ لِي ساجِدِينَ‌).

Ibn Abbas says: Yusuf saw this dream on the night of Friday, which coincided with the Night of Qadr (the night of determining destinies and decrees).

Regarding how old Yusuf was when he saw this dream, some have written nine years, some twelve, and some seven; what is certain is that he was very young at that time.

It is noteworthy that the phrase "I saw" (رأيت) is repeated in this verse for emphasis and decisiveness, indicating: "I am not like many people who forget part of their dream and speak of it with doubt and hesitation; I saw with certainty and conviction that eleven stars and the sun and the moon prostrated before me, and I have no doubt in this matter."

Another point is that the pronoun "hum" (هم), which is used for a plural group of rational masculine beings, is used for the sun, moon, and stars, as is the word "sajidin" (ساجدين).

This indicates that their prostration was not a coincidental matter, but rather it was evident that they prostrated intentionally, like rational and conscious beings.

Of course, it is clear that the meaning of prostration here is humility and submission; otherwise, prostration in the form of the usual human prostration does not make sense regarding the sun, moon, and stars.

This exciting and meaningful dream plunged the Prophet Ya'qub into deep thought: The sun, the moon, and the stars of the sky? And eleven stars at that? They descended and prostrated before my son Yusuf? How meaningful! Surely the sun and the moon are myself and his mother (or myself and his maternal aunt), and the eleven stars are his brothers. My son's status and rank will rise so high that the stars of the sky and the sun and the moon will rub their foreheads at his threshold; he will become so dear and honorable in the sight of Allah that the celestial beings will humble themselves before him. What a magnificent and interesting dream!

Therefore, in a tone mixed with worry and anxiety yet accompanied by joy, he said to his son: "O my son, do not relate your vision to your brothers" (قالَ يا بُنَيَّ لا تَقْصُصْ رُؤْياكَ عَلى‌ إِخْوَتِكَ‌).

"Lest they plot a dangerous scheme against you" (فَيَكِيدُوا لَكَ كَيْداً).

I know that "Satan is an open enemy to man" (إِنَّ الشَّيْطانَ لِلْإِنْسانِ عَدُوٌّ مُبِينٌ‌).

He is waiting for an excuse to begin his whisperings, to fan the flames of malice and envy, and even to set brothers against one another.

Interestingly, Ya'qub did not say: "I fear the brothers might intend ill toward you," but rather he stated it as a certain matter, especially with the repetition of "kayd" (plot) which serves as emphasis, because he was aware of the temperaments of his other sons and knew their sensitivity toward Yusuf. Perhaps the brothers were also not ignorant of dream interpretation; furthermore, this was a dream whose interpretation was not very complex.

On the other hand, this dream did not seem like a typical childhood dream; a child might dream of the moon and stars, but for the moon and stars to prostrate before him like rational and intelligent beings—this is not a childish dream. For these reasons, it was justified for Ya'qub to be fearful of the fire of envy being ignited in the brothers toward Yusuf.

However, this dream did not only signify the greatness of Yusuf's status in the future from a worldly and material perspective; rather, it showed that he would also reach the station of Prophethood, as the prostration of the celestial beings is evidence of his high spiritual rank. Thus, his father Ya'qub added: "And thus your Lord will choose you" (وَ كَذلِكَ يَجْتَبِيكَ رَبُّكَ‌). "And He will teach you the interpretation of dreams" (وَ يُعَلِّمُكَ مِنْ تَأْوِيلِ الْأَحادِيثِ‌) [1] "and complete His favor upon you and upon the family of Ya'qub" (وَ يُتِمُّ نِعْمَتَهُ عَلَيْكَ وَ عَلى‌ آلِ يَعْقُوبَ‌).

"Just as He completed it before upon your fathers, Ibrahim and Ishaq" (كَما أَتَمَّها عَلى‌ أَبَوَيْكَ مِنْ قَبْلُ إِبْراهِيمَ وَ إِسْحاقَ‌).

Indeed, "your Lord is All-Knowing, All-Wise" (إِنَّ رَبَّكَ عَلِيمٌ حَكِيمٌ‌).

[1]: "Ta'wil" (تأويل) originally means returning something to its origin, and whenever an action or speech reaches its final goal, it is called ta'wil. The external realization of a dream is also an instance of ta'wil. "Ahadith" (أحاديث) is the plural of "hadith," meaning the narration of an event; since a person narrates their dream to others, it is used here as an allusion to dreams.

Key Points

1- Visions and Dreaming

The issue of visions and dreaming has always been one of the subjects that has attracted the attention of both ordinary people and scientists from various perspectives.

What are these scenes—ugly and beautiful, terrifying and pleasant, joyful and sorrowful—that man sees in sleep?!

Are they related to the past, nested in the depths of the human soul, manifesting as changes and transformations? Or are they related to the future, where the sensitive receiving apparatus of the human soul captures images of future events through a mysterious connection? Or are there various types, some related to the past, some to the future, and some the result of unfulfilled desires and wishes?

The Quran is explicit in several verses that at least some dreams are a reflection of the near or distant future.

In the story of Yusuf's dream which we read in the above verses, as well as the story of the prisoners' dreams in verse 36 of this surah, and the story of the dream of the Aziz of Egypt which will come in verse 43, we encounter several examples of dreams that have unveiled future events. Some of these events were relatively distant, like Yusuf's dream which is said to have been realized after forty years, and some occurred in the nearer future, like the dreams of the Aziz of Egypt and Yusuf's cellmates.

In other than this surah, reference is made to other "interpretable dreams," such as the vision of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله mentioned in Surah Al-Fath, and the dream of Ibrahim mentioned in Surah As-Saffat (this dream was both a Divine command and had an interpretation).

Interestingly, in a narration from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, we read: "Visions and dreams are of three types: sometimes they are glad tidings from Allah, sometimes a means of grief from Satan, and sometimes matters that a person contemplates in his mind and then sees in his sleep." [1] It is clear that Satanic dreams are not something that have an interpretation, but Rahmani (divine) dreams that have the aspect of glad tidings must certainly be dreams that unveil a joyful event in the future.

In any case, it is necessary here to briefly mention the various opinions expressed regarding the reality of visions:

Regarding the reality of visions, many interpretations have been given, which can be divided into two parts:

1- Materialistic Interpretation. 2- Spiritual Interpretation.

Materialists say dreaming can have several causes:

A- Dreaming and visions may be the direct result of a person's daily activities; that is, what happened to the person in previous days becomes visualized before their mind during sleep.

B- A series of unfulfilled desires may cause one to see certain dreams, just as a thirsty person sees water in a dream and someone waiting for a traveler sees their return in a dream (as the old saying goes: "The camel sees cottonseed in its dream!").

C- Fear of something may cause a person to dream about it, as it has been repeatedly experienced that those who are terrified of thieves dream of thieves at night (the famous proverb "Sleep far from the camel and do not see disturbed dreams" refers to this very fact).

Freud and the followers of the Freudian school have another type of materialistic interpretation for dreams: Through detailed premises, they state that: Dreams and visions consist of the gratification of repressed and suppressed desires that always come to the field of consciousness with changes and transformations to deceive the "ego."

To explain: After accepting the premise that the human psyche consists of two parts—the "conscious part" (related to daily thoughts and voluntary knowledge and choices) and the "unconscious part" (what is hidden in the subconscious as an unfulfilled desire)—they say: It often happens that desires we have, which for some reason we could not satisfy and which have taken place in our subconscious, come to the conscious stage for a type of imaginary saturation during sleep when the conscious system stops working. Sometimes they are reflected without change (like a lover seeing their lost beloved in the world of dreams) and sometimes they change form and are reflected in suitable images, in which case they require interpretation.

Therefore, "visions" are always related to the past and never give news of the future; they can only be a good means for reading the "unconscious mind," and for this reason, in the treatment of psychological illnesses that rely on discovering the subconscious, the patient's dreams are often utilized.

Some nutritional scientists believe in a relationship between "dreams and visions" and "the body's nutritional needs," believing that, for example, if a person sees in a dream that blood is dripping from their teeth, their body must be low on Vitamin C! And if they see in a dream that the hair on their head has turned white, it shows they are suffering from a deficiency of Vitamin B!!

However, spiritual philosophers have another interpretation for dreams; they say dreams and visions are of several types:

1- Dreams related to the past life and desires and wishes, which constitute a significant portion of human dreams.

2- Disturbed and incomprehensible dreams that are the result of the activity of imagination and fancy (even though they may have psychological motives).

3- Dreams that relate to the future and bear witness to it.

There is no doubt that dreams related to past life and the personification and visualization of scenes a person has seen throughout their life do not have a specific interpretation. Likewise, disturbed dreams, so-called "confused dreams" (adghathu ahlam), which are the result of scattered thoughts—similar to the thoughts a person has during fever and delirium—cannot have a specific interpretation regarding future life issues, even though psychologists and psychoanalysts use them as a window to reach the human subconscious and consider them a key to treating psychological illnesses. Therefore, their dream interpretation is for discovering the secrets of the soul and the source of illnesses, not for discovering future life events.

As for dreams related to the future, they also have two branches: some are explicit and clear dreams that do not require any interpretation at all and sometimes, to one's utter amazement, are realized in the near or distant future without the slightest difference.

The second type are dreams that, while recounting future events, have changed form due to specific mental and spiritual factors and require interpretation.

There are many examples for each of these dreams that cannot all be denied; not only are examples mentioned in religious sources and history books, but they have occurred repeatedly in our own private lives or the lives of those we know, to such an extent that they can never all be considered the result of coincidence.

Here we mention a few examples of dreams that have unveiled future events in an extraordinary way, which we have heard from trustworthy individuals:

1- One of the famous and completely reliable scholars of Hamadan, the late Akhund Mulla Ali, narrated from the late Agha Mirza Abdun-Nabi, who was one of the great scholars of Tehran: "When I was in Samarra, every year an amount of about one hundred tomans was sent to me from Mazandaran. Based on this, when I felt a need, I would take loans beforehand, and upon the arrival of that money, I would pay off all my debts. One year I was informed that the crop situation had been very bad this year and therefore no money would be sent! I became very upset and went to sleep with this troubled thought. Suddenly, in a dream, I saw the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله who called me and said: 'So-and-so! Rise and open that cupboard (he pointed to a cupboard), and there are one hundred tomans in it; take them.' I woke up; not long after, there was a knock at the door. It was afternoon. I saw it was the messenger of the late Mirza-ye Shirazi, the great Marja' of the Shiites. He said: 'The Mirza wants you.' I wondered why that great man wanted me at this time. I went and saw him sitting in his room (I had completely forgotten my dream). Suddenly, the late Mirza-ye Shirazi said to me: 'Mirza Abdun-Nabi, open that cupboard; there are one hundred tomans there, take them.' Immediately, the story of the dream came to my mind and I was greatly astonished by this event. I wanted to say something, but I felt he did not wish for anything to be said in this regard. I took the money and came out."

2- A trustworthy friend narrated that the author of the book "Rayhanat al-Adab," the late Tabrizi, had a son whose right hand was ailing (perhaps he had severe rheumatism) such that he could hardly hold a pen. It was decided he would go to Germany for treatment. He says: "While I was on the ship, I dreamed that my mother had passed away. I opened my calendar and wrote down the event, specifying the day and hour. Not long after, I came to Iran. A group of relatives came to welcome me; I saw they were wearing black clothes. I was surprised, and the dream had completely slipped my mind. Finally, they gradually made me understand that my mother had died. Immediately I remembered the dream; I took out the calendar and asked for the day of her death. I saw that my mother had passed away exactly on that same day!"

3- The famous Islamic writer Sayyid Qutb writes in his commentary "Fi Zilal al-Quran" under the verses related to Surah "Yusuf": "If I were to deny everything you have said about visions, I could never deny an event that happened to me while I was in America. There, I saw in a dream that my nephew's eyes were filled with blood and he was unable to see (my nephew was in Egypt with the rest of my family). I was terrified by this; I immediately wrote a letter to my family in Egypt and specifically asked about my nephew's eye condition. Not long after, the reply reached me; they wrote that his eye had suffered an internal hemorrhage and he was unable to see, and was currently undergoing treatment. It is noteworthy that the internal bleeding of his eye was such that it was not visible in a normal observation and could only be seen with medical instruments, yet he had been deprived of sight. I even saw this internal bleeding clearly in the dream!"

Dreams that have unveiled secrets and discovered truths related to the future or hidden truths related to the present are more than even skeptical individuals can deny or attribute to coincidence.

By inquiring from your close friends, you can often find examples of such dreams. These types of dreams can never be interpreted through the "materialistic interpretation of visions" and can only be explained through the interpretation of spiritual philosophers and belief in the independence of the soul. Therefore, they can be used collectively as evidence for the independence of the soul. [1]

2- Ya'qub Interpreted His Son's Dream Summarily

In the verses under discussion, we read that Ya'qub, in addition to warning his son against relating his dream to his brothers, interpreted the dream for him in summary, telling him that he would be chosen by God, taught the interpretation of dreams, and that His favor would be completed upon him and the family of Ya'qub.

The indication of Yusuf's dream that he would reach great spiritual and material stations in the future is quite understandable, but the question arises: how was the matter of Yusuf's future knowledge of dream interpretation discovered by Ya'qub from Yusuf's vision? Was this a coincidental piece of news that Ya'qub gave to Yusuf regardless of his dream, or did he discover it from that very dream of Yusuf?

It appears that Ya'qub understood this matter from Yusuf's dream, and this could have been in one of two ways:

First, Yusuf, at that very young age, narrated this dream privately and away from the eyes of his brothers to his father (since the father advised him to strive to keep it secret), and this shows that Yusuf himself had a specific feeling about his dream such that he did not bring it up in the group. The existence of such a feeling in a young child like Yusuf is evidence that he has a type of spiritual readiness for discovering the interpretation of dreams, and the father felt that by nurturing this readiness, he would gain extensive knowledge in this field in the future.

Second, the connection of Prophets with the world of the unseen was through several ways: sometimes through heart-felt inspirations, sometimes through the descent of the angel of revelation, and sometimes through dreams. Although Yusuf had not yet reached the station of Prophethood at that time, the occurrence of such a meaningful dream for Yusuf showed that in the future he would connect with the world of the unseen through this path, and naturally, he must know the interpretation and meaning of dreams to be able to have such a connection.

3- The Lesson of Keeping Secrets

One of the lessons this section of verses gives us is the lesson of keeping secrets, which must sometimes be practiced even toward brothers. There are always secrets in a person's life which, if revealed, might endanger his future or that of his society. Self-restraint in keeping these secrets is one of the signs of breadth of soul and strength of will. How many are the individuals who, due to weakness in this area, have endangered their own destiny or that of a society, and how many are the troubles that arise in life for a person due to the failure to keep secrets.

In a narration from Imam Ali ibn Musa al-Rida عليه السلام, we read: "A believer will not be a [true] believer until he possesses three characteristics: a Sunnah (practice) from his Lord, a Sunnah from his Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, and a Sunnah from his Wali (Imam) عليه السلام. As for the Sunnah from his Lord, it is the keeping of secrets; as for the Sunnah from his Prophet, it is being courteous with people; and as for the Sunnah from his Wali, it is patience in times of hardship and adversity." (Of course, keeping secrets here refers mostly to keeping the secrets of others). [1] And in a narration from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read: "Your secret is like your blood; it must not flow except through your own veins." [2]

[1]: Bihar al-Anwar, New Edition, Vol. 78, p. 334. [2]: Safinat al-Bihar (Entry: K-T-M).

Ayahs

۞ لَّقَدْ كَانَ فِى يُوسُفَ وَإِخْوَتِهِۦٓ ءَايَـٰتٌ لِّلسَّآئِلِينَ (٧)
In Joseph and his brothers there are certainly signs for the seekers. (7)
إِذْ قَالُوا۟ لَيُوسُفُ وَأَخُوهُ أَحَبُّ إِلَىٰٓ أَبِينَا مِنَّا وَنَحْنُ عُصْبَةٌ إِنَّ أَبَانَا لَفِى ضَلَـٰلٍ مُّبِينٍ (٨)
When they said, ‘Surely Joseph and his brother are dearer to our father than [the rest of] us, though we are a hardy band. Our father is indeed in manifest error.’ (8)
ٱقْتُلُوا۟ يُوسُفَ أَوِ ٱطْرَحُوهُ أَرْضًا يَخْلُ لَكُمْ وَجْهُ أَبِيكُمْ وَتَكُونُوا۟ مِنۢ بَعْدِهِۦ قَوْمًا صَـٰلِحِينَ (٩)
‘Kill Joseph or cast him away into some [distant] land, so that your father’s attention may be exclusively towards you, and thereafter you may become a righteous lot.’ (9)
قَالَ قَآئِلٌ مِّنْهُمْ لَا تَقْتُلُوا۟ يُوسُفَ وَأَلْقُوهُ فِى غَيَـٰبَتِ ٱلْجُبِّ يَلْتَقِطْهُ بَعْضُ ٱلسَّيَّارَةِ إِن كُنتُمْ فَـٰعِلِينَ (١٠)
One of them said, ‘Do not kill Joseph, but cast him into the recess of a well so that some caravan may pick him up, if you are to do [anything].’ (10)

Commentary

The Final Plan is Devised

From here begins the account of the conflict between Joseph’s brothers and Joseph:

In the first verse, referring to the many instructive lessons contained within this story, it says: Verily, in the story of Joseph and his brothers, there were signs for the seekers (لَقَدْ كانَ فِي يُوسُفَ وَ إِخْوَتِهِ آياتٌ لِلسَّائِلِينَ).

Regarding who these "seekers" (questioners) are, some commentators (such as al-Qurtubi in al-Jami' and others) have said that these seekers were a group of Jews from Medina who were asking the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله questions on this subject. However, the apparent meaning of the verse is general, stating: "For all individuals searching for truth, there are signs, symbols, and lessons hidden within this story."

What lesson could be greater than this: a group of powerful individuals, driven by calculated plans born of envy, exert all their efforts to destroy a seemingly weak and lonely individual; yet, through those very actions and without realizing it, they place him upon the throne of power and make him the ruler of a vast land, and in the end, they all bow down before him in humility. This shows that when Allah wills a matter, He can implement it even through the hands of its opponents, so it may become clear that a pure and faithful person is never alone; even if the entire world girds its loins to destroy him, if Allah does not will it, they cannot harm a single hair on his head!

Jacob عليه السلام had twelve sons, two of whom—Joseph and Benjamin—were from the same mother, named Rachel. Jacob showed more affection toward these two sons, especially Joseph, because, firstly, they were considered his youngest children and naturally required more protection and affection. Secondly, according to some narrations, their mother Rachel had passed away, making them more in need of love for this reason as well. Beyond that, signs of genius and extraordinary character were particularly evident in Joseph. The combination of these factors caused Jacob to openly express greater fondness for them.

The envious brothers, ignoring these reasons, became deeply upset by this matter, especially since a natural rivalry likely existed among them due to having different mothers. Thus, they sat together "and said: Joseph and his brother are more beloved to our father than we are, even though we are a powerful and capable group" who manage our father's life well; for this reason, his affection for us should be greater than for these young children who are capable of nothing. (إِذْ قالُوا لَيُوسُفَ وَ أَخُوهُ أَحَبُّ إِلى‌ أَبِينا مِنَّا وَ نحنُ عُصْبَةٌ) [1]. In this way, with their one-sided judgment, they condemned their father and said:

"Indeed, our father is in manifest error!" (إِنَّ أَبانا لَفِي ضَلالٍ مُبِينٍ).

The fire of malice and envy did not allow them to consider all aspects of the matter or understand the reasons for their father's display of affection toward these two children. This is because a person's specific interests often cast a veil over their thoughts, leading them to one-sided judgments that result in straying from the path of truth and justice.

Of course, they did not mean religious or theological error, as future verses show they believed in their father's greatness and prophethood; they were only criticizing him regarding his manner of social conduct.

The feeling of envy finally drove the brothers to devise a plan. They gathered and proposed two suggestions, saying: "Either kill Joseph or cast him out to a distant land so that your father’s affection may be focused solely on you!" (اقْتُلُوا يُوسُفَ أَوِ اطْرَحُوهُ أَرْضاً يَخْلُ لَكُمْ وَجْهُ أَبِيكُمْ).

[1]: "Usbah" (عصبه) refers to a group or party that joins hands and is coordinated in performing a task. This word has a collective meaning and has no singular form.

It is true that by doing this, you will feel guilt and the shame of conscience, for you have committed this crime against your young brother, but compensation for this sin is possible; you will repent "and after that, you will become a righteous people!" (وَ تَكُونُوا مِنْ بَعْدِهِ قَوْماً صالِحِينَ).

Another possibility suggested in the commentary of this last phrase is that they meant: after removing Joseph from your father's sight, your relations with your father will become rectified, and the distress you felt in this regard will vanish. However, the first interpretation seems more correct.

In any case, this phrase is evidence that they felt the guilt of this act and had some fear of Allah deep in their hearts; for this reason, they proposed repentance after committing the sin.

But the important point here is that speaking of repentance before committing a crime is, in reality, a way to deceive the conscience and open the path toward sin; it is by no means evidence of regret or remorse.

In other words, true repentance is when a state of remorse and shame arises in a person after the sin. However, talking about repentance before the sin is not repentance.

To explain: it often happens that when a person decides to sin, they are confronted by their conscience, or religious beliefs create a barrier before them, preventing their progress toward the sin. To bypass this barrier easily and open their way to the sin, they deceive their conscience and belief with this statement: "Immediately after committing the sin, I will set out to compensate; I won't just sit idly by. I will repent, go to the door of Allah's mercy, perform righteous deeds, and eventually wash away the effects of the sin!"

That is to say, just as one devises a satanic plan to commit a sin, they also devise a satanic plan to deceive the conscience and overcome their religious beliefs. Often, this satanic plan proves effective, and they remove that firm barrier from their path using this means. Joseph's brothers entered through this very door.

Another point is that they said after removing Joseph, your father's attention and gaze will be toward you (يَخْلُ لَكُمْ "وَجْهُ" أَبِيكُمْ) and they did not say your father's heart will be in your possession (يخل لكم "قلب" ابيكم), because they were not certain that the father would soon forget his son Joseph. It was enough for them that the father's outward attention be upon them.

There is also the possibility that the face and eyes are the windows to the heart; when the father's gaze is directed toward them, his heart will gradually follow.

However, among the brothers, there was one who was the most intelligent or the most conscientious. For this reason, he opposed the plan to kill Joseph and the plan to exile him to a distant land where there was fear of death. He offered a third plan and said:

"If you must do something, do not kill Joseph, but rather cast him into the bottom of a well (in such a way that he remains safe) so that some wayfarers and caravans may find him and take him away" and he will be removed from our sight and our father's (قالَ قائِلٌ مِنْهُمْ لا تَقْتُلُوا يُوسُفَ وَ أَلْقُوهُ فِي غَيابَتِ الْجُبِّ يَلْتَقِطْهُ بَعْضُ السَّيَّارَةِ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ فاعِلِينَ).

Key Points

[What is the meaning of "Jubb"?]

1- "Jubb" (جب) refers to a well that has not been lined with stones, and perhaps most desert wells are like this. "Ghayabat" (غيابت) refers to a hidden place inside the well that is concealed from view. This expression seemingly refers to something common in desert wells: at the bottom of the well, near the water level, they create a small shelf-like area inside the wall of the well so that if someone goes down, they can sit on it and fill the vessel they brought without entering the water. Naturally, when looking from the top of the well, this spot is not visible, and for this reason, it is called "Ghayabat" [1]. Such wells also exist in our environment.

2- Undoubtedly, the intention of this proposer was not to hurl Joseph into the well in a way that he would be destroyed, but rather the goal was for him to be placed in the hidden part of the well so that he would reach the caravans safely.

3- From the phrase "if you must do so" (إِنْ كُنْتُمْ فاعِلِينَ), it is understood that this speaker did not even present this suggestion as a definitive one; perhaps he preferred that no plan be devised against Joseph at all.

4- There is discussion among commentators regarding the name of this individual. Some have said his name was "Reuben," who was considered the most intelligent, while some have mentioned "Judah" and others "Levi."

[1]: Adapted from Tafsir al-Manar under the verse.

- The Destructive Role of Envy in Human Life

Another important lesson we learn from this story is how envy can drive a person to the point of killing a brother or creating severe troubles for him, and how, if this internal fire is not controlled, it burns both others and the person himself.

Fundamentally, when a blessing reaches another and the person himself remains deprived of it, four different states may arise within him:

First, he wishes to have what others have. This state is called "Ghibtah" (emulation/healthy competition) and is a praiseworthy state because it drives a person toward constructive effort and has no destructive effect on society.

Second, he wishes for that blessing to be stripped from others and exerts effort toward this end. This is the very reprehensible state of "Hasad" (envy), which drives a person toward destructive efforts regarding others without making any constructive effort for himself.

Third, he wishes to possess that blessing himself while others remain deprived of it. This is the state of "Bukhl" (stinginess) and monopolization, where a person wants everything for himself and takes pleasure in the deprivation of others.

Fourth, he loves for others to be in blessing, even if he himself lives in deprivation, and is even willing to place what he has at the disposal of others and overlook his own interests. This lofty state is called "Ithar" (self-sacrifice), which is one of the most important and prominent human qualities.

In any case, envy did not only drive Joseph's brothers to the point of killing their brother, but sometimes envy drives a person to their own destruction as well.

For this reason, shocking expressions are found in Islamic hadiths to combat this vile trait.

As an example: it is narrated from the Noble Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله that he said: Allah prohibited Moses son of Imran from envy and said to him:

"The envious person is angry at My blessings upon My servants, and blocks the portions I have distributed among My servants. Whoever is like that, he is not from Me and I am not from him." [1] We read from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام:

"The blight of religion is envy, vanity, and boasting."

And in another hadith from the same Imam عليه السلام, we read:

"The believer emulates (ghibtah) and does not envy, while the hypocrite envies and does not emulate." [2]

[The Expression of Affection by Parents Toward Children]

6- This lesson can also be learned from this part of the story: that parents must exercise extraordinary care in expressing affection toward their children.

Although Jacob عليه السلام undoubtedly did not commit an error in this regard, and the expression of affection he showed toward Joseph and his brother Benjamin was based on reasons we previously mentioned, this event nonetheless shows that one must be even more sensitive and strict in this matter than necessary. This is because sometimes a single expression of affection toward one child creates such a complex in the heart of another child that it drives them to any extreme. They see their own personality so shattered that they recognize no boundaries in destroying their brother's personality.

Even if they cannot show a reaction, they consume themselves from within and sometimes fall victim to mental illness. I do not forget the young child of a friend who was ill and naturally needed more affection; the father had turned the older brother into a sort of servant for him. It wasn't long before the older son was afflicted with an unknown mental illness. I said to that dear friend: "Don't you think the source of this was the lack of justice in expressing affection?" He did not believe this and consulted a skilled psychiatrist. The doctor told him: "Your son has no specific disease; the source of his illness is that he has suffered from a lack of affection and his personality has been damaged, while the younger brother has received all this love." Therefore, we read in Islamic hadiths:

One day Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام said: "I sometimes express affection toward some of my children, sit them on my lap, give them the best portion of the meat, and put sugar in their mouths, while I know the right is with another. But I do this so that they are not provoked against my other children, lest they do what the brothers of Joseph did to Joseph." [1]

[1]: Usul al-Kafi, Vol. 2, p. 307. [2]: Usul al-Kafi, Vol. 2, p. 307. [1]: Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 74, p. 78.

Ayahs

قَالُوا۟ يَـٰٓأَبَانَا مَا لَكَ لَا تَأْمَ۫نَّا عَلَىٰ يُوسُفَ وَإِنَّا لَهُۥ لَنَـٰصِحُونَ (١١)
They said, ‘Father! Why is it that you do not trust us with Joseph? We are indeed his well-wishers. (11)
أَرْسِلْهُ مَعَنَا غَدًا يَرْتَعْ وَيَلْعَبْ وَإِنَّا لَهُۥ لَحَـٰفِظُونَ (١٢)
Let him go with us tomorrow so that he may eat lots of fruits and play, and we will indeed take [good] care of him.’ (12)
قَالَ إِنِّى لَيَحْزُنُنِىٓ أَن تَذْهَبُوا۟ بِهِۦ وَأَخَافُ أَن يَأْكُلَهُ ٱلذِّئْبُ وَأَنتُمْ عَنْهُ غَـٰفِلُونَ (١٣)
He said, ‘It really upsets me that you should take him away, and I fear the wolf may eat him while you are oblivious of him.’ (13)
قَالُوا۟ لَئِنْ أَكَلَهُ ٱلذِّئْبُ وَنَحْنُ عُصْبَةٌ إِنَّآ إِذًا لَّخَـٰسِرُونَ (١٤)
They said, ‘Should the wolf eat him while we are a hardy band, then we will indeed be losers!’ (14)

Commentary

A Sinister Setup

After the brothers of Joseph (Yusuf) approved the final plan to cast him into the well, they became preoccupied with how to separate Joseph from his father. Therefore, they devised another scheme for this task. Approaching their father with an air of righteousness and using soft, gentle language mixed with a type of pitiable criticism, they "said: 'O our father! Why is it that you do not trust us with Joseph, while we are indeed his sincere well-wishers?'" (قَالُوا يَا أَبَانَا مَا لَكَ لَا تَأْمَنَّا عَلَىٰ يُوسُفَ وَإِنَّا لَهُ لَنَاصِحُونَ).

"Come now, cease this behavior that makes us appear accused. Furthermore, our brother is a youth; he has a heart too, and he needs to enjoy the fresh air outside the city and engage in appropriate recreation. Confining him to the house is not right. 'Send him with us tomorrow that he may enjoy himself and play,' [1] and if you are concerned for his safety, 'we will indeed be his guardians and protectors,' for he is our brother, as dear to us as our own lives!" (أَرْسِلْهُ مَعَنَا غَدًا يَرْتَعْ وَيَلْعَبْ وَإِنَّا لَهُ لَحَافِظُونَ).

In this manner, they skillfully arranged the plan to separate their brother. It is quite possible they spoke these words in front of Joseph himself, so that he too might plead with his father and ask for permission to go to the countryside.

This plan, on one hand, placed the father in a dilemma: if he did not entrust Joseph to them, it would imply he was accusing them. On the other hand, it enticed Joseph with the prospect of fun, recreation, and an excursion outside the city.

Indeed, such are the plans of those who wish to strike a surprise blow; they utilize every psychological and emotional factor to present themselves as being in the right. However, people of faith, by the decree of:

Al-Mu'minu kaysun (المؤمن كيس)

"The believer is sharp-witted," must never be deceived by these beautiful appearances, even if they are proposed by a brother!

In response to the brothers' statements, Ya'qub (Jacob), without accusing them of ill intent,

[^1]: "Yarta'" (يرتع) is derived from the root "rat'" (رتع), which originally means the grazing and abundant eating of animals, but is sometimes used for humans to mean recreation and eating and drinking in abundance.

stated that his reluctance to let Joseph go with them stemmed from two reasons. First: "Indeed, it saddens me that you should take him away" (قَالَ إِنِّي لَيَحْزُنُنِي أَنْ تَذْهَبُوا بِهِ).

And second, that in the surrounding wilderness there might be bloodthirsty wolves: "and I fear lest the wolf should devour him while you are heedless of him" (وَأَخَافُ أَنْ يَأْكُلَهُ الذِّئْبُ وَأَنْتُمْ عَنْهُ غَافِلُونَ).

It was entirely natural that the brothers might become preoccupied with themselves during such a trip and neglect their brother, and in that "wolf-infested wilderness," a wolf might take Joseph's life.

Of course, the brothers had no answer for the father's first reason, as the grief and sorrow of separation from Joseph was not something they could compensate for. In fact, perhaps this expression further fueled the fire of the brothers' envy.

On the other hand, this reason of the father had, from one perspective, an answer that hardly needed mentioning: eventually, for a child to grow and develop, he must inevitably separate from his father. If he wishes to remain like a young sapling constantly in the shadow of the tree of his father's existence, he will not grow. For the perfection of his child, a father must necessarily submit to this separation. Today it is recreation and fun, tomorrow the pursuit of knowledge, and the day after, business and the struggle for life; ultimately, separation is necessary.

Therefore, they did not address the response to this argument at all, but instead went to the second reason, which in their view was significant and fundamental. "They said: 'If the wolf should devour him while we are a strong group, then indeed we would be losers and wretched!'" (قَالُوا لَئِنْ أَكَلَهُ الذِّئْبُ وَنَحْنُ عُصْبَةٌ إِنَّا إِذًا لَخَاسِرُونَ).

Meaning: Are we dead that we would sit by and watch a wolf devour our brother? Aside from the bonds of brotherhood that compel us to protect him, we have a reputation among the people. What would people say about us, other than that a group of strong, robust men sat by and watched a wolf attack their brother? How could we ever live among people again?!

They also responded to the father's remark that they might become preoccupied with play and neglect Joseph, by stating that the issue is one of "loss" (khusran)—the loss of all their capital and reputation. It is not a matter where recreation or play could make one heedless of Joseph, for in that case, we would be incompetent individuals fit for nothing.

Here, the question arises: why did Ya'qub point specifically to the danger of a wolf attack among all possible dangers?

Some say: The wilderness of Canaan was a wolf-infested area, and therefore the danger was felt most from that direction.

Others have said that this was because of a dream Ya'qub had previously seen, in which wolves attacked his son Joseph. It is also possible that Ya'qub spoke metaphorically, referring to wolf-like humans, such as some of Joseph's brothers.

In any case, through whatever trickery and deceit—especially by stimulating Joseph's pure emotions and encouraging him toward recreation outside the city, which was perhaps the first such opportunity for Joseph—they managed to force the father to surrender and gained his agreement for this task in any way possible.

Here, several living lessons derived from this part of the story should be noted

1. Enemy Conspiracies in the Garb of Friendship Usually, enemies never enter the field to strike a blow explicitly and without cover. Rather, to catch the other party off guard and deprive them of any chance for defense, they hide their actions in deceptive attire. Joseph's brothers hid the plan for his death or exile under the cover of the highest brotherly feelings and emotions—emotions that were both stimulating for Joseph and seemingly acceptable to the father.

This is the same method we encounter on a large scale in our daily lives. The hard and heavy blows we have received from sworn enemies through this path are not few. Sometimes under the name of economic aid, sometimes under the title of cultural relations, sometimes in the guise of defending human rights, and sometimes under the title of defense pacts, they imposed the worst shameful colonial contracts upon oppressed nations, including us. Yet, with all these historical experiences, we must have enough intelligence and insight not to be optimistic toward the expressions of affection and emotions of these bloodthirsty wolves who show themselves in the clothing of compassionate humans. We have not forgotten that the dominant powers of the world, under the name of sending doctors and medicine to some war-torn African countries, sent weapons and ammunition to their mercenaries, and under the cover of diplomats, ambassadors, and attachés, dispatched their most dangerous spies to various regions of the world.

Under the name of military advisors and trainers for modern and complex weapons, they took all military secrets with them, and under the name of technicians and technical experts, they guided economic conditions toward the dependent models they desired.

Is all this historical experience not enough for us to never be deceived by these beautiful false wrappings, and to see the true face of these wolves behind these seemingly human masks?

2. The Innate and Natural Human Need for Healthy Recreation Interestingly, the Prophet Ya'qub عليه السلام gave no answer to his sons' argument regarding Joseph's need for an excursion and recreation, and practically accepted it. This itself is proof that no sound intellect can deny this innate and natural need.

A human is not like an iron machine from which they can extract work as much as they want; rather, he has a soul and spirit that becomes tired just like his body. Just as the body needs rest and sleep, the soul and spirit need healthy recreation and fun.

Experience has also shown that if a person continues monotonous work, his output and efficiency gradually decrease due to a lack of vitality. Conversely, after a few hours of healthy recreation and fun, such a zest for work is created in him that both the quantity and quality of work increase. For this reason, the hours spent on recreation and fun are an aid to the hours of work.

In Islamic narrations, this reality is beautifully stated as a command. Where Ali عليه السلام says: "The life of a believer is summarized in three parts: a part in which he engages in spiritual matters and communes with his Lord, a part in which he thinks of providing and repairing his livelihood, and a part he allocates to being free for pleasures that are lawful and decent." [1]

[^1]: Nahj al-Balagha, Short Sayings, Saying 390.

Interestingly, in another hadith, this sentence is added: "And that is an aid for the other hours."

As some have said, recreation and fun are like servicing and oiling the wheels of a machine; although this machine stops for an hour for this task, it later gains a new power, strength, and energy that will compensate for it several times over, and additionally, it will increase the life of the machine.

But the important thing is that the recreation and fun be "healthy"; otherwise, it does not solve a problem but adds to the problems. How many unhealthy recreations there are that crush a person's spirit and nerves such that they take away the power of work and activity for a long time, or at least reduce the output of their work to a minimum.

It is also noteworthy that in Islam, so much importance is given to the issue of healthy recreation that Islam has permitted a series of competitions even with wagering, and history says that some of these competitions were held in the presence of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله himself and under his judging and supervision.

He would even sometimes place his own special camel at the disposal of his companions for a riding competition.

In a narration from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read that he said: "When the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله was returning from Tabuk, he arranged a riding competition among his companions. Usama, who was riding the Prophet's famous camel named 'al-Ghadhba,' took the lead. Because the camel belonged to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, the people shouted: 'The Messenger of Allah has taken the lead!' But the Prophet shouted: 'Usama has taken the lead and won!'" (Pointing out that the rider is what matters, not the mount, for many a swift mount falls into the hands of an unskilled person and achieves nothing). [1]

Another point is that just as Joseph's brothers exploited the interest of a human—especially a youth—in excursions and recreation to reach their goal, in today's world, the mysterious hands of the enemies of truth and justice also make extensive misuse of the issue of sports and recreation to poison the thoughts of the younger generation. One must be alert so that wolf-like superpowers do not implement their sinister plans among the youth under the guise of sports and regional or global competitions.

We do not forget that during the era of the "Taghut" (tyrant), when they wanted to implement specific plans and sell the country's important capital and resources to foreigners for a pittance, they would arrange a series of extensive and lengthy sports competitions and keep the people so preoccupied with these games that they could not attend to the fundamental issues occurring in their society.

3. The Child in the Shadow of the Father Although the intense love of parents for a child requires them to keep him always by their side, it is clear that the philosophy of this love, according to the law of creation, is that very unstinting support for the child when he needs it. For this reason, at older ages, this support must be reduced, and the child must be allowed to step toward independence in life. For if he remains like a young sapling forever in the shadow of a massive tree, he will not find the necessary growth and development.

[^1]: Safinat al-Bihar, Vol. 1, p. 596.

Perhaps this was why Ya'qub, despite all the affection he had for Joseph, was prepared to separate him from himself and send him outside the city. Although this matter was very heavy for Ya'qub, Joseph's interest and his independent growth required that he gradually be allowed to spend hours and days away from his father.

This is an important educational issue that many parents are heedless of, raising their children as "pampered favorites" such that they are never able to live outside the umbrella of parental protection. They fall to their knees before a storm of life, and the pressure of events strikes them to the ground. It is for this same reason that many great personalities were those who lost their parents in childhood and were raised in a self-made manner amidst a multitude of problems.

The important thing is for parents to pay attention to this important educational issue, and for false affections not to prevent them from regaining their independence.

Interestingly, this issue is seen instinctively in some animals; for example, chicks initially stay under the mother's wings, and the mother defends them against every incident like her own sweet life.

But when they grow a bit larger, the mother not only withdraws her support from them but if they come to her, she drives them away sharply with her beak—meaning: go and learn the way and custom of independent life; how long do you want to live dependently and non-independently? You are "someone" for yourselves too!

In any case, this subject never contradicts the issue of kinship ties and the preservation of affection and love; rather, it is a deep love and a calculated bond based on the interests of both parties.

4. Neither Retribution nor Accusation Before the Crime In this segment of the story, we clearly observe that although Ya'qub was aware of the brothers' jealousy toward Joseph—and for this reason ordered him to keep his strange dream hidden from them—he was never willing to accuse them, saying "lest you have some ill intent regarding my son Joseph." Rather, his excuse was only the inability to bear Joseph's absence and the fear of the wilderness wolves.

Ethics, human standards, and the principles of just judgment also require that as long as signs of wrongdoing have not appeared from someone, they should not be accused. The principle is innocence, purity, and integrity, unless the contrary is proven.

5. Suggestion to the Enemy Another point is that in a narration regarding the above verses, we read from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله: "Do not suggest [an excuse] to a liar, for he will then lie to you; for the sons of Ya'qub did not know that a wolf eats a human until their father suggested it to them!" [1]

This points out that sometimes the opposing party is not mindful of an excuse or a pretext or choosing a deviant path. You must be careful that you yourself, with the various possibilities you mention, do not show him the deviant paths.

[^1]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 2, p. 415.

This is exactly like when a person sometimes tells his young child, "Do not hit your ball against the lamp." The child, who until then did not know it was possible to hit the ball against the lamp, becomes aware that such a thing is possible, and subsequently his curiosity is piqued: "I must see what happens if I hit the ball against the lamp!" Following that, he begins to test this matter—a test that will lead to the breaking of the lamp.

This is not just a simple matter regarding children; on the level of a large society, sometimes deviant commands and prohibitions cause people to learn many things they did not know, and then the temptation to test them begins. In such cases, as much as possible, issues should be raised in general terms so that no "bad teaching" occurs.

Of course, the Prophet Ya'qub spoke these words to his children out of purity and sincerity of heart, but the misguided children misused their father's statement.

Similar to this is the method we encounter in many writings where, for example, someone wants to speak about the harms of "narcotics" or "masturbation," and they describe these issues so vividly or show scenes of them through film that the unaware become acquainted with the secrets and mysteries of these acts. Then, they forget the points mentioned in condemnation of these acts and the way to be saved from them. For this reason, the harm and bad teaching of these writings and films are often far more than their benefit.

  1. The final point is that Joseph's brothers said: "If the wolf should devour our brother despite our presence, we would be losers," pointing out that when a person accepts a responsibility, he must stand by it until his last breath; otherwise, he will lose his capital—the capital of personality, the capital of reputation and social standing, and the capital of conscience.

How is it possible for a person to have an awake conscience and a high personality, and be committed to his social reputation and dignity, and yet shirk the responsibilities he has accepted and remain indifferent toward them?

Ayahs

فَلَمَّا ذَهَبُوا۟ بِهِۦ وَأَجْمَعُوٓا۟ أَن يَجْعَلُوهُ فِى غَيَـٰبَتِ ٱلْجُبِّ ۚ وَأَوْحَيْنَآ إِلَيْهِ لَتُنَبِّئَنَّهُم بِأَمْرِهِمْ هَـٰذَا وَهُمْ لَا يَشْعُرُونَ (١٥)
So when they took him away and conspired to put him into the recess of a well, We revealed to him, ‘[A day will come when] you will surely inform them about this affair of theirs while they are not aware [of your identity].’ (15)
وَجَآءُوٓ أَبَاهُمْ عِشَآءً يَبْكُونَ (١٦)
In the evening, they came weeping to their father. (16)
قَالُوا۟ يَـٰٓأَبَانَآ إِنَّا ذَهَبْنَا نَسْتَبِقُ وَتَرَكْنَا يُوسُفَ عِندَ مَتَـٰعِنَا فَأَكَلَهُ ٱلذِّئْبُ ۖ وَمَآ أَنتَ بِمُؤْمِنٍ لَّنَا وَلَوْ كُنَّا صَـٰدِقِينَ (١٧)
They said, ‘Father! We had gone racing and left Joseph with our things, whereat the wolf ate him. But you will not believe us even if we spoke truly.’ (17)
وَجَآءُو عَلَىٰ قَمِيصِهِۦ بِدَمٍ كَذِبٍ ۚ قَالَ بَلْ سَوَّلَتْ لَكُمْ أَنفُسُكُمْ أَمْرًا ۖ فَصَبْرٌ جَمِيلٌ ۖ وَٱللَّهُ ٱلْمُسْتَعَانُ عَلَىٰ مَا تَصِفُونَ (١٨)
And they produced sham blood on his shirt. He said, ‘No, your souls have made a matter seem decorous to you. Yet patience is graceful, and Allah is my resort against what you allege.’ (18)

Commentary

A Scandalous Lie!

Finally, the brothers triumphed and convinced their father to send Joseph with them. That night, they slept with pleasant thoughts, knowing that tomorrow their plan regarding Joseph would be implemented, and they would remove this troublesome brother from their path forever. Their only concern was that their father might regret his decision and change his mind.

In the morning, they came to their father, and he repeated the necessary instructions for Joseph's protection and care. They expressed their obedience, and in their father's presence, they treated Joseph with great respect and affection as they set out.

It is said that the father escorted them to the city gate, took Joseph from them one last time, pressed him to his chest, and tears flowed from his eyes. Then he entrusted Joseph to them and parted ways. However, Jacob’s eyes continued to follow his children as long as they were in sight; they, too, did not cease their caressing and kindness toward Joseph as long as their father could see them. But once they were certain the father could no longer see them, their pent-up emotions suddenly burst, and they poured all the malice they had accumulated over years of envy onto Joseph’s head. They began striking him from all sides, and he sought refuge from one to another, but they gave him no shelter!

In a narration, we read that during this storm of calamity while Joseph was weeping, or when they intended to cast him into the well, Joseph suddenly began to laugh. The brothers were deeply astonished, wondering why he would laugh at such a time, as if he were taking the matter as a joke, unaware of the misery awaiting him. But he revealed the secret of this laughter and taught everyone a great lesson, saying:

"I do not forget the day I looked at you, my powerful brothers, with those strong arms and extraordinary physical strength, and I felt happy. I said to myself: 'One who has such powerful helpers and supporters, what grief shall he have from harsh events?' That day, I relied on you and set my heart on your strength. Now, I am caught in your clutches, and I seek refuge from you in you, yet you give me no shelter. Allah has given you power over me so that I may learn this lesson: not to rely on anyone other than Him—not even on my brothers."

In any case, the Quran says: "So when they took him away and unanimously decided to put him into the depths of the well, they inflicted upon him whatever oppression and tyranny were possible for this task" (فَلَمَّا ذَهَبُوا بِهِ وَ أَجْمَعُوا أَنْ يَجْعَلُوهُ فِي غَيابَتِ الْجُبِ‌) [1]. The word "أجمعوا" (unanimously decided) indicates that all the brothers were in agreement on this plan, even if they had not agreed on killing him.

Fundamentally, "أجمعوا" is derived from the root "جمع," meaning to gather, and in these contexts, it refers to the gathering of opinions and thoughts.

Then it adds: At this moment, We sent revelation to Joseph, comforting him and saying, do not grieve, "A day will come when you will inform them of all these evil plots, while they do not recognize you" (وَ أَوْحَيْنا إِلَيْهِ لَتُنَبِّئَنَّهُمْ بِأَمْرِهِمْ هذا وَ هُمْ لا يَشْعُرُونَ‌).

That same day when you are seated upon the throne of power, and your brothers stretch out the hand of need toward you, and like thirsty ones searching for a pleasant spring in a scorching desert, they come to you with utmost humility and lowliness. But you will have ascended so high that they will not believe you are their brother. On that day, you will say to them: "Did you not do such and such to your younger brother Joseph?" And on that day, how ashamed and regretful they will be.

This divine revelation—by the evidence of verse 22 of this same Surah—was not the revelation of prophethood, but rather an inspiration to Joseph's heart so that he would know he was not alone and that he had a Protector and Guardian. This revelation sprinkled the light of hope upon Joseph's heart and expelled the darkness of despair and hopelessness from his soul and spirit.

Joseph's brothers carried out the plan they had devised for him exactly as they wanted, but eventually, they had to think of a way to return so that their father would believe Joseph had perished naturally, and not through a conspiracy, in order to draw their father's affections toward themselves. The plan they devised to achieve this goal was to enter through the very path the father feared and had predicted, claiming that a wolf had eaten Joseph, and creating fake evidence for it.

The Quran says: "And they came to their father at night, weeping" (وَ جاؤُ أَباهُمْ عِشاءً يَبْكُونَ‌).

False and fabricated weeping; this shows that fake crying is possible, and one cannot be deceived by weeping eyes alone!

The father, who was impatiently awaiting the arrival of his beloved son Joseph, was deeply shaken by a single glance at their group and not seeing Joseph among them. He trembled and inquired about his condition. They said: "O our father, we went racing (horse riding, archery, and the like) and left Joseph, who was small and unable to compete with us, with our belongings. We became so preoccupied with this that we forgot everything, even our brother, and at this moment, a ruthless wolf arrived and devoured him!" (قالُوا يا أَبانا إِنَّا ذَهَبْنا نَسْتَبِقُ وَ تَرَكْنا يُوسُفَ عِنْدَ مَتاعِنا فَأَكَلَهُ الذِّئْبُ‌).

"But we know you will never believe our words, even if we are truthful," because you had already made such a prediction, and you will attribute this to an excuse (وَ ما أَنْتَ بِمُؤْمِنٍ لَنا وَ لَوْ كُنَّا صادِقِينَ‌).

The brothers' words were very calculated. First, they addressed their father with the word "يا أَبانا" (O our father), which has an emotional aspect. Second, it is natural for strong brothers in such a recreational spot to engage in racing and entertainment and leave the younger brother to guard the belongings. Furthermore, to catch their father off guard, they took the initiative and said with those same weeping eyes: "You will never believe us, even if we tell the truth."

And to provide a living sign to their father, "they brought his shirt stained with false blood" (blood they had taken from a goat, a lamb, or a deer) (وَ جاؤُ عَلى‌ قَمِيصِهِ بِدَمٍ كَذِبٍ‌). But since a liar has no memory, and since a true event has various connections with the circumstances and issues surrounding it—which can rarely all be organized in a fabricated arrangement—the brothers were negligent of the fact that they should have at least torn Joseph's shirt in several places to serve as evidence of a wolf's attack. They brought the brother's shirt, which they had removed from his body intact and undamaged, bloodied to their father. The alert and experienced father, as soon as his eyes fell upon that shirt, understood everything and said:

You are lying, "rather, your souls have enticed you to something" and have devised these satanic plots (بَلْ سَوَّلَتْ لَكُمْ أَنْفُسُكُمْ أَمْراً).

In some narrations, we read that he took the shirt, turned it over, and cried out: "Then why are there no marks of a wolf's teeth or claws on it?" According to another narration, he threw the shirt over his face, cried out, shed tears, and said: "What a kind wolf this was, that it ate my son but did not cause the slightest damage to his shirt!" Then he fainted and fell to the ground like a dry piece of wood. Some of the brothers cried out: "Woe to us from the court of Allah's justice on the Day of Resurrection; we have lost our brother and killed our father." The father remained unconscious until dawn, but when the cool morning breeze blew upon his face, he regained consciousness [1]. Although his heart was on fire and his soul was burning, he never uttered a word that indicated ingratitude, despair, hopelessness, or lamentation. Instead, he said: "I will be patient, a beautiful patience (Sabrun Jamil), a patience accompanied by gratitude and praise of Allah" (فَصَبْرٌ جَمِيلٌ‌) [2]. Then he said: "And Allah is the One sought for help against what you describe" (وَ اللَّهُ الْمُسْتَعانُ عَلى‌ ما تَصِفُونَ‌).

I ask Him to make the bitterness of the cup of patience sweet in my mouth and to give me more strength and endurance so that I do not lose self-control in the face of this great storm, and my tongue does not become tainted with improper speech.

[1]: Tafsir al-Alusi, under the verse. [2]: "Sabrun Jamil" is of the category of adjective and noun, and it is the predicate (khabar) for a deleted subject (mubtada); originally it was "My patience is a beautiful patience."

He did not say, "I ask Allah to give me patience over the calamity of Joseph's death," because he knew Joseph had not been killed. Rather, he said, "I seek patience against what you describe," the result of which is, in any case, my separation from my son.

Key Points

1- For a Single "Leaving of the Better" (Tark al-Awla)! ...

Abu Hamza al-Thumali narrates from Imam Sajjad عليه السلام: "I was in Medina on a Friday. I prayed the morning prayer with Imam Sajjad عليه السلام. When the Imam finished the prayer and glorification, he moved toward his house and I was with him. He called the maidservant and said: 'Be careful. Whoever asks or is in need and passes by the door of the house, give them food, for today is Friday.'"

Abu Hamza says: "I said, not everyone who asks for help is deserving!"

The Imam replied: "That is true, but I fear that among them there may be deserving individuals to whom we do not give food and whom we turn away from our door, and what happened to Jacob and the family of Jacob might happen to our family!"

Then he said: "Give food to all of them. (Have you not heard?) For Jacob, they would slaughter a sheep every day; he would give a portion to the needy and he and his children would eat a portion. One day, a believing petitioner who was fasting and held a status with Allah happened to pass through that city. It was Friday eve (Thursday night). He came to the door of Jacob's house at the time of breaking the fast and said: 'Help the poor, hungry, stranger guest from your extra food.' He repeated this several times. They heard him but did not believe his words. When he became disappointed and the darkness of night covered everywhere, he returned while his eyes were weeping and he complained to Allah of hunger. He remained hungry that night and was still fasting in the morning, while he was patient and praising Allah. However, Jacob and Jacob's family were completely full, and in the morning, some of their food remained as leftovers!"

The Imam then added: "Allah sent revelation to Jacob that same morning: 'O Jacob, you humiliated My servant and ignited My anger, and you became deserving of discipline and the descent of punishment upon you and your children... O Jacob, I rebuke and punish My friends sooner than My enemies, and that is because I love them!'" [1] It is noteworthy that following this hadith, we read that Abu Hamza says: "I asked Imam Sajjad عليه السلام, when did Joseph see that dream?" The Imam replied: "On that very night." [2]

From this hadith, it is well understood that a small slip, or more accurately, a "leaving of the better" (Tark al-Awla) which was not even considered a sin or transgression (since the condition of that petitioner was not clear to Jacob), by the prophets and saints of Truth often causes Allah to give them a painful lesson. This is only because their high status requires them to always be mindful of their smallest words and actions, for "the good deeds of the righteous are the bad deeds of those drawn near" (Hasanat al-Abrar Sayyi'at al-Muqarrabin).
