If Jacob suffered all that pain and grief for being unaware of the heartache of one petitioner, one must think: how can a society in which a group is full and a much larger group is hungry not be subject to the wrath and anger of the Lord, and how could Allah not punish them?

[1] and [2]: Tafsir al-Burhan, Vol. 2, p. 243; and Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 2, p. 411.

2- Joseph's Effective Supplication!

In the narrations of the Ahl al-Bayt عليهم السلام and through Sunni sources, we read: When Joseph was placed in the bottom of the well, his hope was cut off from everywhere and all his attention turned toward the Pure Essence of Allah. He communed with his God and, through the teaching of Gabriel, had intimate prayers which have been narrated in various wordings.

In one narration, we read that he communed with Allah thus:

"O Allah, O Companion of every stranger, O Associate of every lonely one, O Refuge of every fearful one, O Remover of every grief, O Knower of every secret conversation, O Ultimate Goal of every complaint, O Present in every gathering, O Ever-Living, O Self-Subsisting! I ask You to cast Your hope into my heart so that I have no worry or preoccupation other than You, and that You grant me relief and a way out of my affair. Indeed, You are Powerful over all things."

Interestingly, at the end of this hadith, we read that the angels heard Joseph's voice and said:

"Our God! We hear a voice and a supplication: the voice is the voice of a child, but the supplication is the supplication of a prophet!" [1]

It is also noteworthy that when the brothers threw Joseph into the well, they had removed his shirt and his body was bare. He cried out: "At least give me my shirt so that if I remain alive, I may cover my body, and if I die, it may be my shroud." The brothers said: "Ask that same sun, moon, and eleven stars that you saw in your dream to be your companions in this well and to clothe your body!" (And following absolute despair of anyone other than Allah, he recited the above supplication). [1]

It is narrated from Imam Sadiq عليه السلام that he said: When they threw Joseph into the well, Gabriel came to him and said: "Child! What are you doing here?" In response, he said: "My brothers have thrown me into the well." He asked: "Would you like to get out of the well?" He said: "It is with Allah; if He wills, He will bring me out." He said: "Your God has commanded you to recite this supplication so that you may come out." He asked: "Which supplication?" He said: "Say:

O Allah, I ask You, for to You belongs all praise, there is no deity but You, the Bestower of Favors, Originator of the heavens and the earth, Possessor of Majesty and Honor, that You bless Muhammad and the family of Muhammad and that You grant me relief and a way out from what I am in." [2]

There is no obstacle to Joseph having recited all these supplications.

3- The Phrase "وَ أَجْمَعُوا أَنْ يَجْعَلُوهُ فِي غَيابَتِ الْجُبِ‌"

The phrase "And they unanimously decided to place him in the depths of the well" is evidence that they did not hurl him into the well, but rather lowered him down and placed him at the bottom of the well where there is a ledge-like area made for those who go down into the well, near the water's surface. They did this by tying a rope around his waist, lowering him near the water, and then releasing him.

Some narrations revealed in the commentary of the above verses also confirm this point.

4- Enticement of the Soul (Taswil al-Nafs)

The word "سولت" (sawwalat) is from the root "تسويل" (taswil), meaning "to beautify" or "to adorn." Sometimes it is interpreted as "to encourage" and sometimes as "to whisper/tempt," all of which essentially return to the same meaning. That is: your carnal desires beautified this act for you.

This refers to the fact that when rebellious desires dominate a person's spirit and thought, they beautify the ugliest crimes—such as killing or exiling a brother—in a person's eyes to the extent that he imagines it to be a sacred and necessary matter. This is a window into a general principle in psychological matters: that extreme inclination toward a matter, especially when combined with moral vices, casts a veil over a person's sense of discernment and makes realities appear distorted to him.

Therefore, correct judgment and the perception of objective realities are not possible without the refinement of the soul. If we see that justice is a condition for a judge, one of the reasons is this. And if the Holy Quran says in Surah Al-Baqarah, verse 282: "Fear Allah, and Allah will teach you," it is again an allusion to this reality.

5- A Liar Has No Memory

The story of Joseph and his encounter with his brothers once again proves the famous principle that a liar cannot keep his secret hidden forever. This is because objective realities, when they take external existence, have countless connections with other subjects around them. A liar who wants to create a false scene with his lie, no matter how clever and skillful he may be, cannot maintain all these connections. Even if he creates several false connections in relation to the issues surrounding the event, keeping all these fabricated connections in memory forever is not an easy task, and the slightest negligence leads to contradictions. Furthermore, many of these links are overlooked, and it is those very things that eventually reveal the reality. This is a great lesson for all those who care for their honor and reputation: never to go near lies, not to jeopardize their social standing, and not to invite the wrath of Allah upon themselves.

6- What is "Beautiful Patience" (Sabrun Jamil)?

Patience in the face of hard events and heavy storms is a sign of a person's character and the vastness of their soul—a vastness such that it accommodates great events and does not tremble.

A gentle breeze can move the water of a small pond, but great oceans like the Pacific accept even the largest storms, and their tranquility is not disturbed.

Sometimes a person apparently shows patience but makes the face of this patience ugly and unsightly by saying offensive words that indicate ingratitude and an inability to endure the event.

But people of faith, strong will, and great capacity are those whose cup of patience never overflows in such events, and they do not utter words that indicate ingratitude, disbelief, restlessness, or lamentation. Their patience is "beautiful patience" (Sabrun Jamil).

Now the question arises: in other verses of this Surah, we read that Jacob wept so much and grieved so much that he lost his eyesight. Does this not contradict "beautiful patience"?

The answer to this question is one sentence: the hearts of the men of Allah are centers of emotion. It is not surprising that in the separation from a child, their tears flow like a flood. This is an emotional matter; the important thing is that they do not lose self-control—meaning they do not say or do anything contrary to the pleasure of Allah.

It is understood from Islamic hadiths that this very objection was made to the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله when he was weeping over the death of his son Ibrahim: "You forbade us from weeping, yet you yourself are shedding tears?"

The Prophet replied: "The eye weeps and the heart grieves, but I do not say that which angers the Lord" (تدمع العين و يحزن القلب و لا نقول ما يسخط الرب). Elsewhere we read that he said: "This is not [prohibited] weeping; this is mercy (emotional weeping)" (ليس هذا بكاء ان هذا رحمة) [1]. This indicates that within a human's chest is a heart, not a stone, and it is natural for it to react to emotional issues, and its simplest reaction is the flow of tears from the eye. This is not a flaw; it is a virtue. The flaw is for a person to speak words that bring upon them the wrath of Allah.

[1]: Bihar al-Anwar, New Edition, Vol. 22, p. 157 and p. 151.

Ayahs

وَجَآءَتْ سَيَّارَةٌ فَأَرْسَلُوا۟ وَارِدَهُمْ فَأَدْلَىٰ دَلْوَهُۥ ۖ قَالَ يَـٰبُشْرَىٰ هَـٰذَا غُلَـٰمٌ ۚ وَأَسَرُّوهُ بِضَـٰعَةً ۚ وَٱللَّهُ عَلِيمٌۢ بِمَا يَعْمَلُونَ (١٩)
There came a caravan, and they sent their water-drawer, who let down his bucket. ‘Good news!’ he said. ‘This is a young boy!’ So they hid him as [a piece of] merchandise, and Allah knew best what they were doing. (19)
وَشَرَوْهُ بِثَمَنٍۭ بَخْسٍ دَرَٰهِمَ مَعْدُودَةٍ وَكَانُوا۟ فِيهِ مِنَ ٱلزَّٰهِدِينَ (٢٠)
And they sold him for a cheap price, a few dirhams, for they set small store by him. (20)

Commentary

Toward the Land of Egypt

Yusuf (Joseph) spent bitter hours in the terrifying darkness of the well, accompanied by a crushing loneliness. However, faith in Allah and the Sakinah (tranquility) resulting from that faith cast a light of hope into his heart, giving him the endurance to withstand this horrific isolation and emerge victorious from the furnace of this trial.

Only Allah knows how many days passed in this state; some commentators have written three days, and others two.

In any case, "a caravan arrived" (وَ جاءَتْ سَيَّارَةٌ) [^1] and halted nearby. It is evident that the first need of a caravan is to secure water. Therefore, "they sent their water-carrier [to the well]" (فَأَرْسَلُوا وارِدَهُمْ‌) [^2]. "The water-carrier let down his bucket into the well" (فَأَدْلى‌ دَلْوَهُ‌).

From the bottom of the well, Yusuf noticed sounds coming from above, followed by the sight of a bucket and rope descending rapidly. He seized the opportunity, making use of this divine gift, and immediately clung to it.

The water-carrier felt that his bucket had become unusually heavy. When he pulled it up with strength, his eyes suddenly fell upon a beautiful, moon-faced young child, and he cried out: "Good news! This is a boy [instead of water]!" (قالَ يا بُشْرى‌ هذا غُلامٌ‌).

Gradually, a group of the caravanners became aware of this matter. However, so that others would not find out and they could sell this beautiful child as a slave in Egypt, "they concealed him as a valuable merchandise" (وَ أَسَرُّوهُ بِضاعَةً).

Of course, other possibilities have been suggested in the interpretation of this phrase. One is that those who found Yusuf concealed the fact that they found him in the well, saying instead: "This is a commodity that the owners of this well have entrusted to us to sell for them in Egypt."

Another is that some of Yusuf's brothers, who would occasionally come to the well to get news of him or bring him food, concealed Yusuf's brotherhood when they became aware of the situation. They simply said, "He is our slave who ran away and hid here," and they threatened Yusuf with death if he were to reveal the truth.

However, the first interpretation seems closest to the context [^3]. At the end of the verse, we read: "And Allah is aware of what they were doing" (وَ اللَّهُ عَلِيمٌ بِما يَعْمَلُونَ‌).

"Finally, they sold him for a small price—a few dirhams" (وَ شَرَوْهُ بِثَمَنٍ بَخْسٍ دَراهِمَ مَعْدُودَةٍ). Although there is discussion regarding the sellers of Yusuf and who they were—some considering them to be Yusuf's brothers—the apparent meaning of the verses is that the caravanners took this action. This is because there is no mention of the brothers in the preceding verses, and the discussion regarding the brothers concluded with the end of the previous verse. Furthermore, the plural pronouns in the verbs "أرسلوا" (they sent), "أسروه" (they concealed him), and "شروه" (they sold him) all refer to the same group: the caravanners.

Here, the question arises: why did they sell Yusuf—who was at the very least considered a high-value slave—for such a small price, or as the Quran expresses it, a thaman bakhs (a deficient price)?

It is common for thieves or those who acquire a significant asset without effort to sell it immediately out of fear that others might find out. Naturally, with such haste, they cannot secure a high price for themselves.

"Bakhs" originally means to diminish something through injustice. This is why the Quran says: وَ لا تَبْخَسُوا النَّاسَ أَشْياءَهُمْ‌: "And do not deprive people of their due [by diminishing their things through injustice]" (Hud: 85).

Regarding how many dirhams they sold Yusuf for and how they divided it among themselves, there is again discussion among commentators. Some have written 20 dirhams, some 22, some 40, and some 18. Given that the number of sellers was considered to be ten, the share of each from this meager amount is clear.

At the end of the verse, He says: "And they were indifferent to [the selling of] him" (وَ كانُوا فِيهِ مِنَ الزَّاهِدِينَ‌). In reality, this sentence serves as an explanation for the reason behind the previous phrase, indicating that if they sold Yusuf for a small price, it was because they were uninterested and indifferent toward this transaction.

This was either because the caravanners had acquired Yusuf cheaply—and a person often lets go of what they acquired cheaply just as cheaply—or because they feared their secret would be exposed and a claimant would appear. Alternatively, it could be because they did not see the signs of slavery in Yusuf; rather, the marks of nobility and freedom were apparent in his face. For this reason, neither the sellers were very keen on selling him, nor were the buyers [keen on buying him].

[^1]: A caravan is called "sayyarah" because it is constantly in motion and travel (sayr). [^2]: "Warid" means a water-carrier, originally derived from "wurud," which—as Raghib says in Al-Mufradat—means the intention to fetch water, though it was later expanded to mean any entry or arrival. [^3]: "Bida'ah" (merchandise) is originally from the root "bad'" (rhyming with nadhr), meaning a piece of meat that is cut off. It was later generalized to refer to a significant portion of wealth or capital. "Bid'ah" means a part of one's body, and "hasan al-bad'" refers to a plump or fleshy person. "Bid'" (rhyming with hizb) refers to a number from three to ten (Mufradat al-Raghib).

Ayahs

وَقَالَ ٱلَّذِى ٱشْتَرَىٰهُ مِن مِّصْرَ لِٱمْرَأَتِهِۦٓ أَكْرِمِى مَثْوَىٰهُ عَسَىٰٓ أَن يَنفَعَنَآ أَوْ نَتَّخِذَهُۥ وَلَدًا ۚ وَكَذَٰلِكَ مَكَّنَّا لِيُوسُفَ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَلِنُعَلِّمَهُۥ مِن تَأْوِيلِ ٱلْأَحَادِيثِ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ غَالِبٌ عَلَىٰٓ أَمْرِهِۦ وَلَـٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ ٱلنَّاسِ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ (٢١)
The man from Egypt who had bought him said to his wife, ‘Give him an honourable place [in the household]. Maybe he will be useful to us, or we may adopt him as a son.’ Thus We established Joseph in the land and that We might teach him the interpretation of dreams. Allah has [full] command of His affairs, but most people do not know. (21)
وَلَمَّا بَلَغَ أَشُدَّهُۥٓ ءَاتَيْنَـٰهُ حُكْمًا وَعِلْمًا ۚ وَكَذَٰلِكَ نَجْزِى ٱلْمُحْسِنِينَ (٢٢)
When he came of age, We gave him judgement and [sacred] knowledge, and thus do We reward the virtuous. (22)

Commentary

In the Palace of the Aziz of Egypt

The adventurous story of Yusuf and his brothers, which culminated in him being cast into the depths of the well, came to an end in one way or another, and a new chapter began in the life of this young child in Egypt.

The sequence of events led to Yusuf finally being brought to Egypt and put up for sale. As was customary, since he was a precious treasure, he became the lot of the "Aziz of Egypt," who in reality held the position of minister or prime minister to the Pharaoh; for it was they who could pay a higher price for this "slave, distinguished in every aspect." Now let us see what transpires in the house of the Aziz of Egypt.

The Quran says: "The one from Egypt who bought him said to his wife: 'Make his stay honorable; perhaps he will bring us much benefit in the future, or we may adopt him as a son'" (وَ قالَ الَّذِي اشْتَراهُ مِنْ مِصْرَ لِامْرَأَتِهِ أَكْرِمِي مَثْواهُ عَسى‌ أَنْ يَنْفَعَنا أَوْ نَتَّخِذَهُ وَلَداً) [1]. From this sentence, it is inferred that the Aziz of Egypt had no children and lived in longing for a child. When his eyes fell upon this beautiful and robust child, he set his heart on him to be as a son to him.

Then It adds: "Thus did We establish Yusuf in the land, providing him with status and comfort" (وَ كَذلِكَ مَكَّنَّا لِيُوسُفَ فِي الْأَرْضِ‌).

This "establishment" (tamkin) in the land was either because Yusuf’s arrival in Egypt, and specifically his entry into the living environment of the Aziz, served as a prelude to his extraordinary power in the future, or because life in the palace of the Aziz was incomparable to life at the bottom of a well. Where was that intense loneliness, hunger, and terror, and where was all this bounty, welfare, and tranquility?

After that, It adds: "We did this so that We might teach him the interpretation of events (dreams)" (وَ لِنُعَلِّمَهُ مِنْ تَأْوِيلِ الْأَحادِيثِ‌).

The meaning of "interpretation of events" (ta’wil al-ahadith), as previously mentioned, is the science of dream interpretation through which Yusuf could become aware of a significant portion of future secrets. Alternatively, it may refer to Divine revelation, as Yusuf, by passing through the difficult passes of Divine trials in the court of the Aziz, gained the worthiness to

[1]: "Mathwa" means a place of stay, derived from the root "thawa" meaning to reside, but here it means position, status, and rank.

become a carrier of the message and revelation; however, the first possibility seems more appropriate.

At the end of the verse, It says: "And Allah is predominant over His affairs, but most people do not know" (وَ اللَّهُ غالِبٌ عَلى‌ أَمْرِهِ وَ لكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ النَّاسِ لا يَعْلَمُونَ‌).

One of the wondrous manifestations of Allah's power and His dominance over affairs is that in many instances, He provides the means for a person's victory and salvation through the hands of their enemies. Just as in the case of Yusuf, had it not been for the brothers' plot, he would never have gone into the well; and had he not gone into the well, he would not have come to Egypt; and had he not come to Egypt, he would neither have gone to prison nor seen the Pharaoh's strange dream, nor eventually become the Aziz of Egypt.

In reality, Allah seated Yusuf upon the throne of power by the hands of his brothers, even though they imagined they had cast him into the well of misfortune.

In this new environment, which was in fact one of the major political centers of Egypt, Yusuf faced new issues. On one side, he observed the dazzling apparatus of dreamlike palaces and the boundless wealth of the Egyptian tyrants, and on the other side, the scene of the slave market was etched in his mind. From the comparison of these two, the immense suffering and pain endured by the majority of the masses weighed heavily on his soul and mind, and he thought of putting an end to this situation—should he gain power.

Yes, he learned many things in this new, clamorous environment. A storm of grief and sorrow was always flowing in his heart, for under those conditions, he could do nothing, and during this period, he was constantly engaged in self-building and spiritual refinement. The Quran says: "When he reached his full strength (maturity of body and soul) and became ready to receive the lights of revelation, We gave him judgment and knowledge" (وَ لَمَّا بَلَغَ أَشُدَّهُ آتَيْناهُ حُكْماً وَ عِلْماً).

"And thus do We reward the doers of good" (وَ كَذلِكَ نَجْزِي الْمُحْسِنِينَ‌).

"Ashudd" is derived from "shadd," meaning a firm knot, and here it refers to physical and spiritual strength. Some have said "ashudd" is a plural that has no singular form, while others consider it the plural of "shidd" (rhyming with sadd); regardless, its plural meaning cannot be denied.

The "judgment and knowledge" mentioned in the verse, which Allah granted him after he reached physical and spiritual maturity, refers either to the station of revelation and prophethood, as some commentators have said, or "judgment" (hukm) refers to intellect, understanding, and the power of correct judgment free from caprice and error, while "knowledge" (ilm) refers to awareness and learning untainted by ignorance. Whatever it was, this "judgment and knowledge" were two distinguished and precious Divine gifts that Allah gave to Yusuf due to his purity, piety, patience, endurance, and trust in Him—all of which are gathered in the word "muhsinin" (doers of good).

Some commentators have listed three possibilities for the combination of judgment and knowledge here:

1- Judgment refers to the station of prophethood (since a Prophet is a judge of truth) and knowledge refers to the knowledge of religion.

2- Judgment means self-restraint against rebellious desires, which here refers to practical wisdom, and knowledge refers to theoretical wisdom and science. Placing judgment before knowledge is because a person does not find the path to true knowledge until they refine their soul and engage in self-building.

3- Judgment means that a person reaches the station of the "reassured soul" (nafs al-mutma'innah) and gains self-mastery such that they can control the commanding and whispering soul; and knowledge refers to the holy lights and rays of Divine grace that shine from the celestial realm upon the

pure heart of a human [1].

Key Points

1- Why the name of the Aziz of Egypt was not mentioned

One of the matters that draws attention in the above verses is that the name of the Aziz of Egypt is not mentioned. It is only said: "the one from Egypt who bought Yusuf." Who this person was is not stated in the verse. In future verses, we see again that the veil is not lifted from this person's title all at once; rather, he is introduced gradually. For example, in verse 25, It says: "وَ أَلْفَيا سَيِّدَها لَدَى الْبابِ" (When Yusuf did not submit to Zulaikha's love and fled toward the exit, they suddenly encountered her master at the door).

Moving past these verses, we reach verse 30, where the expression "the wife of the Aziz" (imra'at al-Aziz) is used.

This gradual disclosure is either because the Quran, according to its tradition, recounts every matter to the necessary extent—which is a sign of eloquence and rhetoric—or because, as is common in literature today, when telling a story, they begin from a closed point to pique the reader's curiosity and draw their attention toward the narrative.

2- The relationship between dream interpretation and Yusuf's arrival at the palace of the Aziz

Another point that raises a question in the above verses is: what relationship does the subject of knowledge of dream interpretation have with Yusuf's arrival at the palace of the Aziz, which is indicated by the "lam" in "li-nu'allimahu" (so that We might teach him), serving as the "lam of purpose"?

[1]: Tafsir al-Kabir, Fakhr Razi, Vol. 18, p. 111.

However, paying attention to this point may provide an answer: Allah grants many intellectual gifts in exchange for abstaining from sin and resisting rebellious desires. In other words, these gifts, which are the fruit of spiritual insights, are a prize that Allah bestows upon such individuals.

In the accounts of "Ibn Sirin," the famous dream interpreter, we read that he was a cloth merchant and very handsome. A woman set her heart on him and, through specific ruses, brought him to her house and locked the doors. However, he did not submit to her desires and repeatedly enumerated the evils of this great sin to her. Yet the fire of her desire was so rebellious that the water of admonition could not extinguish it. To escape her clutches, Ibn Sirin devised a plan: he rose and defiled his body with filthy things found in that house, making himself so foul and repulsive that when the woman saw that sight, she became disgusted with him and turned him out of the house. It is said that after this incident, Ibn Sirin was granted extraordinary intuition and alertness in dream interpretation, and strange stories of his dream interpretations are recorded in books, testifying to the depth of his knowledge in this field.

Therefore, Yusuf may have gained this specific science and awareness because of his self-mastery in the face of the extraordinary attraction of the Aziz's wife.

Furthermore, in that era, the courts of great rulers were centers for dream interpreters, and an alert youth like Yusuf could become aware of the experiences of others in the court of the Aziz and gain the spiritual readiness for the bestowal of Divine knowledge in this field.

In any case, this is neither the first nor the last time that Allah bestows gifts of sciences and knowledge, which cannot be measured by any material scale, upon sincere servants who emerge victorious over rebellious desires in the field of the "jihad of the soul." The famous hadith:

"Knowledge is a light that Allah casts into the heart of whomever He wills"

can also refer to this reality.

This is not a science to be read in the presence of a teacher, nor is it given to someone without account. These are prizes for the winners of the competition of the jihad against the soul!

3- What is meant by "reaching full strength" (bulugh al-ashudd)?

We said "ashudd" means physical and spiritual strength and firmness, and "reaching full strength" means arriving at this stage. However, this title in the Holy Quran is applied to various stages of human life.

Sometimes it refers to the age of puberty, as in: "And do not approach the orphan's property except in a way that is best until he reaches his full strength" (Isra: 34).

Sometimes it refers to reaching the age of forty, as in: "Until, when he reaches his full strength and reaches forty years..." (Ahqaf: 15).

And sometimes it refers to the stage before old age, as in: "Then He brings you out as a child, then [lets you grow] so that you may reach your full strength, then so that you may become old" (Ghafir: 67).

This variation in expressions may be because a person passes through stages to reach spiritual and physical firmness; undoubtedly, reaching puberty is one of them, and reaching forty years—which is usually accompanied by a type of maturity in thought and intellect—is another stage, as is the period before a person begins their downward arc and tends toward weakness and frailty.

In any case, in the verse under discussion, it refers to the stage of physical and spiritual maturity that appeared in Yusuf at the beginning of his youth. "Fakhr Razi" has a statement in his commentary in this regard:

"The duration of the moon's cycle (until it reaches the waning phase) is 28 days. When we divide it into four parts, each part becomes 7 days (forming the number of days in a week).

Therefore, scholars have divided the states of the human body into four seven-year periods:

First, when he is born, he is weak and powerless, both in body and soul. But when he reaches the age of 7, signs of intelligence, thought, and physical strength appear in him.

He enters the second stage and continues his perfection until he leaves fourteen years behind and becomes 15. At this time, he reaches the stage of physical and spiritual puberty, and sexual desire is stirred within him, and (with the completion of the fifteenth year) he becomes religiously accountable.

He continues his perfection until he completes the third cycle and undergoes a new stage. Finally, with the end of the fourth cycle and reaching 28 years, the period of physical growth ends, and the human enters a new stage which is the stage of plateauing, and this is the time of 'reaching full strength.' This state of plateauing continues until the end of the fifth cycle, i.e., 35 years (and from then on, the downward arc begins)" [1]. Although the above classification is somewhat acceptable, it does not seem precise because, firstly, the stage of puberty is not at the end of the second cycle, and also the end of physical growth, according to what modern scientists say, is at 25 years, and full intellectual maturity, according to

[1]: Tafsir Fakhr Razi, Vol. 18, p. 111.

some narrations, is at forty years. Above all, what was said above is not considered a universal law that applies to all individuals.

4- The reward of the doers of good

The final point necessary to note here is that when the Quran speaks of giving wisdom and knowledge to Yusuf in the above verses, It adds: "And thus do We reward the doers of good," meaning that Divine gifts, even to Prophets, are not without account. Everyone benefits from the boundless sea of Divine grace according to their goodness and excellence, just as Yusuf received a vast portion in exchange for his patience and steadfastness in the face of all those difficulties.

Ayahs

وَرَٰوَدَتْهُ ٱلَّتِى هُوَ فِى بَيْتِهَا عَن نَّفْسِهِۦ وَغَلَّقَتِ ٱلْأَبْوَٰبَ وَقَالَتْ هَيْتَ لَكَ ۚ قَالَ مَعَاذَ ٱللَّهِ ۖ إِنَّهُۥ رَبِّىٓ أَحْسَنَ مَثْوَاىَ ۖ إِنَّهُۥ لَا يُفْلِحُ ٱلظَّـٰلِمُونَ (٢٣)
The woman in whose house he was, solicited him. She closed the doors and said, ‘Come!!’ He said, ‘God forbid! Indeed He is my Lord; He has given me a good abode. Indeed the wrongdoers are not felicitous.’ (23)
وَلَقَدْ هَمَّتْ بِهِۦ ۖ وَهَمَّ بِهَا لَوْلَآ أَن رَّءَا بُرْهَـٰنَ رَبِّهِۦ ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ لِنَصْرِفَ عَنْهُ ٱلسُّوٓءَ وَٱلْفَحْشَآءَ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ مِنْ عِبَادِنَا ٱلْمُخْلَصِينَ (٢٤)
She certainly made for him; and he would have made for her [too] had he not beheld the proof of his Lord. So it was, that We might turn away from him all evil and indecency. He was indeed one of Our dedicated servants. (24)

Commentary

The Burning Passion of the Wife of the Al-Aziz of Egypt

Yusuf, with his beautiful and celestial countenance, not only captivated the Al-Aziz of Egypt but also swiftly conquered the heart of the Al-Aziz’s wife. Her love for him cast its claws into the depths of her soul, and as time passed, this love grew hotter and more burning by the day. However, the pure and God-fearing Yusuf thought of nothing but Allah, and his heart was pledged only to the "Love of Allah."

Other factors also joined hands to fan the flames of the Al-Aziz’s wife’s fiery passion. Her childlessness on one hand, her immersion in a luxurious aristocratic life on the other, the lack of any domestic troubles—as is common among the nobility and the affluent—on a third, and the intense atmosphere of moral laxity prevailing in the Egyptian court on a fourth, all plunged this woman, who possessed no share of faith or piety, into waves of satanic temptation. Eventually, she decided to reveal the secret of her heart to Yusuf and demand gratification from him.

She utilized every means and method to reach her goal in this path, and with pleading and entreaty, she sought to influence his heart, as the Quran says: "And she, in whose house he was, sought to seduce him repeatedly" (وَ راوَدَتْهُ الَّتِي هُوَ فِي بَيْتِها عَنْ نَفْسِهِ‌).

The word "راوَدَتْهُ" (rawadat-hu) is derived from the root "مراوده" (murawadah), which originally means searching for a pasture or grazing land. The famous proverb: "الرائد لا يكذب قومه" (The scout does not lie to his people [about finding pasture]) refers to this. Similarly, the applicator used to gently apply kohl to the eye is called "مرود" (mirwad), and subsequently, the term was applied to any action sought with gentleness and suavity.

This expression indicates that the wife of the Al-Aziz invited Yusuf to her purpose through what might be called peaceful means, devoid of any threat, using the utmost gentleness and expressions of affection.

Finally, the last resort that occurred to her was to trap him one day alone in her private quarters, providing every means of stimulation. She wore the most attractive clothes, the finest makeup, and the most fragrant perfumes, arranging the scene in such a way as to bring the powerful Yusuf to his knees.

The Quran says: "She bolted the doors firmly and said: 'Come, I am yours!'" (وَ غَلَّقَتِ الْأَبْوابَ وَ قالَتْ هَيْتَ لَكَ‌).

The word "غَلَّقَتِ" (ghallaqat) conveys an intensive meaning, showing that she locked all the doors firmly. This indicates that she had drawn Yusuf into a part of the palace consisting of interconnected inner rooms; as mentioned in some narrations, she locked seven doors so that Yusuf would have no way to escape.

Furthermore, perhaps by this act, she wanted to make Yusuf understand that he should not worry about the outcome being revealed, for no one had the power to penetrate behind these closed doors.

At this moment, when Yusuf saw everything trending toward a lapse and sin, and no outward way remained for him, he sufficed with this sentence in response to Zulaikha and said: "I seek refuge in Allah!" (قالَ مَعاذَ اللَّهِ‌).

In this way, Yusuf decisively rejected the illegitimate demand of the Al-Aziz’s wife, making her understand that he would never surrender to her. Simultaneously, he made her and everyone else realize that in such difficult and critical conditions, the only way to escape the clutches of satanic temptations and those with satanic temperaments is to seek refuge in Allah—the God for whom privacy and public are the same, and against whose Will nothing can resist.

By mentioning this short sentence, he confessed to the Oneness of God both in belief and in action.

Then he added: "Beyond everything else, how can I surrender to such a demand while I live in the house of the Al-Aziz of Egypt and eat at his table, and he has honored my position?" (إِنَّهُ رَبِّي أَحْسَنَ مَثْوايَ‌).

Is this not an act of oppression, transgression, and manifest betrayal? "Indeed, the wrongdoers will not succeed" (إِنَّهُ لا يُفْلِحُ الظَّالِمُونَ‌).

Who is meant by "Rabb" (Lord/Master) in this sentence?

There is much discussion among commentators. Most commentators, as Marhum Tabarsi says in Majma' al-Bayan and the author of Al-Manar says in Al-Manar, have taken "Rabb" in the broad sense of the word and said it refers to the "Al-Aziz of Egypt," who did not fall short in respecting and honoring Yusuf, and from the very beginning had recommended Yusuf to his wife with the phrase "أَكْرِمِي مَثْواهُ‌" (Make his stay honorable).

The assumption that the word "Rabb" is not used in this sense is completely mistaken, for in this very Surah, the word "Rabb" is applied to someone other than Allah several times, sometimes from the tongue of Yusuf and sometimes from others.

For example, in the story of the interpretation of the prisoners' dreams, we read that Yusuf said to the prisoner to whom he had given the glad tidings of freedom: "Mention me to your lord (the ruler of Egypt)" (وقالَ لِلَّذِي ظَنَّ أَنَّهُ ناجٍ مِنْهُمَا اذْكُرْنِي عِنْدَ رَبِّكَ‌ - Verse 42).

And again, we read from Yusuf’s tongue when the messenger of Pharaoh came to him, he said: "Return to your lord (Pharaoh) and ask him to investigate why the women of Egypt cut their hands" (فلما جاءه‌الرَّسُولُ قالَ ارْجِعْ إِلى‌ رَبِّكَ فَسْئَلْهُ ما بالُ النِّسْوَةِ اللَّاتِي قَطَّعْنَ أَيْدِيَهُنَ‌ - Verse 50).

In Verse 41 of this Surah from Yusuf’s tongue, and at the end of Verse 42 from the Quran’s tongue, we read that the word "Rabb" is applied to an owner or a benefactor.

Therefore, you see that in this Surah, in four instances other than the one under discussion, the word "Rabb" is applied to someone other than Allah, even though in this Surah and other Surahs of the Quran, this word is repeatedly used for the Lord of the Worlds. The point is that this is a shared word applied to both meanings.

However, some commentators have preferred that the word "Rabb" in the verse under discussion (إِنَّهُ رَبِّي أَحْسَنَ مَثْوايَ‌) means Allah, because the word "Allah" mentioned alongside it causes the pronoun to refer back to Him. In this case, the meaning of the sentence would be: "I seek refuge in Allah, the God who is my Lord and who has honored my rank and position, and every blessing I have is from Him." Yet, paying attention to the Al-Aziz’s recommendation with the phrase "أَكْرِمِي مَثْواهُ‌" and its repetition in the verse under discussion strengthens the first meaning.

In the Torah, in Chapter 39, numbers 8, 9, and 10, it is stated: "And it came to pass after these things that his master’s wife cast her eyes upon Joseph and said, 'Lie with me.' But he refused and said to his master’s wife, 'Behold, my master does not know what is with me in the house, and he has committed all that he has to my hand. There is none greater in this house than I, nor has he kept back anything from me but you, because you are his wife. How then can I do this great wickedness and sin against God?'" These sentences from the Torah also support the first meaning.

At this point, the situation between Yusuf and the Al-Aziz’s wife reaches its narrowest stage and most sensitive state, which the Quran speaks of with a meaningful expression: "The wife of the Al-Aziz desired him, and he would have desired her, had he not seen the proof of his Lord!" (وَ لَقَدْ هَمَّتْ بِهِ وَ هَمَّ بِها لَوْ لا أَنْ رَأى‌ بُرْهانَ رَبِّهِ‌).

There is much discussion among commentators regarding the meaning of this sentence, which can be summarized into the following three interpretations:

  1. The wife of the Al-Aziz intended to seek gratification from Yusuf and exerted her utmost effort in this path. Yusuf, too, according to human nature—being a young man in his prime, not yet married, and placed before the most provocative of sexual scenes—would have made such a decision had the proof of his Lord, meaning the spirit of faith, piety, self-discipline, and ultimately the station of "Infallibility" (Ismah), not intervened!

Therefore, the difference between the "hamm" (intent) of the Al-Aziz’s wife and Yusuf was that Yusuf’s was conditional upon a condition that did not occur (the absence of the Lord’s proof), whereas the Al-Aziz’s wife’s was absolute. Since she did not possess such a station of piety and godliness, she made that decision and stood by it until the very last stage until she hit a dead end.

We have similar expressions in Arabic and Persian literature, such as saying: "The profligate individuals decided to plunder the fruits of such-and-such farmer's garden; I, too, would have made such a decision had I not been trained in the school of my master for many years."

Thus, Yusuf’s intent was conditional upon a condition that was not met, and this not only does not contradict Yusuf’s station of infallibility and piety but is an explanation and manifestation of this lofty station.

According to this interpretation, no movement indicating an intent to sin was made by Yusuf, nor did he even form the intent in his heart.

Therefore, some narrations stating that Yusuf prepared to take gratification from the Al-Aziz’s wife, or even removed his clothes, and other expressions which we are ashamed to quote, are all baseless and fabricated. These are actions befitting polluted, profligate, impure, and dishonest individuals. How can Yusuf, with that sanctity of soul and station of piety, be accused of such things?

Interestingly, in a tradition from Imam Ali ibn Musa al-Rida عليه السلام, this first interpretation is expressed in a very concise and brief manner. When the Abbasid Caliph "Ma'mun" asked the Imam: "Do you not say that the Prophets are infallible?" He replied: "Yes." Ma'mun said: "Then what is the interpretation of this verse: وَ لَقَدْ هَمَّتْ بِهِ وَ هَمَّ بِها لَوْ لا أَنْ رَأى‌ بُرْهانَ رَبِّهِ‌?"

The Imam replied: "لَقَدْ هَمَّتْ بِهِ‌ and had he not seen the proof of his Lord, he would have intended her as she intended him, but he was infallible, and the infallible never intends a sin nor commits it." Ma'mun (delighted by this answer) said: "May Allah bless you, O Abu al-Hasan!" [^1]

  1. Neither the intent of the Al-Aziz’s wife nor Yusuf’s was related to sexual gratification; rather, it was an intent to attack and strike one another. The Al-Aziz’s wife because she had been defeated in love and a spirit of revenge had arisen in her, and Yusuf for the sake of self-defense and not surrendering to the woman’s imposition.

Among the clues mentioned for this subject is that the Al-Aziz’s wife had made her decision for gratification long before this and had performed all its preliminaries; therefore, it would not make sense for the Quran to say she "intended" this act at that moment, for that moment was not the moment of decision.

Another point is that the emergence of a state of violence and revenge after this defeat is natural, for she had exhausted everything in her power through gentleness with Yusuf, and when she could not influence him that way, she resorted to another weapon, which was the weapon of violence.

Thirdly, at the end of this verse, we read: كَذلِكَ لِنَصْرِفَ عَنْهُ السُّوءَ وَ الْفَحْشاءَ: "Thus [it was] that We might turn away from him evil and immorality." "Immorality" (fahsha) is the pollution of unchastity, and "evil" (su') is salvation from the clutches of the Al-Aziz’s wife’s blow.

However, in any case, Yusuf, because he saw the proof of his Lord, refrained from grappling with that woman lest he attack her and strike her, which would itself become a reason to claim he intended transgression; thus, he preferred to distance himself from that place and flee toward the door.

  1. Undoubtedly, Yusuf was a young man with all the feelings of youth, even though his powerful instincts were under the command of his reason and faith. However, it is natural that whenever such a human being is placed before extraordinarily provocative scenes, a storm arises within him, and instinct and reason rise to struggle against each other. The stronger the waves of stimulating factors, the more the scale of instincts gains weight, to the point where it might reach the final stage of power in a fleeting moment. If one were to take a step further from this stage, it would be a terrifying precipice. Suddenly, the power of faith and reason is stirred and, so to speak, mobilized; it carries out a coup and pushes back the power of instinct that had dragged him to the edge of the abyss.

The Holy Quran has depicted this fleeting, sensitive, and critical moment—which was situated between two periods of calm and reliability—in the above verse. Therefore, the meaning of the phrase "هَمَّ بِها لَوْ لا أَنْ رَأى‌ بُرْهانَ رَبِّهِ‌" is that in the struggle between instinct and reason, Yusuf was drawn to the edge of the abyss, but suddenly, the extraordinary mobilization of the power of faith and reason shattered the storm of instinct [^1], so that no one would think that if Yusuf was able to save himself from this abyss, he performed a simple task because the factors of sin and excitement were weak in his being. Not at all; he too, to preserve his purity in this sensitive moment, engaged in the most intense struggle and Jihad against the self (Jihad al-Nafs).

What is meant by the "Proof of the Lord"?

"Burhan" (Proof) is originally the infinitive of "baraha," meaning to become white, and subsequently, any kind of firm and powerful evidence that leads to the illumination of the objective is called a "burhan." Therefore, the proof of the Lord that caused Yusuf’s salvation was a type of clear divine evidence about which commentators have given many possibilities, including:

  1. Knowledge, faith, human upbringing, and outstanding attributes.
  2. His awareness regarding the ruling of the prohibition of adultery.
  3. The station of Prophethood and being infallible from sin.
  4. A type of divine aid and assistance that came to him in this sensitive moment because of his good deeds.
  5. It is inferred from a narration that there was an idol there, which was considered the deity of the Al-Aziz’s wife. Suddenly, the woman’s eyes fell upon the idol; it was as if she felt it was staring at her with its eyes and watching her treacherous movements with anger. She stood up and threw a cloth over the idol. Witnessing this scene created a storm in Yusuf’s heart; he was shaken and said: "If you feel shame before an idol that lacks reason, consciousness, sense, and discernment, how is it possible for me not to feel shame and modesty before my Lord, who knows everything and is aware of all secrets and private places?"

This feeling gave Yusuf new strength and power and helped him in the intense struggle that was in the depths of his soul between instinct and reason, enabling him to push back the rebellious waves of instinct. [^1] At the same time, there is no obstacle to all these meanings being intended together, for they are all gathered in the general concept of "Burhan," and in the verses of the Quran and narrations, the word "Burhan" has been applied to many of the above meanings.

As for the undocumented narrations that some commentators have quoted, which say Yusuf had made his decision to sin when suddenly, in a state of vision, he saw Jibra'il or Ya'qub biting his finger with his teeth—Yusuf saw this scene and retreated—these kinds of narrations, which have no valid chain of transmission, resemble "Isrā'īliyyāt" (Judeo-Christian traditions) born from the brains of small-minded people who never understood the station of the Prophets.

Now pay attention to the commentary of the rest of the verse: The Holy Quran says: "Thus [it was] that We might show him Our proof, to turn away from him evil and immorality" (كَذلِكَ لِنَصْرِفَ عَنْهُ السُّوءَ وَ الْفَحْشاءَ). "For he was of Our chosen and sincere servants" (إِنَّهُ مِنْ عِبادِنَا الْمُخْلَصِينَ‌).

This indicates that if We sent divine aid and spiritual help to his assistance to save him from evil and sin, it was not without reason. He was a servant who had built himself with awareness, faith, piety, and pure action, and his heart and soul had been purified and made sincere from the darkness of polytheism. For this very reason, he was worthy of such divine aid.

The mention of this reason shows that these kinds of divine aids, which rushed to the help of Prophets like Yusuf in stormy and critical moments, were not exclusive to them. Anyone who enters the ranks of the sincere servants of God and the "Sincere Servants of Allah" will also be worthy of such gifts.

Key Points

1. Jihad against the Self (Jihad al-Nafs)

We know that in Islam, the highest Jihad is the Jihad against the self, which in the famous tradition of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله is called "The Greater Jihad" (Jihad al-Akbar), meaning it is superior to the Jihad against the enemy, which is called "The Lesser Jihad" (Jihad al-Asghar). Fundamentally, until the Greater Jihad is implemented in a person in the true sense, they will not be victorious in the Jihad against the enemy.

In the Holy Quran, various scenes from the field of the Greater Jihad in relation to the Prophets and other saints of God are depicted, and the story of Yusuf and the fiery love of the Al-Aziz’s wife is one of the most important of them. Although the Holy Quran has not explained all its angles for the sake of brevity, it has expressed the intensity of this storm with one short sentence: (وَ هَمَّ بِها لَوْ لا أَنْ رَأى‌ بُرْهانَ رَبِّهِ‌).

Yusuf emerged from the field of this struggle with honor for three reasons: First, he entrusted himself to God and sought refuge in His grace (قالَ مَعاذَ اللَّهِ‌). Second, his consideration of gratitude toward the Al-Aziz of Egypt in whose house he lived, or his consideration of the endless blessings of God who brought him from the bottom of a terrifying well to a safe and calm environment, led him to think more about his past and future and not surrender to fleeting storms. Third, Yusuf’s self-building and his servitude coupled with sincerity—which is inferred from the phrase "إِنَّهُ مِنْ عِبادِنَا الْمُخْلَصِينَ‌"—gave him the power and strength not to kneel in this great field against the multiplied temptations attacking him from within and without.

And this is a lesson for all free human beings who want to be victorious over this dangerous enemy in the field of Jihad against the self.

How beautifully the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, says in the Supplication of Sabah: "And if Your help should fail me when I am at war with the self and Satan, then Your failure of me would entrust me to where there is hardship and deprivation."

In a tradition, we read: "The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله sent a military detachment; when they returned, he said: 'Welcome to a people who have completed the Lesser Jihad, but the Greater Jihad remains for them.' It was asked: 'O Messenger of Allah, what is the Greater Jihad?' He said: 'Jihad against the self.'" [^1] Ali عليه السلام says: "The Mujahid is he who struggles against his self." [^2] And it is narrated from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام: "Whoever controls his self when he desires, when he fears, when he craves, when he is angry, and when he is pleased, Allah forbids his body to the Fire." [^1]

2. The Reward of Sincerity

As we indicated in the commentary of the above verses, the Holy Quran attributes Yusuf’s salvation from this dangerous whirlpool—which the Al-Aziz’s wife had placed in his path—to God, saying: "We turned away evil and immorality from Yusuf."

However, considering the subsequent sentence which says "he was of Our sincere servants," this reality becomes clear: that God never leaves His sincere servants alone in these critical moments and does not withhold His spiritual help from them. Rather, with His hidden favors and divine aids—the description of which is impossible with any words—He preserves His servants. This is, in fact, a reward that the Great God bestows upon such servants: the reward of purity, piety, and sincerity.

Additionally, it is necessary to mention that in the above verses, Yusuf is mentioned as one of the "Mukhlasīn" (with a fatha on the 'lam'), in the form of a passive participle, meaning "those made sincere," not in the form of "Mukhlisīn" (with a kasra on the 'lam'), which is an active participle meaning "those who make [their religion] sincere."

A careful study of the Quranic verses shows that "Mukhlis" (with a kasra) is mostly used in cases where a person is in the initial stages of perfection and in the state of self-building: "When they board the ship, they call upon Allah, making their religion sincere for Him" (Al-Ankabut: 65); "And they were not commanded except to worship Allah, making the religion sincere for Him" (Al-Bayyinah: 5).

But "Mukhlas" (with a fatha) is applied to the lofty stage that is achieved after a period of Jihad against the self—the very stage where Satan despairs of his temptation influencing the person; in truth, they become divinely insured: "He [Satan] said: 'By Your might, I will surely mislead them all, except Your sincere servants among them'" (Sad: 83).

And Yusuf had reached this stage, such that in that critical state, he stood firm like a mountain; one must strive to reach this stage.

3. Dignity and Chastity of Expression

One of the wonders of the Quran, which is one of the signs of its inimitability (I'jaz), is that there is no offensive, coarse, discordant, vulgar, or unchaste expression in it. It is not at all consistent with the style of expressions of an ordinary unlettered individual raised in an environment of ignorance, even though everyone's speech is usually consistent with and colored by their thoughts and environment.

Among all the stories the Quran narrates, there is one real love story, and that is the story of Yusuf and the wife of the Al-Aziz of Egypt. A story that speaks of the burning and fiery love of a beautiful, lustful woman for a moon-faced and pure-hearted youth.

When speakers and writers encounter such scenes, they are either forced to let their tongue or pen loose to depict the characters and the main scenes of the story—so to speak, to do justice to the narrative—even if a thousand types of provocative, offensive, or immoral expressions are used. Or they are forced, to preserve the decency and chastity of the tongue and pen, to wrap some scenes in a veil of ambiguity and deliver them to the readers and listeners in a closed manner. No matter how skilled a speaker or writer is, they are often caught in one of these two difficulties.

Can it be believed that an unlettered individual could provide both a precise and complete depiction of the narrowest and most sensitive chapters of such a passionate love, without using the slightest provocative or unchaste expression?

Yet the Quran, in depicting the sensitive scenes of this story, has wonderfully blended "precision in expression" with "dignity and chastity," and without ignoring the events or showing inability, it has also applied all the principles of morality and chastity.

We know that of all the scenes in this story, the most sensitive is the description of the events of that "private chamber of love" which the "initiative" and "lust" of the Al-Aziz’s wife joined hands to create. The Quran has said everything that needs to be said in describing this event, yet it has not made the slightest deviation from the principles of chaste speech, where it says:

وَ راوَدَتْهُ الَّتِي هُوَ فِي بَيْتِها عَنْ نَفْسِهِ وَ غَلَّقَتِ الْأَبْوابَ وَ قالَتْ هَيْتَ لَكَ قالَ مَعاذَ اللَّهِ إِنَّهُ رَبِّي أَحْسَنَ مَثْوايَ إِنَّهُ لا يُفْلِحُ الظَّالِمُونَ‌ (Yusuf: 23): "And the lady in whose house he was sought to seduce him, and she closed the doors and said, 'Come, [I am] for you.' He said, '[I seek] refuge in Allah! Indeed, he is my master, he has made my stay honorable. Indeed, the wrongdoers will not succeed.'"

[Points Worthy of Reflection in the Verse]

The points worthy of reflection in this verse are as follows:

  1. The word "rawada" is used where someone asks for something from another with persistence mixed with softness and gentleness. (But what had the Al-Aziz’s wife asked of Yusuf?) Since it was clear, the Quran sufficed with this obvious allusion and did not name it.

  2. The Quran here does not even use the expression "Imra'at al-Aziz" (the wife of the Al-Aziz); rather, it says: "الَّتِي هُوَ فِي بَيْتِها" (the lady in whose house he was), to be closer to veiling and chastity of expression. Simultaneously, with this expression, it portrays Yusuf’s sense of gratitude, just as it portrays Yusuf’s difficulties in not surrendering to someone in whose grasp his life lay.

  3. "Ghallaqat al-abwab" (she bolted the doors) conveys an intensive meaning and indicates that she closed all the doors firmly, and this is a depiction of that provocative scene.

  4. The phrase "قالَتْ هَيْتَ لَكَ‌" which means "Hasten to what is prepared for you" or "Come, for I am at your disposal," describes the final words from the tongue of the Al-Aziz’s wife to reach union with Yusuf, but in a heavy, dignified, and meaningful phrase without any provocative or ill-teaching aspect.

  5. The phrase "مَعاذَ اللَّهِ إِنَّهُ رَبِّي أَحْسَنَ مَثْوايَ‌" which Yusuf said in response to the invitation of that beautiful, enchanting woman, according to most commentators, means: "I seek refuge in Allah; the Al-Aziz of Egypt, your husband, is my master and superior, and he respects me and has trusted me; how can I betray him? This act is both betrayal and oppression." "إِنَّهُ لا يُفْلِحُ الظَّالِمُونَ‌" (Indeed, the wrongdoers will not succeed). In this way, it describes Yusuf’s effort to awaken the human emotions of the Al-Aziz’s wife.

  6. The phrase "وَ لَقَدْ هَمَّتْ بِهِ وَ هَمَّ بِها لَوْ لا أَنْ‌ رآ برهان ربه" is, on one hand, a precise depiction of that private chamber of love—that the situation was so provocative that if Yusuf did not possess the station of "reason" or "faith" or "infallibility," he would have been caught—and on the other hand, it beautifully describes Yusuf’s final victory in such conditions over the demon of rebellious lust.

Interestingly, only the word "hamm" (intent/desire) is used, meaning "the Al-Aziz’s wife had made her decision, and Yusuf, too, would have made his decision had he not seen the proof of his Lord." Can a more dignified word than "intent and decision" be found here?!

Ayahs

وَٱسْتَبَقَا ٱلْبَابَ وَقَدَّتْ قَمِيصَهُۥ مِن دُبُرٍ وَأَلْفَيَا سَيِّدَهَا لَدَا ٱلْبَابِ ۚ قَالَتْ مَا جَزَآءُ مَنْ أَرَادَ بِأَهْلِكَ سُوٓءًا إِلَّآ أَن يُسْجَنَ أَوْ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ (٢٥)
They raced to the door, and she tore his shirt from behind, and they ran into her husband at the door. She said, ‘What is to be the requital of him who has evil intentions for your wife except imprisonment or a painful punishment?’ (25)
قَالَ هِىَ رَٰوَدَتْنِى عَن نَّفْسِى ۚ وَشَهِدَ شَاهِدٌ مِّنْ أَهْلِهَآ إِن كَانَ قَمِيصُهُۥ قُدَّ مِن قُبُلٍ فَصَدَقَتْ وَهُوَ مِنَ ٱلْكَـٰذِبِينَ (٢٦)
He said, ‘It was she who solicited me.’ A witness of her own household testified [thus]: ‘If his shirt is torn from the front, she tells the truth and he lies. (26)
وَإِن كَانَ قَمِيصُهُۥ قُدَّ مِن دُبُرٍ فَكَذَبَتْ وَهُوَ مِنَ ٱلصَّـٰدِقِينَ (٢٧)
But if his shirt is torn from behind, then she lies and he tells the truth.’ (27)
فَلَمَّا رَءَا قَمِيصَهُۥ قُدَّ مِن دُبُرٍ قَالَ إِنَّهُۥ مِن كَيْدِكُنَّ ۖ إِنَّ كَيْدَكُنَّ عَظِيمٌ (٢٨)
So when he saw that his shirt was torn from behind, he said, ‘This is [a case] of you women’s guile! Your guile is indeed great! (28)
يُوسُفُ أَعْرِضْ عَنْ هَـٰذَا ۚ وَٱسْتَغْفِرِى لِذَنۢبِكِ ۖ إِنَّكِ كُنتِ مِنَ ٱلْخَاطِـِٔينَ (٢٩)
Joseph, let this matter alone, and you, woman, plead for forgiveness for your sin, for you have indeed been erring.’ (29)

Commentary

The Scandal of Al-Aziz’s Wife is Exposed!

Yusuf’s stubborn resistance nearly drove the wife of al-Aziz to despair. However, Yusuf, who had emerged victorious in this round of struggle against that flirtatious woman and the rebellious desires of the soul, felt that staying any longer in that place of temptation was dangerous and that he must distance himself from that location. Therefore, "he raced toward the palace door to open it and exit. The wife of al-Aziz did not remain indifferent; she also ran after Yusuf toward the door to prevent his departure. To achieve this, she grabbed his shirt from behind and pulled it back, such that the back of the shirt was torn lengthwise" (وَ اسْتَبَقَا الْبابَ وَ قَدَّتْ قَمِيصَهُ مِنْ دُبُرٍ).

"Istibaq" (استباق) literally means two or more people racing to surpass one another. "Qadd" (قد) means to tear lengthwise, just as "Qatt" (قط) means to tear crosswise. Hence, we have in a Hadith:

كانت ضربات على بن ابى طالب (ع) ابكارا كان اذا اعتلى قد، و اذا اعترض قط "The strikes of Ali ibn Abi Talib عليه السلام were unprecedented; when he struck from above, he split [the opponent] lengthwise, and when he struck crosswise, he cut them in two." [^1]

Regardless, Yusuf reached the door and opened it. Suddenly, they saw the Aziz of Egypt behind the door, as the Quran says: "And they found her lord [husband] at the door" (وَ أَلْفَيا سَيِّدَها لَدَى الْبابِ).

"Alfayta" (ألفيت) comes from the root "Alfa" (الفاء), meaning to find suddenly. The use of the term "Sayyid" (lord/master) for the husband was, as some commentators have noted, according to the custom and habit of the Egyptian people, where wives addressed their husbands as "Sayyid." In modern Persian as well, women refer to their husbands as "Aqa" (Sir/Master).

At this moment, as the wife of al-Aziz saw herself on the brink of scandal on one hand, and the flames of revenge were leaping within her soul on the other, the first thing that came to her mind was to turn to her husband with a self-righteous air and accuse Yusuf, saying: "She cried out: 'What is the recompense for one who intended evil against your wife except that he be imprisoned or a painful punishment?'" (قالَتْ ما جَزاءُ مَنْ أَرادَ بِأَهْلِكَ سُوءاً إِلَّا أَنْ يُسْجَنَ أَوْ عَذابٌ أَلِيمٌ).

It is interesting that this treacherous woman, until she saw herself on the verge of disgrace, had forgotten she was the wife of the Aziz of Egypt. But at this moment, by using the term ahlika (your family/wife), she wants to stir the sense of honor (ghayrah) in the Aziz, implying: "I belong specifically to you; no one else should cast a covetous eye upon me!" This speech is not unlike the words of the Pharaoh of Egypt in the era of Musa عليه السلام, who, while leaning on his throne of power, would say: "Is not the kingdom of Egypt mine?" (أَ لَيْسَ لِي مُلْكُ مِصْرَ - Az-Zukhruf: 51). But when he saw his throne and crown in danger and his star of fortune on the verge of setting, he said: "These two brothers (Musa and Harun) want to drive you out of your land!" (يُرِيدانِ أَنْ يُخْرِجاكُمْ مِنْ أَرْضِكُمْ - Taha: 63).

Another noteworthy point is that the wife of the Aziz never explicitly said Yusuf had an evil intention toward her; rather, she spoke to the Aziz about the level of his punishment, as if the core issue was already a proven fact, and the discussion was merely about the degree and type of penalty. This calculated expression at a moment when the woman should have lost her composure is a sign of her intense cunning. [^1]

[^1]: Majma' al-Bayan, under the verse. [^1]: There is a discussion among commentators whether "Ma" in the phrase "Ma jazau..." is negative or interrogative, but the result in either case does not differ much.

Furthermore, this phrasing—where she first speaks of imprisonment and then, as if not satisfied with prison, goes further and speaks of "painful punishment" which extends to torture or execution—is significant.

At this point, Yusuf did not consider silence permissible at all and explicitly unveiled the secret of the Aziz's wife's passion, saying: "It was she who sought to seduce me" (قالَ هِيَ راوَدَتْنِي عَنْ نَفْسِي). Naturally, in such an event, anyone at the start would find it difficult to believe that a young, unmarried slave boy is innocent and a married woman of apparent status is the sinner. Therefore, the flame of accusation would likely scorch Yusuf more than the wife of the Aziz!

However, since Allah is the protector of the good and the pure, He does not allow this pious young man, who struggled against his own soul, to burn in the flames of false accusation. Thus, the Quran says: "And a witness from her household testified: 'To find the true culprit, use this clear evidence: If Yusuf's shirt is torn from the front, then she has told the truth and he is of the liars'" (وَ شَهِدَ شاهِدٌ مِنْ أَهْلِها إِنْ كانَ قَمِيصُهُ قُدَّ مِنْ قُبُلٍ فَصَدَقَتْ وَ هُوَ مِنَ الْكاذِبِينَ).

"And if his shirt is torn from the back, then she has lied and he is of the truthful" (وَ إِنْ كانَ قَمِيصُهُ قُدَّ مِنْ دُبُرٍ فَكَذَبَتْ وَ هُوَ مِنَ الصَّادِقِينَ). What evidence could be more vivid than this? For if the demand had come from the wife of the Aziz, she would have run after Yusuf while he was fleeing, and as she grabbed his shirt, it would certainly be torn from the back. But if Yusuf had attacked the wife of the Aziz and she had fled or stood face-to-face to defend herself, Yusuf's shirt would certainly have been torn from the front. How remarkable it is that this simple matter of a torn shirt changes the life path of an innocent man, and this small thing becomes a document of his purity and a proof of the culprit's disgrace!

The Aziz of Egypt liked this judgment, which was very calculated, and stared at Yusuf's shirt. "And when he saw that his shirt was torn from the back (especially considering that he had never heard a lie from Yusuf until that day), he turned to his wife and said: 'Indeed, this is of your [women's] guile; indeed, your guile is great'" (فَلَمَّا رَأى‌ قَمِيصَهُ قُدَّ مِنْ دُبُرٍ قالَ إِنَّهُ مِنْ كَيْدِكُنَّ إِنَّ كَيْدَكُنَّ عَظِيمٌ).

At this moment, the Aziz of Egypt, fearing that this tragic event might become public and his reputation in the land of Egypt would be ruined, saw fit to wrap up the matter and cover it up. He turned to Yusuf and said: "Yusuf, overlook this and say no more of this matter" (يُوسُفُ أَعْرِضْ عَنْ هذا). Then he turned to his wife and said: "And ask forgiveness for your sin; indeed, you were of the sinners" (وَ اسْتَغْفِرِي لِذَنْبِكِ إِنَّكِ كُنْتِ مِنَ الْخاطِئِينَ). [^1] Some have said the speaker of these words was not the Aziz of Egypt but rather the witness, but there is no evidence for this possibility, especially since this sentence occurs after the speech of the Aziz.

[^1]: In this sentence, "min al-khati'in" (مِنَ الْخاطِئِينَ) is used, which is the masculine plural, rather than "min al-khati'at" (من الخاطئات), the feminine plural. This is because in many cases, the masculine plural is used to encompass both groups by way of predominance (taghlib), meaning "you are among the ranks of the sinners."

Key Points

1- Who Was the Witness?

There is a discussion among commentators as to who the witness was that settled the case of Yusuf and the Aziz's wife so quickly, distinguishing the innocent from the guilty. Some have said it was a relative of the Aziz's wife, and the phrase "min ahliha" (from her household) supports this. He was likely a wise, learned, and sharp-witted man who, in an event where no other witness was present, was able to see the truth through a tear in a shirt! They say this man was one of the advisors to the Aziz of Egypt and was with him at that hour.

Another interpretation is that an infant from the relatives of the Aziz's wife was nearby, and Yusuf asked the Aziz to seek judgment from this child. The Aziz was initially astonished—how could such a thing be possible? But when the nursing infant spoke—like Jesus in the cradle—and provided this criterion for identifying the sinner from the innocent, he realized that Yusuf was not just a slave, but a prophet or like a prophet!

This interpretation is mentioned in narrations from both the Ahl al-Bayt and Sunni sources. Among them, Ibn Abbas narrates from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله that he said: "Four spoke in infancy: the son of Pharaoh's hairdresser, the witness of Yusuf, the companion of Jurayj, and Isa ibn Maryam." [^1] In the Tafsir of Ali ibn Ibrahim, it is also narrated from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام that the witness was an infant in the cradle. [^2]

[^1]: Tafsir al-Manar, Vol. 12, p. 287. [^2]: Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 2, p. 422.

However, it should be noted that neither of the two Hadiths above has a strong chain of narration; rather, both are marfu' (interrupted).

A third possibility suggested is that the "witness" was the very tear in the shirt, which testified through its state (lisan al-hal). However, given the phrase "min ahliha" (a witness from her household), this possibility seems very unlikely, if not impossible.

2- Why Was the Reaction of the Aziz So Mild?

One of the issues that draws one's attention in this story is how, in such a significant matter where the honor of the Aziz's wife was compromised, he contented himself with a single sentence, merely saying: "Ask forgiveness for your sin, for you were of the sinners." Perhaps this very issue was why the wife of the Aziz, after her secrets were exposed in Egypt, invited the noblewomen to a special gathering and recounted her story of love explicitly and openly.

Did the fear of scandal force the Aziz to be lenient in this matter? Or is it that for autocratic rulers and tyrants, the issue of honor and protecting one's wife is not very significant? They are so immersed in sin, corruption, and indecency that the importance and gravity of this subject have vanished in their eyes. The second possibility seems stronger.

3- Divine Support in Critical Moments

Another great lesson this part of Yusuf's story teaches us is the extensive support of the Lord, who rushes to the aid of a human in the most critical states. According to the principle "He will make for him a way out and provide for him from where he does not expect," a window of hope opens for him through ways he never imagined, and a tear in a shirt becomes the document of his purity and exoneration. That same eventful shirt which one day disgraced Yusuf's brothers before their father because it was not torn, and another day disgraced the lustful wife of the Aziz because it was torn, and on another day brought light to the sightless eyes of Ya'qub, its familiar scent traveling with the morning breeze from Egypt to Canaan to bring the old man of Canaan the glad tidings of the bearer of good news!

In any case, Allah has hidden favors (altaf khafiyyah) whose depths no one knows. When the breeze of this grace blows, scenes change in such a way that no one, even the most intelligent individuals, could have predicted. A shirt, for all its smallness, is a significant thing. Sometimes, a few spider webs change the course of a nation's life forever, as happened in the story of the Cave of Thawr and the migration of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله.

4- The Plot of the Aziz's Wife

In the verses above, reference is made to the guile of women (of course, women like the wife of the Aziz who are unrestrained and lustful), and this guile is described as "great" (إِنَّ كَيْدَكُنَّ عَظِيمٌ). In history, as well as in stories that are shadows of history, much has been narrated in this regard. Studying them collectively shows that lustful women, to reach their goals, devise plans that are unique in their kind.

In the story above, we saw how the wife of the Aziz, after failing in love and standing on the brink of scandal, presented her own innocence and Yusuf's guilt with special skill. She did not even say that Yusuf had an evil intention toward her; rather, she assumed it as an established fact and only asked about the punishment for such a person—a punishment that did not stop at imprisonment but was presented in an unknown and unlimited form.

In the story of this same woman regarding the Egyptian women's reproach of her restless love for her slave boy in the following verses, we see that she uses such a calculated trick to vindicate herself, and this is another emphasis on the guile of such women.

Ayahs

۞ وَقَالَ نِسْوَةٌ فِى ٱلْمَدِينَةِ ٱمْرَأَتُ ٱلْعَزِيزِ تُرَٰوِدُ فَتَىٰهَا عَن نَّفْسِهِۦ ۖ قَدْ شَغَفَهَا حُبًّا ۖ إِنَّا لَنَرَىٰهَا فِى ضَلَـٰلٍ مُّبِينٍ (٣٠)
Some of the townswomen said, ‘The chieftain’s wife has solicited her slave boy! He has captivated her love. Indeed we see her to be in manifest error.’ (30)
فَلَمَّا سَمِعَتْ بِمَكْرِهِنَّ أَرْسَلَتْ إِلَيْهِنَّ وَأَعْتَدَتْ لَهُنَّ مُتَّكَـًٔا وَءَاتَتْ كُلَّ وَٰحِدَةٍ مِّنْهُنَّ سِكِّينًا وَقَالَتِ ٱخْرُجْ عَلَيْهِنَّ ۖ فَلَمَّا رَأَيْنَهُۥٓ أَكْبَرْنَهُۥ وَقَطَّعْنَ أَيْدِيَهُنَّ وَقُلْنَ حَـٰشَ لِلَّهِ مَا هَـٰذَا بَشَرًا إِنْ هَـٰذَآ إِلَّا مَلَكٌ كَرِيمٌ (٣١)
When she heard of their machinations, she sent for them and arranged a repast, and gave each of them a knife, and said [to Joseph], ‘Come out before them.’ So when they saw him, they marvelled at him and cut their hands [absent-mindedly], and they said, ‘Good heavens! This is not a human being! This is but a noble angel!’ (31)
قَالَتْ فَذَٰلِكُنَّ ٱلَّذِى لُمْتُنَّنِى فِيهِ ۖ وَلَقَدْ رَٰوَدتُّهُۥ عَن نَّفْسِهِۦ فَٱسْتَعْصَمَ ۖ وَلَئِن لَّمْ يَفْعَلْ مَآ ءَامُرُهُۥ لَيُسْجَنَنَّ وَلَيَكُونًا مِّنَ ٱلصَّـٰغِرِينَ (٣٢)
She said, ‘He is the one on whose account you blamed me. Certainly, I did solicit him, but he was continent, and if he does not do what I bid him, surely he shall be imprisoned and humbled.’ (32)
قَالَ رَبِّ ٱلسِّجْنُ أَحَبُّ إِلَىَّ مِمَّا يَدْعُونَنِىٓ إِلَيْهِ ۖ وَإِلَّا تَصْرِفْ عَنِّى كَيْدَهُنَّ أَصْبُ إِلَيْهِنَّ وَأَكُن مِّنَ ٱلْجَـٰهِلِينَ (٣٣)
He said, ‘My Lord! The prison is dearer to me than to what they invite me. If You do not turn away their schemes from me, I will incline towards them and become one of the senseless.’ (33)
فَٱسْتَجَابَ لَهُۥ رَبُّهُۥ فَصَرَفَ عَنْهُ كَيْدَهُنَّ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ هُوَ ٱلسَّمِيعُ ٱلْعَلِيمُ (٣٤)
So his Lord answered him and turned away their stratagems from him. Indeed, He is the All-hearing, the All-knowing. (34)

Commentary

Another Plot by the Wife of the Aziz

Although the matter of the Aziz’s wife expressing her love—with the story that has passed—was a private matter that the Aziz himself insisted on concealing, such secrets do not remain hidden. Especially in the palaces of kings and the possessors of gold and power, where the walls have listening ears, this secret eventually leaked from within the palace to the outside. As the Quran says: "A group of women in the city began to talk among themselves and spread the word that the wife of the Aziz is seeking to seduce her slave-boy" (وَ قالَ نِسْوَةٌ فِي الْمَدِينَةِ امْرَأَتُ الْعَزِيزِ تُراوِدُ فَتاها عَنْ نَفْسِهِ‌).

"And love for the youth has so overwhelmed her that it has conquered the depths of her heart" (قَدْ شَغَفَها حُبًّا).

And then they reproached her with this sentence: "We indeed see her in manifest error!" (إِنَّا لَنَراها فِي ضَلالٍ مُبِينٍ‌).

It is clear that those who said this were the aristocratic women of Egypt, for whom news of the corruption-filled palaces of the Pharaohs and the arrogant was interesting, and they were always in search of it.

This group of aristocratic women, who were no less hedonistic than the wife of the Aziz, were essentially "acting holier-than-thou" because they had not laid hands on Yusuf themselves, and thus they viewed the wife of the Aziz as being in manifest error because of this love!

Some commentators have even suggested that the spreading of this secret by this group of Egyptian women was a plan to provoke the wife of the Aziz so that, to vindicate herself, she would invite them to the palace and they could see Yusuf there! They perhaps thought that if they reached Yusuf’s presence, they might be able to attract his attention to themselves! For they might have been more beautiful than the wife of the Aziz, and their beauty would be new to Yusuf. Furthermore, Yusuf’s respectful view toward the wife of the Aziz—which was the view of a child toward a mother, or a mentor, or a benefactor—did not apply to them. For this reason, the possibility of their influence over him was much greater than that of the wife of the Aziz!

"Shaghaf" (شغف) is derived from "shaghaf" (شغاف), meaning the knot at the top of the heart or the thin membrane over the heart that covers it like a sheath. Shaghafaha hubban means she became so fond of him that his love penetrated the interior of her heart and encompassed its depths. This refers to an intense and fiery love.

Alusi, in the commentary Ruh al-Ma'ani, citing the book Asrar al-Balaghah, mentions stages for love and affection, some of which are noted here:

The first stage of affection is "hawa" (meaning inclination), then "alaqah," which is love that clings to the heart. After that is "kalaf," meaning intensity of love, then "ishq" (passionate love), followed by "sha'af" (with the letter ayn), which is a state where the heart burns in the fire of love and feels pleasure from this burning. After that is "law'ah," then "shaghaf," which is the stage where love penetrates all corners of the heart. Then comes "tadalluh," the stage where love robs a person of their reason, and the final stage is "huyum," which is the stage of absolute restlessness that drives the lover involuntarily in every direction [1]. It is also noteworthy who revealed this secret: the wife of the Aziz, who never favored such a scandal? Or the Aziz himself, who insisted on concealment? Or the wise judge who gave the verdict, for whom such an act seemed unlikely? However, in any case, such matters—especially in those palaces full of corruption, as we said—are not things that can be hidden. Eventually, they leak out from the main orchestrators to the courtiers and from there to the outside in bits and pieces, and naturally, others pass it on from tongue to tongue with much embellishment.

"When the wife of the Aziz became aware of the scheme of the cunning women of Egypt, she was first upset, then she devised a plan. This was to invite them to a banquet and prepare a luxurious setting with expensive cushions for them, and give each of them a knife to cut fruit" (but sharp knives, sharper than needed for cutting fruit!) (فَلَمَّا سَمِعَتْ بِمَكْرِهِنَّ أَرْسَلَتْ إِلَيْهِنَّ وَ أَعْتَدَتْ لَهُنَّ مُتَّكَأً وَ آتَتْ كُلَّ واحِدَةٍ مِنْهُنَّ سِكِّيناً) [2]

[1]: Tafsir Ruh al-Ma'ani, Vol. 12, p. 203.

[2]: "Muttaqa" (متكا) means something to lean upon, such as cushions, couches, and chairs, as was customary in the palaces of that time. However, some have interpreted "muttaqa" as "utrujj," which is a type of fruit. Those who interpreted "muttaqa" as cushions and the like also said the fruit was "utrujj." Utrujj is the citron, a sour fruit with a thick rind from which jam is made. It is possible that this fruit in the land of Egypt was not completely sour like what we have, but had a streak of sweetness, making it a fruit for hospitality.

And this act itself is proof that she did not take her husband into account and did not learn a lesson from her past scandal.

"Then she ordered Yusuf to step into that gathering" so that the reproachful women, upon seeing his beauty, would not blame her for this love (وَ قالَتِ اخْرُجْ عَلَيْهِنَ‌).

The expression ukhruj alayhinna ("come out before them") instead of udkhul ("enter") implies that the wife of the Aziz did not keep Yusuf outside, but rather kept him busy in an inner room—likely where the food and fruit were—so that his entry into the gathering would not be through the main entrance, but completely unexpected and shocking!

But the women of Egypt—who according to some narrations were ten or more—when they saw that beautiful stature and radiant face, and their eyes fell upon Yusuf’s enchanting countenance—a face like the sun suddenly appearing from behind a cloud and dazzling the eyes—as he rose in that gathering, they became so enamored and bewildered that they lost all self-control. "When they saw Yusuf, they extolled him and considered him extraordinary" (فَلَمَّا رَأَيْنَهُ أَكْبَرْنَهُ‌).

"And they became so beside themselves that (instead of the fruit) they cut their hands" (وَ قَطَّعْنَ أَيْدِيَهُنَّ).

And when they saw the spark of modesty and chastity shining from his attractive eyes, and his innocent face flushed with intense modesty and shame, "they all cried out: No! This youth is never defiled; he is not a human at all; he is but a noble heavenly angel" (وَ قُلْنَ حاشَ لِلَّهِ ما هذا بَشَراً إِنْ هذا إِلَّا مَلَكٌ كَرِيمٌ‌) [1].

[1]: Hasha lillah (حاشَ لِلَّهِ‌) is from the root hasha, meaning side or direction, and tahashi means to step aside. The concept of the phrase hasha lillah is "God is pure/exempt," implying that Yusuf is also a pure and exempt servant.

There is discussion among commentators as to how much the women of Egypt cut their hands at this moment; some have narrated it in exaggerated forms, but what can be gathered from the Quran is that they generally wounded their hands.

At this moment, the women of Egypt completely lost their composure, and with wounded hands dripping with blood, standing frozen like soulless statues in a state of distress, they showed that they were no different from the wife of the Aziz. She took advantage of this opportunity and "said: This is the one for whose love you reproached me" (قالَتْ فَذلِكُنَّ الَّذِي لُمْتُنَّنِي فِيهِ‌).

The wife of the Aziz seemingly wanted to tell them: You, who by seeing Yusuf just once, lost your reason and senses, unknowingly cut your hands, became absorbed in his beauty, and began to praise him—how can you blame me, who sits and rises with him morning and evening?

The wife of the Aziz, feeling pride and joy at the success of the plan she had laid and having made her excuse appear justified, suddenly cast aside all veils and confessed to her sin with complete clarity, saying: "Yes, I did seek to seduce him, but he remained firm in chastity" (وَ لَقَدْ راوَدْتُهُ عَنْ نَفْسِهِ فَاسْتَعْصَمَ‌).
