Then, without expressing any remorse for this stain of sin, or at least maintaining some appearances before the guests, with utter audacity and a serious tone indicating her firm will, she explicitly declared: "If he (Yusuf) does not do what I command him and does not surrender to my burning love, he shall certainly be imprisoned" (وَ لَئِنْ لَمْ يَفْعَلْ ما آمُرُهُ لَيُسْجَنَنَ‌).

Not only will I throw him into prison, but "inside the prison, he will also be among the disgraced" (وَ لَيَكُوناً مِنَ الصَّاغِرِينَ‌).

It is natural that when the Aziz of Egypt contents himself with the sentence "and ask forgiveness for your sin" in the face of his wife's blatant betrayal, his wife would take the scandal to this stage. Fundamentally, in the courts of Pharaohs, kings, and nobles, as we said, these matters are nothing new.

Some have narrated a startling report here: that a group of the Egyptian women present in that session rose to support the wife of the Aziz, agreed with her, and surrounded Yusuf. Each spoke a different kind of word to encourage Yusuf to surrender:

One said: O youth! Why all this self-restraint and playing hard to get? Why do you not have mercy on this devoted lover? Do you not see this dazzling, heart-stealing beauty? Do you not have a heart, and are you not young, and do you not enjoy love and beauty? Are you made of stone or wood?!

The second said: Suppose you understand nothing of beauty and love, but do you not know that she is the wife of the Aziz of Egypt and the powerful woman of this land? Do you not think that if you win her heart, all this apparatus will be at your disposal? And any position you desire will be ready for you?

The third said: Suppose you have neither an inclination for her beautiful beauty nor a need for her position and wealth, but do you not know that she is a dangerous, vengeful woman? And she has the means of revenge fully at her disposal? Do you not fear her terrifying and dark prison, and do you not think of the double exile in this prison of loneliness?!

The explicit threat of the wife of the Aziz with prison and humiliation on one hand, and the whispers of these defiled women who were now playing the role of intermediaries on the other, created an intense moment of crisis for Yusuf. A storm of difficulties surrounded him from every side. But he, who had built himself up beforehand, and in whose soul the light of faith, purity, and piety had created a special tranquility and sakinah, made his decision with courage and bravery. Without entering into conversation with the hedonistic and lustful women, he turned to the threshold of the Lord and prayed thus: "O Allah, my Lord! Prison, with all its hardships, is more beloved to me than that to which these women call me" (قالَ رَبِّ السِّجْنُ أَحَبُّ إِلَيَّ مِمَّا يَدْعُونَنِي إِلَيْهِ‌).

Then, since he knew that in all circumstances, especially in moments of crisis, there is no way of salvation except by relying on the grace of the Lord, he entrusted himself to Allah with these words and sought His help: "My Lord, if You do not turn away from me the guile and dangerous schemes of these defiled women, my heart will incline toward them and I shall be among the ignorant" (وَ إِلَّا تَصْرِفْ عَنِّي كَيْدَهُنَّ أَصْبُ إِلَيْهِنَّ وَ أَكُنْ مِنَ الْجاهِلِينَ‌).

O Allah! For the sake of observing Your command and preserving my own chastity, I welcome that terrifying prison—a prison where my soul is free and my honor is pure—and I turn my back on this apparent freedom that would imprison my soul in the jail of lust and defile my honor.

O Allah! Help me, give me strength, and increase the power of my reason, faith, and piety so that I may triumph over these satanic whispers.

And since the divine promise has always been to help the sincere strivers (whether against the self or against the enemy), He did not leave Yusuf alone in this state, and the grace of the Truth rushed to his aid, as the Quran says: "So his Lord answered his sincere prayer" (فَاسْتَجابَ لَهُ رَبُّهُ‌).

"And He turned away their guile and scheme from him" (فَصَرَفَ عَنْهُ كَيْدَهُنَ‌).

"For He is the All-Hearing, the All-Knowing" (إِنَّهُ هُوَ السَّمِيعُ الْعَلِيمُ).

He both hears the prayers of His servants and is aware of their inner secrets, and He knows the solution to their problems.

Key Points

1- To what means did the wife of the Aziz and the women of Egypt resort to reach their goal?

As we saw, the wife of the Aziz and the women of Egypt used various means to achieve their goal: expressing intense love and affection, total surrender, then enticement, and after that, threat. Or in other words, resorting to "lust" and "gold," and then "force."

These are the standard principles that all autocrats and tyrants in every age and time have resorted to. Even we have repeatedly seen that to make men of truth surrender, they initially show extraordinary flexibility and a friendly face in a meeting, entering through enticement and various types of aid, and at the end of the same meeting, they resort to the most severe threats. They do not consider how ugly, repulsive, and worthy of contempt and all kinds of reproach this contradiction is, especially in a single gathering.

The reason is clear: they seek their goal; the means are not important to them. Or in other words, they consider the use of any means permissible to reach the goal.

In the midst of this, weak and immature individuals surrender in the first or last stages and are caught in their trap forever. But the saints of Truth, with the courage and bravery they have found in the light of faith, pass through all these stages and show their uncompromising nature with the utmost decisiveness, going as far as death, and the end of it is victory—the victory of themselves and their school of thought, or at least the victory of the school of thought.

2- Those on the sidelines show themselves as pure

There are many who, like the hedonistic women of Egypt, show themselves as pure and clean when they are sitting on the sidelines, boasting of piety and virtue, and seeing those defiled like the wife of the Aziz in "manifest error."

But when they are pulled into the center of the ring, they are defeated at the very first blow and practically prove that all they said was nothing more than words. If the wife of the Aziz fell for Yusuf’s love after years of sitting and rising with him, they fell into such a fate in the very first meeting and cut their own hands instead of the citron!

3- Why did Yusuf enter the gathering of the women?

A question arises here: why did Yusuf accept the word of the wife of the Aziz and agree to step into her gathering—a gathering arranged for sin or to vindicate a sinner?

However, considering that Yusuf was apparently a slave and servant and was obliged to serve in the palace, the wife of the Aziz might have used this excuse and brought him into the gathering on the pretext of bringing a dish of food or a drink, while Yusuf was absolutely unaware of this feminine scheme and guile.

Especially since we said the apparent expression of the Quran (ukhruj alayhinna) shows that he was not outside that establishment but was in an adjacent room which was the place for food and fruit or the like.

4- The phrase "they call me" and "their guile"

The phrase "they call me to it" (يَدْعُونَنِي إِلَيْهِ‌) and "their guile" (كَيْدَهُنَ‌) clearly shows that after the incident of cutting the hands and the infatuation of the hedonistic Egyptian women with Yusuf, they too, in turn, entered the field and invited Yusuf to surrender to them or to the wife of the Aziz, and he rejected all of them. This shows that the wife of the Aziz was not alone in this sin and had "accomplices."

5- One must take refuge in Allah at the edge of precipices

When caught in the clutches of difficulties and at times when events pull a person to the edge of precipices, one must only take refuge in Allah and seek His help, for if His grace and aid are not there, nothing can be done. This is the lesson that the great and chaste Yusuf taught us. It is he who says: My Lord, if You do not turn away their evil schemes from me, I too will incline toward them; if You leave me alone in this peril, the storm of events will carry me away. It is You who are my Guardian and Protector, not my own strength, power, or piety!

This state of "absolute dependence" on the grace of the Lord, in addition to granting unlimited strength and steadfastness to the servants of Allah, causes them to benefit from His hidden graces—those graces whose description is impossible and which must only be observed and affirmed.

These are the ones who are under the grace of the Lord both in this world and in the next.

In a hadith from the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, we read:

"There are seven groups whom Allah will shade in the shade of His Throne on the day when there is no shade but His shade: a just leader; a youth who grew up in the worship of Allah, the Mighty and Sublime; a man whose heart is attached to the mosque when he leaves it until he returns to it; two people who were in the obedience of Allah, the Mighty and Sublime, meeting upon that and parting upon it; a man who remembered Allah, the Mighty and Sublime, in private and his eyes overflowed with tears; a man whom a woman of beauty and charm invited to herself and he said, 'I fear Allah the Exalted'; and a man who gave charity and hid it so that his left hand did not know what his right hand had given!" [1]

[1]: Safinat al-Bihar, Vol. 1, p. 595 (Zill).

Ayahs

ثُمَّ بَدَا لَهُم مِّنۢ بَعْدِ مَا رَأَوُا۟ ٱلْـَٔايَـٰتِ لَيَسْجُنُنَّهُۥ حَتَّىٰ حِينٍ (٣٥)
Then it appeared to them, after they had seen all the signs [of his innocence], that they should confine him for some time. (35)
وَدَخَلَ مَعَهُ ٱلسِّجْنَ فَتَيَانِ ۖ قَالَ أَحَدُهُمَآ إِنِّىٓ أَرَىٰنِىٓ أَعْصِرُ خَمْرًا ۖ وَقَالَ ٱلْـَٔاخَرُ إِنِّىٓ أَرَىٰنِىٓ أَحْمِلُ فَوْقَ رَأْسِى خُبْزًا تَأْكُلُ ٱلطَّيْرُ مِنْهُ ۖ نَبِّئْنَا بِتَأْوِيلِهِۦٓ ۖ إِنَّا نَرَىٰكَ مِنَ ٱلْمُحْسِنِينَ (٣٦)
There entered the prison two youths along with him. One of them said, ‘I dreamt that I am pressing grapes.’ The other said, ‘I dreamt that I am carrying bread on my head from which the birds are eating.’ ‘Inform us of its interpretation,’ [they said], ‘for indeed we see you to be a virtuous man.’ (36)
قَالَ لَا يَأْتِيكُمَا طَعَامٌ تُرْزَقَانِهِۦٓ إِلَّا نَبَّأْتُكُمَا بِتَأْوِيلِهِۦ قَبْلَ أَن يَأْتِيَكُمَا ۚ ذَٰلِكُمَا مِمَّا عَلَّمَنِى رَبِّىٓ ۚ إِنِّى تَرَكْتُ مِلَّةَ قَوْمٍ لَّا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِٱللَّهِ وَهُم بِٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ هُمْ كَـٰفِرُونَ (٣٧)
He said, ‘Before the meals you are served come to you I will inform you of its interpretation. That is among things my Lord has taught me. Indeed, I renounce the creed of the people who have no faith in Allah and who [also] disbelieve in the Hereafter. (37)
وَٱتَّبَعْتُ مِلَّةَ ءَابَآءِىٓ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ وَإِسْحَـٰقَ وَيَعْقُوبَ ۚ مَا كَانَ لَنَآ أَن نُّشْرِكَ بِٱللَّهِ مِن شَىْءٍ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ مِن فَضْلِ ٱللَّهِ عَلَيْنَا وَعَلَى ٱلنَّاسِ وَلَـٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ ٱلنَّاسِ لَا يَشْكُرُونَ (٣٨)
I follow the creed of my fathers, Abraham, Isaac and Jacob. It is not for us to ascribe any partner to Allah. That is by virtue of Allah’s grace upon us and upon all mankind, but most people do not give thanks. (38)

Commentary

Imprisonment for the Crime of Innocence!

The extraordinary gathering of the Egyptian women with Yusuf in the Aziz’s palace ended in a clamor, but naturally, the news reached the ears of the Aziz. From the sum of these events, it became clear that Yusuf was no ordinary young man; he was so pure that no power could compel him toward corruption. The signs of this purity were evident from various angles: the tearing of Yusuf’s shirt from behind, his resistance against the temptations of the Egyptian women, his readiness to go to prison, and his refusal to submit to the threats of the Aziz’s wife regarding imprisonment and painful torment—all of these were proofs of his innocence, proofs that no one could cover up or deny.

The logical consequence of these proofs was the establishment of the guilt and impurity of the wife of the Aziz of Egypt. Following the confirmation of this guilt, the fear of a public sex scandal and the disgrace of the Aziz’s household in the eyes of the masses grew day by day. The only remedy seen by the Aziz of Egypt and his advisors was to remove Yusuf entirely from the scene, so that people would consign him and his name to oblivion. The best way to achieve this was to send him to the dungeon of a prison, which would both cause him to be forgotten and be interpreted by the public as meaning that the primary culprit was Yusuf!

Therefore, the Quran says: "Then it occurred to them, after they had seen the signs (of Yusuf’s innocence), to imprison him for a time" (ثُمَّ بَدا لَهُمْ مِنْ بَعْدِ ما رَأَوُا الْآياتِ لَيَسْجُنُنَّهُ حَتَّى حِينٍ).

The expression "بَدا" (it occurred/appeared), which signifies the emergence of a new opinion, shows that they previously had no such intention regarding him. This idea was likely proposed by the wife of the Aziz for the first time. Thus, the innocent Yusuf went to prison for the "crime" of his chastity. This was neither the first nor the last time a worthy human being would be imprisoned for the crime of being pure.

Indeed, in a polluted environment, freedom belongs to the corrupt who drift with the current; not just freedom, but everything belongs to them. Pure and valuable individuals like Yusuf, who are not of the same kind or color as that environment and move against the current, must be isolated. But for how long? Is it forever?

No, certainly not!

Among those who entered the prison with Yusuf were two young men (وَ دَخَلَ مَعَهُ السِّجْنَ فَتَيانِ). Since a person who cannot access news through normal and routine channels often finds their other senses heightened to seek out and predict the course of events, dreams and visions also become significant to them.

For this reason, one day these two young men—one of whom is said to have been the king’s cupbearer and the other the supervisor of food and the kitchen, both imprisoned due to the slander of enemies and accusations of plotting to poison the king—came to Yusuf. Each recounted a dream he had seen the previous night, which seemed strange and interesting to him.

"One of them said: 'I saw myself in a dream pressing grapes [for wine]!'" (قالَ أَحَدُهُما إِنِّي أَرانِي أَعْصِرُ خَمْراً).

"And the other said: 'I saw in a dream that I was carrying some bread on my head, and the birds of the sky were coming and eating from it'" (وَ قالَ الْآخَرُ إِنِّي أَرانِي أَحْمِلُ فَوْقَ رَأْسِي خُبْزاً تَأْكُلُ الطَّيْرُ مِنْهُ). Then they added: "Inform us of its interpretation, for we see you as one of the righteous" (نَبِّئْنا بِتَأْوِيلِهِ إِنَّا نَراكَ مِنَ الْمُحْسِنِينَ).

There is discussion among commentators as to how these two young men knew that Yusuf had extensive knowledge of dream interpretation.

Some have said: Yusuf himself had introduced himself to the prisoners as having vast knowledge of dream interpretation. Others have said: Yusuf’s celestial countenance showed that he was no ordinary person, but rather an enlightened individual possessed of thought and insight; surely such a person could solve their problem regarding the interpretation of dreams.

Others have said: From the beginning of his entry into the prison, Yusuf had shown through his excellent character, good temperament, comforting of prisoners, serving them, and visiting the sick that he was a righteous person and a solver of problems. For this reason, they turned to him in their difficulties and sought his help.

It is also necessary to mention that here the Quran uses the term "فتى" (young man) instead of "عبد" (servant) or "slave," which is a form of respect. We have in a Hadith:

لا يقولن احدكم عبدى و امتى و لكن فتاى و فتاتى

"None of you should say 'my slave' (male) or 'my slave' (female), but rather say 'my young man' or 'my young woman'" [1] (so that during the period of the gradual liberation of slaves, for which Islam devised a precise program, slaves would be safe from any form of humiliation).

The expression "أَعْصِرُ خَمْراً" (I was pressing wine) is either because he saw himself in the dream pressing grapes to make wine, or he was pressing grapes that had fermented in a vat to filter and extract the wine from them,

[1]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 5, p. 232.

or he was pressing grapes to give the juice to the king before it became wine, and since these grapes were capable of being turned into wine, the word was applied to them.

The expression "إِنِّي أَرانِي" (I see myself)—whereas normally he should have said "I saw in a dream"—is used as a narrative of the present state; that is, he imagines himself at the very moment he is seeing the dream and speaks this way to depict that state.

In any case, Yusuf, who never missed an opportunity to guide and direct the prisoners, seized the occasion of these two prisoners approaching him for dream interpretation. Using it as a pretext, He expressed important truths that were path-finding for them and for all of humanity. First, to gain their trust regarding His knowledge of dream interpretation—which was of great concern to the two prisoners—He said: "I will inform you of the interpretation of your dream soon, before your provided meal arrives" (قالَ لا يَأْتِيكُما طَعامٌ تُرْزَقانِهِ إِلَّا نَبَّأْتُكُما بِتَأْوِيلِهِ قَبْلَ أَنْ يَأْتِيَكُما).

In this way, He assured them that they would find their sought-after answer before their mealtime arrived.

Commentators have offered many possibilities in interpreting this sentence.

Among them is that Yusuf said: By the command of my Lord, I am aware of some secrets; not only can I tell you the interpretation of your dream, but I can even tell you now what kind of food will be brought for you today, what its quality is, and I can list its characteristics.

Therefore, "ta'wil" (interpretation) would mean mentioning the characteristics of that food (but of course, "ta'wil" is rarely used in such a sense, especially since in the previous sentence it means dream interpretation).

Another possibility is that Yusuf meant that whatever food you see in a dream, I can interpret it for you (but this possibility is not consistent with the phrase "before it comes to you"). Therefore, the best interpretation of the above sentence is what we stated at the beginning.

Then Yusuf, the believer and worshiper of God, in whose depths of being Monotheism (Tawhid) with all its dimensions had taken root, continued as follows to clarify that nothing happens except by the command of the Lord:

"This knowledge and awareness of mine regarding dream interpretation is from what my Lord has taught me" (ذلِكُما مِمَّا عَلَّمَنِي رَبِّي).

And so they would not imagine that Allah grants something to someone without reason, He added: "Indeed, I have abandoned the religion of a people who do not believe in Allah and who are disbelievers in the Hereafter; and this light of faith and piety has made me worthy of such a gift" (إِنِّي تَرَكْتُ مِلَّةَ قَوْمٍ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِاللَّهِ وَ هُمْ‌ بالآخرة هم كافرون).

The "people" referred to here are the idol-worshipping people of Egypt or the idolaters of Canaan.

I must separate myself from such beliefs, for they are contrary to pure human nature. Furthermore, I have been raised in a household that is the household of revelation and prophethood: "And I have followed the religion of my fathers and ancestors, Ibrahim, Ishaq, and Ya'qub" (وَ اتَّبَعْتُ مِلَّةَ آبائِي إِبْراهِيمَ وَ إِسْحاقَ وَ يَعْقُوبَ). Perhaps this was the first time Yusuf introduced Himself this way to the prisoners, so they would know He was a child of revelation and prophethood and had fallen into prison innocently, like many other prisoners in tyrannical systems.

Then, as an emphasis, He adds: "It is not for us to associate anything with Allah" because our family is the family of Monotheism, the family of Ibrahim the idol-breaker (ما كانَ لَنا أَنْ نُشْرِكَ بِاللَّهِ مِنْ شَيْ‌ءٍ).

"This is from the grace of Allah upon us and upon mankind" (ذلِكَ مِنْ فَضْلِ اللَّهِ عَلَيْنا وَ عَلَى النَّاسِ).

Therefore, do not imagine that this grace and love only include us, the family of the Prophets; this is a general gift that includes all servants of Allah, deposited within their souls as a natural disposition (fitrah), and it reaches perfection through the leadership of the Prophets.

"But unfortunately, most people do not give thanks for these divine blessings" and they deviate from the path of Monotheism and faith (وَ لكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ النَّاسِ لا يَشْكُرُونَ).

It is noteworthy that in the above verse, Ishaq is counted among the fathers (آباء) of Yusuf, while we know that Yusuf is the son of Ya'qub and Ya'qub is the son of Ishaq. Therefore, the word "ab" (father) is also applied to a grandfather.

Ayahs

يَـٰصَـٰحِبَىِ ٱلسِّجْنِ ءَأَرْبَابٌ مُّتَفَرِّقُونَ خَيْرٌ أَمِ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْوَٰحِدُ ٱلْقَهَّارُ (٣٩)
O my prison mates! Are different masters better, or Allah, the One, the All-paramount? (39)
مَا تَعْبُدُونَ مِن دُونِهِۦٓ إِلَّآ أَسْمَآءً سَمَّيْتُمُوهَآ أَنتُمْ وَءَابَآؤُكُم مَّآ أَنزَلَ ٱللَّهُ بِهَا مِن سُلْطَـٰنٍ ۚ إِنِ ٱلْحُكْمُ إِلَّا لِلَّهِ ۚ أَمَرَ أَلَّا تَعْبُدُوٓا۟ إِلَّآ إِيَّاهُ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ ٱلدِّينُ ٱلْقَيِّمُ وَلَـٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ ٱلنَّاسِ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ (٤٠)
You do not worship besides Him but [mere] names that you and your fathers have coined, for which Allah has not sent down any authority. Sovereignty belongs only to Allah. He has commanded you to worship none except Him. That is the upright religion, but most people do not know. (40)
يَـٰصَـٰحِبَىِ ٱلسِّجْنِ أَمَّآ أَحَدُكُمَا فَيَسْقِى رَبَّهُۥ خَمْرًا ۖ وَأَمَّا ٱلْـَٔاخَرُ فَيُصْلَبُ فَتَأْكُلُ ٱلطَّيْرُ مِن رَّأْسِهِۦ ۚ قُضِىَ ٱلْأَمْرُ ٱلَّذِى فِيهِ تَسْتَفْتِيَانِ (٤١)
O my prison mates! As for one of you, he will serve wine to his master, and as for the other, he will be crucified and vultures will eat from his head. The matter about which you inquire has been decided.’ (41)
وَقَالَ لِلَّذِى ظَنَّ أَنَّهُۥ نَاجٍ مِّنْهُمَا ٱذْكُرْنِى عِندَ رَبِّكَ فَأَنسَىٰهُ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ ذِكْرَ رَبِّهِۦ فَلَبِثَ فِى ٱلسِّجْنِ بِضْعَ سِنِينَ (٤٢)
Then he said to the one whom he knew would be delivered from among the two: ‘Mention me to your master.’ But Satan caused him to forget mentioning [it] to his master. So he remained in the prison for several years. (42)

Commentary

Prison or a Center for Education?

When Yusuf, by mentioning the previous discussion, prepared the hearts of those two prisoners to accept the truth of Monotheism (Tawhid), he turned to them and said: "O my two companions of the prison! Are many lords differing among themselves better, or Allah, the One, the Irresistible (the Subduer of all things)?" (يَا صَاحِبَيِ السِّجْنِ أَأَرْبَابٌ مُتَفَرِّقُونَ خَيْرٌ أَمِ اللَّهُ الْوَاحِدُ الْقَهَّارُ).

It is as if Yusuf wants to make them realize: Why do you only see freedom in your dreams? Why do you not see it while awake? Why? Is it not because this scattering, division, and hypocrisy of yours—which stems from polytheism, idol worship, and "diverse lords"—has allowed oppressive tyrants (Taghuts) to overcome you? Why do you not gather under the banner of Monotheism and cling to the worship of "Allah, the One, the Irresistible," so that you may drive out from your society these self-willed oppressors who cast you into prison while innocent, merely upon an accusation?

Then he added: "Those whom you worship besides Him are nothing but names which you have named, you and your fathers" (مَا تَعْبُدُونَ مِنْ دُونِهِ إِلَّا أَسْمَاءً سَمَّيْتُمُوهَا أَنْتُمْ وَآبَاؤُكُمْ). "For which Allah has sent down no authority"; rather, they are the fabrications of your weak minds (مَا أَنْزَلَ اللَّهُ بِهَا مِنْ سُلْطَانٍ).

"Know that the command (sovereignty) belongs to none but Allah," and for this reason, you must not bow your heads in submission before these idols, tyrants, and Pharaohs (إِنِ الْحُكْمُ إِلَّا لِلَّهِ).

And again, for further emphasis, he added: "He has commanded that you worship none but Him" (أَمَرَ أَلَّا تَعْبُدُوا إِلَّا إِيَّاهُ).

"That is the right and straight religion (Deen), in which there is no deviation" (ذَلِكَ الدِّينُ الْقَيِّمُ).

This means that Monotheism in all its dimensions—in worship, in governance, in society, in culture, and in everything—is the straight and enduring Divine path.

"But what can be done, for most people do not know," and because of this lack of awareness, they wander in the byways of polytheism and submit to the rule of other than "Allah," and what agonies, imprisonments, and miseries afflict them as a result (وَلَكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ النَّاسِ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ).

After guiding and instructing his two prison companions and inviting them to the truth of Monotheism in its various dimensions, Yusuf proceeded to interpret their dreams. This was because they had come to him for this purpose from the beginning, and he had promised to interpret these dreams. However, he seized the opportunity to recount a clear and vivid chapter on Monotheism and the struggle against polytheism. Then, turning to the two prison companions, he said: "O my two companions of the prison! As for one of you, he will be poured out wine for his lord to drink" (يَا صَاحِبَيِ السِّجْنِ أَمَّا أَحَدُكُمَا فَيَسْقِي رَبَّهُ خَمْرًا).

"But as for the other, he will be crucified, and he will remain there so long that the birds of the air will eat from his head!" (وَأَمَّا الْآخَرُ فَيُصْلَبُ فَتَأْكُلُ الطَّيْرُ مِنْ رَأْسِهِ).

Although, based on the nature of the dreams they had seen, it was generally clear which of the two would be freed and which would be hanged, Yusuf did not wish to state this unpleasant news more explicitly than this. Therefore, he pursued the matter under the phrasing "one of you two."

Then, to emphasize his words, he added: "The matter about which you both inquired (sought a ruling) is decreed and final." This was an indication that this was not a simple dream interpretation, but rather stemmed from news of the unseen (Ghayb) which he had acquired through Divine teaching; therefore, there was no room for doubt or debate! (قُضِيَ الْأَمْرُ الَّذِي فِيهِ تَسْتَفْتِيَانِ). In many commentaries, it is mentioned regarding this sentence that as soon as the second person heard this unpleasant news, he attempted to deny his own words, saying, "I lied; I did not see such a dream, I was joking," thinking that if he denied his dream, this fate would change. Thus, Yusuf followed this by saying that what you sought a ruling on is unchangeable.

It is also possible that Yusuf was so certain in his interpretation of the dream that he stated this sentence as an emphasis.

At this moment, feeling that these two would soon be separated from him, and in order to find a window to freedom and to clear himself of the sin attributed to him, "he said to the one of the two whom he knew would be saved: 'Mention me to your lord (the King),'" so that he might investigate and my innocence be proven (وَقَالَ لِلَّذِي ظَنَّ أَنَّهُ نَاجٍ مِنْهُمَا اذْكُرْنِي عِنْدَ رَبِّكَ). But this "forgetful servant"—as is the way of shallow individuals who, when they attain a blessing, forget the benefactor—completely forgot Yusuf's matter. However, the Quran's expression is that "Satan made him forget to mention [Yusuf] to his lord" (فَأَنْسَاهُ الشَّيْطَانُ ذِكْرَ رَبِّهِ).

And thus, Yusuf was consigned to oblivion, "and he remained in prison for several years" (فَلَبِثَ فِي السِّجْنِ بِضْعَ سِنِينَ).

There is a discussion among commentators as to whether the pronoun in "فَأَنْسَاهُ الشَّيْطَانُ" (Satan made him forget) refers to the King's cupbearer or to Yusuf. Many have referred this pronoun back to Yusuf; thus, the meaning of the sentence would be that Satan made the remembrance of Allah slip from Yusuf's mind, and for this reason, he sought recourse in someone other than Him.

However, considering the preceding sentence where Yusuf advises him to "mention me to your lord," it appears that the pronoun refers to the cupbearer, and the word "Rabb" (lord/master) in both places would have the same meaning.

Furthermore, the sentence "وَادَّكَرَ بَعْدَ أُمَّةٍ" (and he remembered after a time), which we read a few verses later in this same story regarding the cupbearer, shows that it was he who was the forgetful one, not Yusuf.

[Tafsir Nemouneh, Vol. 9, p. 414]

In any case, whether the pronoun refers to Yusuf or the cupbearer, there is no doubt that Yusuf here sought recourse in someone other than Allah for his salvation.

Of course, such efforts to escape prison and other problems are not a significant issue for ordinary people and are considered a form of resorting to natural means. However, for exemplary individuals and those at the highest level of faith and Monotheism, it cannot be free of criticism. Perhaps this is why Allah did not overlook this "leaving of the better" (Tark al-Awla) by Yusuf, and because of it, his imprisonment continued for several years.

In a narration from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, we read that he said: "I wonder at my brother Yusuf, how he sought refuge and help from a creature and not from the Creator?" [1] In another Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read that after this incident, Jibra'il came to Yusuf and said: "Who made you the most beautiful of people?" He said: "My Lord!"

He said: "Who placed your love so deeply in your father's heart?" He said: "My Lord."

He said: "Who sent the caravan to you, to save you from the well?" He said: "My Lord."

He said: "Who diverted the stone (which had been thrown from the top of the well) away from you?" He said: "My Lord."

He said: "Who granted you deliverance from the well?" He said: "My Lord."

He said: "Who turned away the plot and guile of the women of Egypt from you?" He said: "My Lord!" At this point, Jibra'il said: "Your Lord says: What caused you to take your need to a creature and not bring it to Me? And for this reason, you must remain in prison for several years." [1]

Key Points

1- Prison: A Center for Guidance or a University of Corruption?

Prison has a very painful and tragic history in the world. Both the worst criminals and the best of human beings have been cast into prison. For this reason, prison has always been a center for the best lessons of construction or the worst of misguidance.

In prisons where wrongdoers gather together, a "higher academy of corruption" is effectively formed. In these prisons, they exchange destructive plans and share their experiences, and every criminal essentially teaches his specialized lesson to others. For this reason, after being released from prison, they continue their crimes better and more skillfully than before, and that too while maintaining unity and a new organization—unless those overseeing the prison monitor this issue and, through guidance and education, transform the prisoners (who are often energetic and talented individuals) into righteous, useful, and constructive elements.

As for prisons composed of the pure, the good, the innocent, and the fighters for truth and freedom, they are centers for ideological training, practical methods of struggle, and organization. Such prisons provide a good opportunity for the fighters of truth to coordinate and organize their efforts after release.

Yusuf, who had been victorious in the struggle against a lustful, deceitful, and bullying woman like the wife of the Aziz of Egypt, endeavored to transform the prison environment into an environment of guidance and a center for education, and even laid the foundation for his own freedom and that of others within those very programs.

This story teaches us the important lesson that guidance and education are not limited or confined to a specific center like a mosque or a school; rather, every opportunity must be used for this goal, even from within a prison under the chains of captivity.

It is also necessary to mention that there is discussion regarding the years of Yusuf's imprisonment, but the famous view is that Yusuf's total imprisonment was 7 years. However, some have said that before the incident of the prisoners' dreams, he was in prison for 5 years, and after that, it continued for another seven years—years full of suffering and hardship, yet fruitful and blessed in terms of guidance and construction. [1]

2- Where the Righteous Go to the Gallows

Interestingly, we read in this story that the one who saw in his dream that he was handing a cup of wine to the King was freed, while the one who saw in his dream that he carried a basket of bread on his head and the birds of the air were eating from it was hanged.

Does this not imply that in corrupt environments and tyrannical (Taghuti) regimes, those who serve the desires of the self-willed have freedom, while those who step on the path of serving society and helping and providing bread to the people have no right to life and must die? This is the fabric of a society ruled by a corrupt system, and this is the fate of good and bad people in such societies.

It is true that Yusuf made such a prediction relying on Divine revelation and the science of dream interpretation, but no interpreter can ignore such correlations in his interpretation.

In truth, service in these societies is a sin and treason, and sin is the very essence of merit!

3- The Greatest Lesson of Freedom!

We saw that the highest lesson Yusuf gave to the prisoners was the lesson of Monotheism and the worship of the One, the same lesson whose product is freedom and nobility.

He knew that "diverse lords" (أَرْبَابٌ مُتَفَرِّقُونَ), scattered goals, and different deities are the source of division and fragmentation in society. As long as division and fragmentation exist, tyrants and oppressors will dominate the people. Therefore, to cut their roots, he commanded the use of the sword of Monotheism so that they would not be forced to see freedom only in dreams, but rather witness it while awake.

After all, how many are the tyrants and oppressors who ride upon the backs of the people in any society, such that the people are unable to fight them? Is it not that they are a limited number of individuals, but by creating division and hypocrisy through "diverse lords" and breaking the organized power of society, they gain the possibility of ruling over the vast masses of people?

And the day that nations become acquainted with the power of Monotheism and the unity of the word, and all gather under the banner of "Allah, the One, the Irresistible," and realize their own immense power—that day is the day of the tyrants' destruction. This is a very important lesson for our today, for our tomorrow, and for all humans in the entirety of human society and throughout history.

Specifically, it is necessary to pay attention to this point that Yusuf says: Sovereignty belongs only to Allah (إِنِ الْحُكْمُ إِلَّا لِلَّهِ), and then emphasizes that worship, humility, and submission must also only be before His command (أَمَرَ أَلَّا تَعْبُدُوا إِلَّا إِيَّاهُ), and then stresses that the straight and enduring religion is nothing but this (ذَلِكَ الدِّينُ الْقَيِّمُ). But finally, he also says that despite all these attributes, unfortunately, most people are unaware of this reality (وَلَكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ النَّاسِ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ). Therefore, if people receive correct education, gain awareness, and the truth of Monotheism is revived within them, these problems of theirs will be solved.

4- Misuse of a Constructive Slogan

The slogan "إِنِ الْحُكْمُ إِلَّا لِلَّهِ" (Sovereignty belongs to none but Allah), which is a positive Quranic slogan and negates any form of rule except the rule of "Allah" and what leads to "Allah," has unfortunately been subject to strange misuses throughout history. Among these, as we know, were the Kharijites of Nahrawan, who were superficial, rigid, foolish, and very narrow-minded people. To negate arbitration (Tahkim) in the Battle of Siffin, they clung to this slogan and said that appointing an arbitrator to end the war or appointing a Caliph is a sin because Allah says "إِنِ الْحُكْمُ إِلَّا لِلَّهِ":

Governance and arbitration belong specifically to God!

They were oblivious to this obvious matter—or they feigned ignorance—that if arbitration is determined by leaders whose command of leadership has been issued by God, their ruling is also the ruling of God because it ultimately leads back to Him.

It is true that the arbitrators (judges) in the story of the Battle of Siffin were not appointed with the approval of the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, but if they had been, their ruling would have been the ruling of Ali عليه السلام, and the ruling of Ali عليه السلام would be the ruling of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, and the ruling of the Prophet would be the ruling of God.

In principle, does God directly govern and remind human society? Is it not that persons of the human species—authorized by God's command—must take the reins of this matter? But the Kharijites, without paying attention to this clear reality, criticized Ali عليه السلام for the principle of the story of arbitration and even—God forbid—considered it proof of his deviation from Islam. What self-centeredness, ignorance, and rigidity!

And thus, the most constructive programs, when they fall into the hands of ignorant and foolish individuals, turn into the worst destructive tools.

And today, a group who are in fact followers of the Kharijites and are no less than them in terms of ignorance and stubbornness, consider the above verse as proof for negating the following (Taqlid) of Mujtahids or negating their eligibility for governance, but the answer to all of these was given above.

Reliance on Other Than God

Monotheism is not summarized only in the fact that God is One and Unique; rather, it must be implemented in all affairs of human life. One of its most prominent signs is that a monotheistic person does not lean on other than God and does not seek refuge in other than Him.

We do not say that he ignores the world of causes and does not seek means and causes in life; rather, we say that he does not see the real influence in the cause, but rather sees the end of all strings of causes in the hand of the "Causer of Causes" (Musabbib al-Asbab). In other words, he does not grant independence to causes and considers all of them a reflection of the Pure Essence of the Lord.

It might be excusable for ordinary people to lack attention to this great reality, but even a needle's point of inattention to this principle for the friends (Awliya) of Truth warrants punishment, even if it is no more than a "leaving of the better." We saw how Yusuf, due to a moment of inattention to this vital issue, had his imprisonment extended for years so that he might become even more refined and tempered in the furnace of events, gain more readiness for the struggle against the Taghut and the Taghutis, and know that on this path, he must not rely on anything except the power of Allah and the oppressed people who walk in the way of Allah.

And this is a great lesson for all seekers of this path and true fighters: that they should never entertain the thought of coalition with the power of one Satan to crush another Satan, nor lean toward the East or the West, and walk in no path except the Straight Path (Sirat al-Mustaqim), which is the road of the Middle Nation (Ummah Wast).

[^1]: Majma' al-Bayan, under the verse. [^1]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 5, p. 235. [^1]: For further explanation regarding the years of Yusuf's imprisonment, refer to the commentaries of "Al-Manar," "Qurtubi," "Al-Mizan," and "Fakhr Razi."

Ayahs

وَقَالَ ٱلْمَلِكُ إِنِّىٓ أَرَىٰ سَبْعَ بَقَرَٰتٍ سِمَانٍ يَأْكُلُهُنَّ سَبْعٌ عِجَافٌ وَسَبْعَ سُنۢبُلَـٰتٍ خُضْرٍ وَأُخَرَ يَابِسَـٰتٍ ۖ يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلْمَلَأُ أَفْتُونِى فِى رُءْيَـٰىَ إِن كُنتُمْ لِلرُّءْيَا تَعْبُرُونَ (٤٣)
[One day] the king said, ‘I saw [in a dream] seven fat cows being devoured by seven lean ones, and seven green ears and [seven] others [that were] dry. O courtiers, give me your opinion about my dream, if you can interpret dreams.’ (43)
قَالُوٓا۟ أَضْغَـٰثُ أَحْلَـٰمٍ ۖ وَمَا نَحْنُ بِتَأْوِيلِ ٱلْأَحْلَـٰمِ بِعَـٰلِمِينَ (٤٤)
They said, ‘[These are] confused nightmares, and we do not know the interpretation of nightmares.’ (44)
وَقَالَ ٱلَّذِى نَجَا مِنْهُمَا وَٱدَّكَرَ بَعْدَ أُمَّةٍ أَنَا۠ أُنَبِّئُكُم بِتَأْوِيلِهِۦ فَأَرْسِلُونِ (٤٥)
Said the one of the two who had been delivered, remembering [Joseph] after a long time: ‘I will inform you of its interpretation; so let me go [to meet Joseph in the prison].’ (45)
يُوسُفُ أَيُّهَا ٱلصِّدِّيقُ أَفْتِنَا فِى سَبْعِ بَقَرَٰتٍ سِمَانٍ يَأْكُلُهُنَّ سَبْعٌ عِجَافٌ وَسَبْعِ سُنۢبُلَـٰتٍ خُضْرٍ وَأُخَرَ يَابِسَـٰتٍ لَّعَلِّىٓ أَرْجِعُ إِلَى ٱلنَّاسِ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَعْلَمُونَ (٤٦)
‘Joseph,’ [he said], ‘O truthful one, give us your opinion concerning seven fat cows who are eaten by seven lean ones, and seven green ears and [seven] others dry, that I may return to the people so that they may know [the truth of the matter].’ (46)
قَالَ تَزْرَعُونَ سَبْعَ سِنِينَ دَأَبًا فَمَا حَصَدتُّمْ فَذَرُوهُ فِى سُنۢبُلِهِۦٓ إِلَّا قَلِيلًا مِّمَّا تَأْكُلُونَ (٤٧)
He said, ‘You will sow for seven consecutive years. Then leave in the ear whatever [grain] you harvest, except a little that you eat. (47)
ثُمَّ يَأْتِى مِنۢ بَعْدِ ذَٰلِكَ سَبْعٌ شِدَادٌ يَأْكُلْنَ مَا قَدَّمْتُمْ لَهُنَّ إِلَّا قَلِيلًا مِّمَّا تُحْصِنُونَ (٤٨)
Then after that there will come seven hard years which will eat up whatever you have set aside for them—all except a little which you preserve [for seed]. (48)
ثُمَّ يَأْتِى مِنۢ بَعْدِ ذَٰلِكَ عَامٌ فِيهِ يُغَاثُ ٱلنَّاسُ وَفِيهِ يَعْصِرُونَ (٤٩)
Then after that there will come a year wherein the people will be granted relief and provided with rains therein. (49)

Commentary

The Story of the Egyptian King’s Dream

Yusuf (peace be upon him) remained in the constraints of prison for years as a forgotten man. His only occupation was self-purification, guiding and instructing the prisoners, visiting and nursing the sick, and offering heart-felt comfort and consolation to those in pain.

This continued until a seemingly small incident changed his destiny—and not only his destiny, but the destiny of the entire nation of Egypt and its surrounding regions. The King of Egypt, whose name is said to have been Walid ibn Rayyan (and for whom the Aziz of Egypt served as a minister), saw a seemingly disturbing dream. In the morning, he summoned the dream interpreters and his courtiers and said: "I saw in a dream seven lean cows attacking seven fat cows and eating them; and I also saw seven green ears of grain and seven shriveled ones that wrapped themselves around the green ones and destroyed them" (وَ قالَ الْمَلِكُ إِنِّي أَرى‌ سَبْعَ بَقَراتٍ سِمانٍ يَأْكُلُهُنَّ سَبْعٌ عِجافٌ وَ سَبْعَ سُنْبُلاتٍ خُضْرٍ وَ أُخَرَ يابِساتٍ‌).

He then turned to them and said: "O assembly of nobles! Give me your opinion regarding my dream, if you are able to interpret dreams" (يا أَيُّهَا الْمَلَأُ أَفْتُونِي فِي رُءْيايَ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ لِلرُّءْيا تَعْبُرُونَ‌).

However, the Sultan's attendants immediately stated: "These are but confused dreams, and we are not acquainted with the interpretation of such muddled visions!" (قالُوا أَضْغاثُ أَحْلامٍ وَ ما نَحْنُ بِتَأْوِيلِ الْأَحْلامِ بِعالِمِينَ‌).

"Adghath" is the plural of "dighth", meaning a bundle of firewood, dried grass, herbs, or something similar. "Ahlam" is the plural of "hulm", meaning a dream or vision. Therefore, "adghath ahlam" refers to confused and mixed-up dreams, as if they are composed of various bundles of different objects. The word "al-ahlam" in the phrase "ما نَحْنُ بِتَأْوِيلِ الْأَحْلامِ بِعالِمِينَ" (we are not learned in the interpretation of [such] dreams) uses the definite article to indicate that they were unable to interpret this specific type of confused dream. It is necessary to note that their expression of inability was truly because the actual meaning of this dream was not clear to them, and thus they categorized it as a "confused dream." They used to divide dreams into two types:

Meaningful dreams that were interpretable, and confused, meaningless dreams for which they had no interpretation, considering them the result of the activity of the imaginative faculty—unlike the first group of dreams, which they viewed as the result of the soul's contact with the unseen realms.

There is also the possibility that they foresaw distressing events in the future from this dream. As is common among the sycophants of kings and tyrants, they only mention matters to the king that would, so to speak, "expand the royal heart" and bring joy, while they refrain from mentioning anything that might upset his "blessed essence." This is indeed one of the causes of the downfall and misery of such despotic governments!

Here, the question arises: how did they dare to make such a statement to the Sultan of Egypt and accuse him of seeing confused dreams? Usually, such courtiers weave elaborate philosophies and provide expansive interpretations for even the smallest, meaningless movement of a king!

This might be because they found the King distressed and anxious after seeing this dream. He had every right to be anxious, for he had seen lean cows—weak creatures—overpowering and eating fat, powerful cows, and similarly with the dried ears of grain.

Did this not suggest that weak individuals might suddenly seize the government from his hands?

Therefore, to remove the King's distress, they labeled his dream as a "confused dream," essentially saying: "Do not worry, it is nothing important; such dreams cannot be evidence of anything!"

Some commentators have also suggested that by "adghath ahlam," they did not mean your dream has no interpretation, but rather that the dream is complex and composed of different parts. They meant: "We can only interpret straightforward and uniform dreams, not this kind." Thus, they did not deny that a skilled master might be found who could interpret it; they merely expressed their own inadequacy.

At this point, the King’s cupbearer, who had been released from prison years earlier, remembered the memory of the prison and Yusuf’s interpretation of dreams. He turned to the Sultan and the courtiers and said: "I can inform you of the interpretation of this dream; send me to the skilled master of this craft who is in a corner of the prison, so that I may bring you the first-hand, correct news" (وَ قالَ الَّذِي نَجا مِنْهُما وَ ادَّكَرَ بَعْدَ أُمَّةٍ أَنَا أُنَبِّئُكُمْ بِتَأْوِيلِهِ فَأَرْسِلُونِ‌).

Yes, in a corner of this prison lives an enlightened, faithful, and pure-hearted man whose heart is a mirror of future events. He is the one who can lift the veil from this secret and recount the interpretation of this dream.

The phrase "فَأَرْسِلُونِ" (so send me) might indicate that Yusuf was forbidden from having visitors in prison, and the cupbearer wanted permission from the King and his circle for this task.

This speech changed the atmosphere of the assembly, and all eyes were fixed on the cupbearer. Finally, he was given permission to go on this mission as soon as possible and report the result immediately.

The cupbearer came to the prison to see his old friend Yusuf—the same friend to whom he had shown much unfaithfulness. However, perhaps he knew that Yusuf’s nobility would prevent him from opening a door of grievance. He turned to Yusuf and said: "Yusuf, O man of truth! What do you say regarding this dream: that someone saw seven lean cows eating seven fat cows, and seven green ears of grain and seven shriveled ones (which wrapped around the first and destroyed them)?" (يُوسُفُ أَيُّهَا الصِّدِّيقُ أَفْتِنا فِي سَبْعِ بَقَراتٍ سِمانٍ يَأْكُلُهُنَّ سَبْعٌ عِجافٌ وَ سَبْعَ سُنْبُلاتٍ خُضْرٍ وَ أُخَرَ يابِساتٍ‌).

"Perhaps I may return to the people, so that they may become aware of the secrets of this dream" (لَعَلِّي أَرْجِعُ إِلَى النَّاسِ لَعَلّهمْ يَعْلَمُونَ‌).

The word "النَّاسِ" (the people) might indicate that the King’s dream, as a major event of the day, had been spread among the public by the flattering courtiers, and this anxiety had moved from the court into the masses.

In any case, Yusuf, without setting any conditions or asking for a reward, immediately interpreted the dream in the most excellent manner—an interpretation that was clear, devoid of any ambiguity, and accompanied by guidance and planning for the dark future they faced. He said: "You shall sow for seven consecutive years with diligence, for in these seven years there will be abundant rainfall. But whatever you reap, leave it in its ear and store it in the granaries, except for a small amount for your rationing and eating needs" (قالَ تَزْرَعُونَ سَبْعَ سِنِينَ دَأَباً فَما حَصَدْتُمْ فَذَرُوهُ فِي سُنْبُلِهِ إِلَّا قَلِيلًا مِمَّا تَأْكُلُونَ‌) [1].

"But know that after these seven years, seven dry years of low rainfall and hardship lie ahead, during which you must use only what you have stored from the previous years, otherwise you will perish" (ثُمَّ يَأْتِي مِنْ بَعْدِ ذلِكَ سَبْعٌ شِدادٌ يَأْكُلْنَ ما قَدَّمْتُمْ لَهُنَ‌). "But be careful; in those seven years of drought and famine, you must not consume the entire inventory of the granaries for food; rather, you must keep a small amount for the sowing of the following year, which will be a good year" (إِلَّا قَلِيلًا مِمَّا تُحْصِنُونَ‌).

[1]: Da’ab originally means the continuation of movement and also refers to a continuous habit. Therefore, the meaning of this word is that you should continue your work according to your constant habit of prioritizing agriculture in the land of Egypt, but be economical in consuming its produce. There is also the possibility that it means to pursue farming with the utmost diligence and effort, as da’ab and du’ub also mean effort and fatigue—meaning, work until you are exhausted.

If you pass these seven dry and difficult years with a calculated plan and map, no further danger will threaten you, because "after that, a year will come with abundant rain, in which the people will benefit from this heavenly blessing" (ثُمَّ يَأْتِي مِنْ بَعْدِ ذلِكَ عامٌ فِيهِ يُغاثُ النَّاسُ‌).

Not only will the cultivation of crops and food grains improve, but in addition, oilseeds and fruits that people press and use for various extracts will also be abundant (وَ فِيهِ يَعْصِرُونَ‌).

Key Points

1- A Calculated Interpretation

The interpretation Yusuf provided for this dream was remarkably calculated. In ancient lore, the cow was a symbol of a "year." Being fat indicated an abundance of blessings, and being lean indicated drought and hardship. The attack of the lean cows on the fat cows meant that during these seven years, the reserves of the previous years must be used.

The seven shriveled ears of grain wrapping around the seven green ones were another emphasis on these two periods of abundance and drought, with the added point that the harvested crop must be stored in its ear so it does not spoil quickly and can be kept for seven years.

The fact that the number of lean cows and shriveled ears was no more than seven showed that with the end of these seven difficult years, that situation would conclude, and naturally, a pleasant, rainy, and blessed year would follow. Therefore, they must also think of the seeds for that year and keep some of the granary reserves for it!

In reality, Yusuf was not a mere interpreter of dreams; he was a leader who, from a corner of the prison, was planning the future of a country. He presented them with a multi-point plan spanning at least fifteen years. As we shall see, this interpretation, combined with guidance and future planning, shook the despotic King and his courtiers. it resulted in the people of Egypt being saved from a deadly famine, Yusuf being released from prison, and the government being taken out of the hands of the autocrats!

2- Another Great Lesson from This Story

Once again, this story teaches us the great lesson that Allah's power is far beyond what we imagine. He is the One who can, through a simple dream seen by a tyrant of his time, rescue a great nation from a massive catastrophe and also liberate His special servant after years of suffering and calamity.

The Sultan had to see this dream, the cupbearer had to be present at that moment, he had to remember the memory of his own prison dream, and finally, significant events had to occur.

He is the One who creates great events through a small matter. Yes, it is in such a God that we must place our hearts.

3- The Multiple Dreams Mentioned in This Surah

The various dreams mentioned in this Surah—from Yusuf’s own dream to the dreams of the prisoners and the dream of the Pharaoh of Egypt—and the great importance the people of that era gave to dream interpretation, show that in that age, dream interpretation was considered one of the advanced sciences of the time. Perhaps for this reason, the Prophet of that era, Yusuf, was endowed with such knowledge at the highest level, which in fact served as a miracle for him.

Is it not the case that the miracle of every Prophet must be related to the most advanced sciences of the time? So that when the scholars of the age are unable to match it, certainty is gained that this knowledge has a divine source, not a human one.

Ayahs

وَقَالَ ٱلْمَلِكُ ٱئْتُونِى بِهِۦ ۖ فَلَمَّا جَآءَهُ ٱلرَّسُولُ قَالَ ٱرْجِعْ إِلَىٰ رَبِّكَ فَسْـَٔلْهُ مَا بَالُ ٱلنِّسْوَةِ ٱلَّـٰتِى قَطَّعْنَ أَيْدِيَهُنَّ ۚ إِنَّ رَبِّى بِكَيْدِهِنَّ عَلِيمٌ (٥٠)
The king said, ‘Bring him to me!’ When the messenger came to him, he said, ‘Go back to your master, and ask him about the affair of women who cut their hands. My Lord is indeed well aware of their stratagems.’ (50)
قَالَ مَا خَطْبُكُنَّ إِذْ رَٰوَدتُّنَّ يُوسُفَ عَن نَّفْسِهِۦ ۚ قُلْنَ حَـٰشَ لِلَّهِ مَا عَلِمْنَا عَلَيْهِ مِن سُوٓءٍ ۚ قَالَتِ ٱمْرَأَتُ ٱلْعَزِيزِ ٱلْـَٔـٰنَ حَصْحَصَ ٱلْحَقُّ أَنَا۠ رَٰوَدتُّهُۥ عَن نَّفْسِهِۦ وَإِنَّهُۥ لَمِنَ ٱلصَّـٰدِقِينَ (٥١)
The king said, ‘What was your business, women, when you solicited Joseph?’ They said, ‘Heaven be praised! We know of no evil in him.’ The prince’s wife said, ‘Now the truth has come to light! It was I who solicited him, and he is indeed telling the truth.’ (51)
ذَٰلِكَ لِيَعْلَمَ أَنِّى لَمْ أَخُنْهُ بِٱلْغَيْبِ وَأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يَهْدِى كَيْدَ ٱلْخَآئِنِينَ (٥٢)
[Joseph said], [‘I initiated] this [inquiry], that he may know that I did not betray him in his absence, and that Allah does not further the schemes of the treacherous.’ (52)
۞ وَمَآ أُبَرِّئُ نَفْسِىٓ ۚ إِنَّ ٱلنَّفْسَ لَأَمَّارَةٌۢ بِٱلسُّوٓءِ إِلَّا مَا رَحِمَ رَبِّىٓ ۚ إِنَّ رَبِّى غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ (٥٣)
‘Yet I do not absolve my [own carnal] soul, for the [carnal] soul indeed prompts [men] to evil, except inasmuch as my Lord has mercy. Indeed my Lord is all-forgiving, all-merciful.’ (53)

Commentary

The Exoneration of Yusuf from Every Accusation

The interpretation that Yusuf (peace be upon him) provided for the dream of the King of Egypt was, as we have said, so calculated and logical that it captivated the King and his courtiers. He saw that an unknown prisoner, without expecting any reward or having any demands, solved the problem of his dream's interpretation in the best possible way and even provided a calculated plan for the future.

He understood in general that this man was not a mere slave prisoner, but an extraordinary individual who had fallen into prison through a mysterious series of events. Therefore, he became eager to meet him, but not to the extent that he would set aside the pride and arrogance of his sovereignty to rush to visit Yusuf himself; rather, "the King said: 'Bring him to me'" (وَ قالَ الْمَلِكُ ائْتُونِي بِهِ).

"But when his messenger came to Yusuf," instead of losing his composure—considering that after years of being in a dark dungeon, the breeze of freedom was now blowing—he gave a negative response to the King's messenger and said: "I will not leave the prison until you return to your master and ask him: what was the case of those women in the palace of the Aziz of Egypt (your minister) who cut their hands?" (فَلَمَّا جاءَهُ الرَّسُولُ قالَ ارْجِعْ إِلى‌ رَبِّكَ فَسْئَلْهُ ما بالُ النِّسْوَةِ اللَّاتِي قَطَّعْنَ أَيْدِيَهُنَ‌).

He did not want to be released from prison simply and accept the stigma of a royal pardon. He did not want to live after his release as a criminal, or at least as an accused person who had been granted amnesty by the King. He wanted the reason for his imprisonment to be investigated first, and for his innocence and chastity to be fully proven, so that he could be released with his head held high after being exonerated. Furthermore, he wanted to prove the corruption within the Egyptian government's organization—what was happening in the court of his minister?

Indeed, he valued his honor and reputation more than his freedom, and this is the path of the free-spirited.

Interestingly, Yusuf showed such nobility in this statement that he was not even willing to mention the name of the wife of the Aziz of Egypt, who was the primary cause of the accusation and his imprisonment. He only referred generally to the group of Egyptian women who were involved in this incident.

Then he added: "If the masses of the Egyptian people and even the royal apparatus do not know how the plan for my imprisonment was devised and by whom, my Lord is well aware of their plot and scheme" (إِنَّ رَبِّي بِكَيْدِهِنَّ عَلِيمٌ‌).

The special messenger returned to the King and conveyed Yusuf's proposal. This proposal, which was accompanied by magnanimity and high ambition, further impressed the King with Yusuf's greatness and stature. Therefore, he immediately sent for the women who had participated in this incident and summoned them. He turned to them and "said: 'Tell me, what was your business when you sought to seduce Yusuf?'" (قالَ ما خَطْبُكُنَّ إِذْ راوَدْتُنَّ يُوسُفَ عَنْ نَفْسِهِ‌). "Speak the truth, reveal the reality; did you find any fault, transgression, or sin in him?!"

At this moment, their dormant consciences were suddenly awakened by this question, and they all unanimously testified to Yusuf's purity and said: "Allah forbid! We know of no evil in him" (قُلْنَ حاشَ لِلَّهِ ما عَلِمْنا عَلَيْهِ مِنْ سُوءٍ).

The wife of the Aziz, who was present and listening carefully to the words of the Sultan and the women of Egypt, did not find the power to remain silent even though no one had questioned her.

She felt that the time had come to compensate for years of conscientious shame with her decisive testimony to Yusuf's purity and her own guilt—especially since she realized Yusuf's unparalleled nobility from the message he had sent to the King, in which he had not mentioned a single word about her and had only spoken of the Egyptian women in a general manner.

Suddenly, as if an explosion occurred within her, "she cried out: 'Now the truth has become manifest; it was I who sought to seduce him, and he is indeed of the truthful'" and if I said anything about him, it was a lie, a lie! (قالَتِ امْرَأَةُ الْعَزِيزِ الْآنَ حَصْحَصَ الْحَقُّ أَنَا راوَدْتُهُ عَنْ نَفْسِهِ وَ إِنَّهُ لَمِنَ الصَّادِقِينَ‌).

The wife of the Aziz continued her speech, saying: "I made this explicit confession so that Yusuf may know that I did not betray him in his absence" (ذلِكَ لِيَعْلَمَ أَنِّي لَمْ أَخُنْهُ بِالْغَيْبِ‌). For after this time has passed and through the experiences I have had, I have realized that "Allah does not guide the snare of the betrayers" (وَ أَنَّ اللَّهَ لا يَهْدِي كَيْدَ الْخائِنِينَ‌).

In reality (assuming the above sentence is the speech of the wife of the Aziz, as the outward expression implies), she provides two reasons for her explicit confession of Yusuf's purity and her own guilt. First: her conscience—and possibly the remnants of her affection for Yusuf!—did not allow her to conceal the truth any longer and betray this chaste young man in his absence. Second: with the passage of time and seeing the lessons of experience, this truth became clear to her that Allah is the supporter of the pure and the good, and He never supports the betrayers. For this reason, the veils of the dreamlike life of the court were gradually removed from before her eyes, and she touched the reality of life. Especially with the failure in love, which dealt a blow to her pride and legendary persona, her realistic eyes were opened wider, and thus it is not surprising that she would make such an explicit confession.

She continued: "And I do not acquit my own self, for I know that the rebellious self (nafs) commands us to evil (وَ ما أُبَرِّئُ نَفْسِي إِنَّ النَّفْسَ لَأَمَّارَةٌ بِالسُّوءِ). "Except that which my Lord has mercy upon," and through His protection and help, we remain immune (إِلَّا ما رَحِمَ رَبِّي‌).

"And in any case, I hope for His pardon and forgiveness for this sin, for my Lord is Forgiving, Merciful" (إِنَّ رَبِّي غَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ‌).

A group of commentators have considered these last two verses to be the words of Yusuf, stating that these two verses are actually the continuation of the message Yusuf sent to the Sultan via the messenger, and its meaning is as follows:

"If I say to investigate the Egyptian women, it is so that the King (or the Aziz of Egypt, his minister) may know that I did not betray him regarding his wife in his absence, and Allah does not guide the snare of the betrayers. At the same time, I do not acquit myself, for the rebellious self commands man to evil, except that which God has mercy upon, for my Lord is Forgiving, Merciful."

Apparently, the motivation for this interpretation—which goes against the outward meaning—is that they did not want to accept this level of knowledge and gnosis for the wife of the Aziz, that she would speak in such a sincere tone indicative of awakening and realization.

However, it is not at all unlikely that when a person's foot strikes a stone in life, a state of awakening coupled with a sense of guilt and shame arises within them. Especially since it has often been seen that failure in metaphorical (worldly) love opens a way for a person toward true love (love for the Lord)! And in the terms of modern psychoanalysis, those intense repressed desires are "sublimated" and, without disappearing, manifest in a higher form.

Some narrations mentioned regarding the life of the wife of the Aziz in her later years also testify to this realization and awakening.

Furthermore, linking these two verses to Yusuf is so remote and contrary to the outward meaning that it does not align with any literary standards because:

Firstly: "Dhalika" (that), mentioned at the beginning of the verse, is actually stated as a reason—a reason for the preceding speech, which is nothing other than the speech of the wife of the Aziz. Attaching this reason to Yusuf's speech, which appeared in the verses before it with a gap, is very strange.

Secondly: If these two verses were the statement of Yusuf, there would be a kind of contradiction and paradox within them. On one hand, Yusuf says, "I committed no betrayal against the Aziz," and on the other hand, he says, "I do not acquit myself, for the rebellious self commands to evil." Such words are spoken by someone from whom a slip, however small, has occurred, while we know that Yusuf had no slip whatsoever.

Thirdly: If the intention was for the Aziz to know he was innocent, he had already realized this fact from the beginning (after the testimony of that witness) and thus told his wife to seek forgiveness for her sin. And if the intention was to say he had not betrayed the King, this matter had no relation to the King, and resorting to this excuse—that betraying the minister's wife is betrayal of the tyrannical King—seems like a weak and hollow excuse. Especially since courtiers are usually not bound by such matters.

In short, the connection and link between the verses show that all of these are the words of the wife of the Aziz, who had found a brief moment of realization and awakening and confessed to these truths.

Key Points

1- The Enemy's Confession of Their Own Guilt and Yusuf's Innocence

In this part of Yusuf's story, we saw that finally his fierce enemy confessed to his purity and admitted her own guilt and his innocence. This is the end result of piety, chastity, and refraining from sin, and this is the meaning of the verse: "And whoever fears Allah, He will make for him a way out and will provide for him from where he does not expect" (وَمَنْ يَتَّقِ اللَّهَ يَجْعَلْ لَهُ مَخْرَجاً وَ يَرْزُقْهُ مِنْ حَيْثُ لا يَحْتَسِبُ‌).

Be pure and remain steadfast in the path of purity; Allah will not allow the impure to destroy your reputation!

2- Failures That Cause Awakening

Failures are not always failures; rather, in many cases, they are failures in appearance but are considered a kind of spiritual victory in reality. These are the very failures that cause human awakening, tear the veils of pride and negligence, and are considered a turning point in a person's life.

The wife of the Aziz (whose name was "Zulaikha" or "Ra'eel"), although she suffered the worst of failures in her endeavor, this failure in the path of sin caused her realization. Her dormant conscience was awakened, she regretted her unseemly behavior, and she turned toward God's threshold. The story narrated in the Hadiths about her meeting with Yusuf after he became the Aziz of Egypt also testifies to this claim, for she turned to him and said: "Praise be to Allah who made slaves into kings due to their obedience to Him, and made kings into slaves due to their disobedience to Him" (الحمد لله الذي جعل العبيد ملوكا بطاعته و جعل الملوك عبيدا بمعصيته). At the end of this same Hadith, we read that Yusuf eventually married her [1]. Fortunate are those who create victory out of failures and success out of setbacks, and find the correct paths of life from their mistakes, finding their happiness amidst their misfortunes.

Of course, the reaction of all individuals to failure is not like this. Those who are weak and shallow are overcome by despair and hopelessness upon failure, and sometimes go as far as suicide, which is a complete failure. But those who have substance try to make it a ladder for their progress and build a bridge of victory from it!

3- Preserving Honor is Superior to Apparent Freedom

We saw that Yusuf not only went to prison to preserve his chastity, but even after his freedom was announced, he was not willing to leave the prison until the King's messenger returned and sufficient investigations were conducted regarding him with the Egyptian women, and his innocence was proven. He did not want to be included in a royal pardon as a tainted criminal lacking reputation, which itself is a great stigma.

And this is a lesson for all humans in the past, present, and future.

4- The Rebellious Self (Nafs)

Scholars of ethics consider three stages for the self (human feelings, instincts, and emotions), which are mentioned in the Holy Quran.

First is the "Nafs al-Ammarah" (the commanding self), the rebellious self that commands man to sin and drags him in every direction; hence it is called "Ammarah" (constantly commanding). At this stage, intellect and faith have not yet gained the power to rein in the rebellious self and tame it; rather, in many cases, they surrender to it, or if they wish to grapple with it, the rebellious self knocks them to the ground and defeats them.

This stage is the one referred to in the above verse in the speech of the wife of the Aziz, and all human misfortunes stem from it.

The second stage is the "Nafs al-Lawwamah" (the self-reproaching self), to which man ascends after education, training, and struggle. At this stage, due to the rebellion of instincts, he may occasionally commit wrongs, but he immediately regrets it and begins to blame and reproach himself, deciding to compensate for the sin and washing his heart and soul with the water of repentance. In other words, in the struggle between intellect and the self, sometimes the intellect wins and sometimes the self, but in any case, the heavy side of the scale belongs to intellect and faith.

Of course, to reach this stage, the "Greater Jihad" (Jihad al-Akbar) is necessary, along with sufficient practice, training in the school of a master, and seeking inspiration from the Word of God and the tradition of the leaders.

This stage is the one by which the Holy Quran swears in Surah Al-Qiyamah, an oath that is a sign of its greatness: "I swear by the Day of Resurrection, and I swear by the self-reproaching self!" (لا أُقْسِمُ بِيَوْمِ الْقِيامَةِ وَ لا أُقْسِمُ بِالنَّفْسِ اللَّوَّامَةِ).

The third stage is the "Nafs al-Mutma'innah" (the soul at peace). This is the stage where, after complete purification, refinement, and training, man reaches a level where the rebellious instincts become tame before him and surrender. They do not see in themselves the ability to battle with intellect and faith because intellect and faith have become so powerful that the carnal instincts have little power against them.

This is the very stage of tranquility and serenity—the tranquility that governs great oceans, oceans that do not show even a ripple on their surface even in the face of the harshest storms.

This is the station of the Prophets, the Saints (Awliya), and their true followers—those who learned the lesson of faith and piety in the school of men of God, spent years refining the self, and brought the Greater Jihad to its final stage.

This is what the Quran refers to in Surah Al-Fajr, where it says: "O soul at peace! Return to your Lord, well-pleased and well-pleasing [to Him]. So enter among My [righteous] servants, and enter My Paradise!" (يا أَيَّتُهَا النَّفْسُ الْمُطْمَئِنَّةُ ارْجِعِي إِلى‌ رَبّكِ راضِيَةً مَرْضِيَّةً فَادْخُلِي فِي عِبادِي وَ ادْخُلِي جَنَّتِي‌).

O Lord! Help us to elevate the "Ammarah" self to "Lawwamah" and from there to the stage of the "Mutma'innah" self in the light of the luminous verses of Your Quran. May we find a soul so peaceful and tranquil that the storm of events does not make it waver or anxious. May we be indifferent toward strong and powerful enemies and the glitz and glamour of the world, and be patient and steadfast in hardships.

O God! Now that more than a year and a half has passed since our Islamic Revolution, signs of disagreement have unfortunately appeared in the ranks of the fighters—signs that have worried all those who love Islam, those enamored with the Revolution, and the guardians of the blood of the martyrs!

O Allah! Grant us all an intellect that triumphs over rebellious whims, and if we are in error, place a bright lamp of success and guidance before our path.

O Allah! We have not traversed this path to this point on our own feet; rather, in every stage, You have been our leader and guide. Do not withhold Your grace from us! And if the ingratitude for all these blessings has made us deserving of Your punishment, awaken us before we fall into the trap of retribution! (Amen, O Lord of the Worlds).

"End of Volume Nine of Tafsir Nemouneh"

[1] Safinat al-Bihar, Vol. 1, p. 554.

Ayahs

وَقَالَ ٱلْمَلِكُ ٱئْتُونِى بِهِۦٓ أَسْتَخْلِصْهُ لِنَفْسِى ۖ فَلَمَّا كَلَّمَهُۥ قَالَ إِنَّكَ ٱلْيَوْمَ لَدَيْنَا مَكِينٌ أَمِينٌ (٥٤)
The king said, ‘Bring him to me, I will make him my favourite.’ Then, when he had spoken with him, he said, ‘Indeed today [onwards] you will be honoured and trustworthy with us.’ (54)
قَالَ ٱجْعَلْنِى عَلَىٰ خَزَآئِنِ ٱلْأَرْضِ ۖ إِنِّى حَفِيظٌ عَلِيمٌ (٥٥)
He said, ‘Put me in charge of the country’s granaries. I am indeed fastidious [and]well-informed.’ (55)
وَكَذَٰلِكَ مَكَّنَّا لِيُوسُفَ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ يَتَبَوَّأُ مِنْهَا حَيْثُ يَشَآءُ ۚ نُصِيبُ بِرَحْمَتِنَا مَن نَّشَآءُ ۖ وَلَا نُضِيعُ أَجْرَ ٱلْمُحْسِنِينَ (٥٦)
That is how We established Joseph in the land that he may settle in it wherever he wished. We confer Our mercy on whomever We wish, and We do not waste the reward of the virtuous. (56)
وَلَأَجْرُ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ خَيْرٌ لِّلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَكَانُوا۟ يَتَّقُونَ (٥٧)
And the reward of the Hereafter is surely better for those who have faith and are Godwary. (57)

Commentary

Yusuf Becomes the Treasurer of Egypt

In the account of the eventful life of Yusuf, this great Prophet of Allah, we have reached the point where his chastity was finally proven to all, and even his enemies bore witness to his purity. It was established that his only "sin," for which he had been cast into prison, was nothing but virtue, piety, and God-wariness.

Furthermore, it became clear that this innocent prisoner was a fountain of knowledge, awareness, and insight, possessing management talent of the highest order; for, while interpreting the dream of the "Malik" (the King of Egypt), he also showed them the way to escape the complex economic problems of the future.

Following this event, the Quran states:

"The King said: 'Bring him to me, that I may attach him to my person as a special advisor and representative,'" so that I may seek help from his knowledge and management to solve my problems (وَ قالَ الْمَلِكُ ائْتُونِي بِهِ أَسْتَخْلِصْهُ لِنَفْسِي).

The special representative of the King, carrying his warm message, entered the prison and rushed to meet Yusuf. He conveyed the King's greetings to Yusuf and expressed that the King had developed a deep affection for him. He added that the request Yusuf had made—regarding the investigation into the Egyptian women—had been fulfilled, and they had all explicitly testified to his purity and innocence.

Now there was no more room for delay: "Arise, let us go to him."

Yusuf came to the King and sat in conversation with him. "When the King spoke with him and heard Yusuf’s profound and substantial words, which indicated extraordinary knowledge, intelligence, and sagacity, he became more enamored and captivated by him than ever before. He said: 'Verily, today you are in our presence established in high rank and authority, and you shall be trusted and relied upon by us'" (فَلَمَّا كَلَّمَهُ قالَ إِنَّكَ الْيَوْمَ لَدَيْنا مَكِينٌ أَمِينٌ).

"Today, you must be the source of important affairs in this country and strive to reform its matters. For, according to the interpretation you gave of my dream, a severe economic crisis lies ahead for this land, and I believe the only person who can overcome this crisis is you." Yusuf proposed that he be the treasurer of Egypt and "said:

'Set me over the storehouses of the land, for I am a skilled guardian and well-versed in this matter'" (قالَ اجْعَلْنِي عَلى‌ خَزائِنِ الْأَرْضِ إِنِّي حَفِيظٌ عَلِيمٌ).

Yusuf knew that a major root of the disorders in that society—filled with oppression and tyranny—lay in its economic issues. Now that they had come to him out of necessity, what better way than to take hold of the economic pulse of Egypt and rush to the aid of the oppressed? He sought to reduce discrimination as much as his power allowed, take the rights of the oppressed from the oppressors, and bring order to the chaotic state of that vast country.

Specifically, he aimed to oversee agricultural matters, which were of primary importance in that land. Given that years of abundance followed by years of drought were ahead, he would encourage the people toward greater agriculture and production, and practice economy in the consumption of agricultural products to the point of rationing, storing them for the years of famine. Therefore, he saw no better way than to propose his supervision over the treasuries of Egypt.

Some have said that the King, who found himself in a severe predicament that year and was waiting to save himself somehow, handed over the reins of all affairs to Yusuf and withdrew himself.

However, others have said that he appointed him to the position of Prime Minister in place of the "Aziz of Egypt." It is also possible that, according to the literal meaning of the verse above, he became only the treasurer of Egypt.

But verses 100 and 101 of this same Surah—the commentary of which will come, Allah willing—provide evidence that he eventually sat in the place of the King and became the ruler of all Egypt's affairs. Although verse 88, which says the brothers said to him, "O Aziz," indicates that he took the place of the Aziz of Egypt, there is no obstacle to him having climbed these ranks gradually: first to the position of treasurer, then Prime Minister, and finally taking the place of the King.

In any case, Allah says here: "And thus We established Yusuf in the land, to settle therein wherever he willed" (وَ كَذلِكَ مَكَّنَّا لِيُوسُفَ فِي الْأَرْضِ يَتَبَوَّأُ مِنْها حَيْثُ يَشاءُ).

Indeed, "We bestow Our mercy and Our material and spiritual blessings upon whom We will and whom We deem worthy" (نُصِيبُ بِرَحْمَتِنا مَنْ نَشاءُ).

"And We never let the reward of the doers of good be lost." Even if it takes a long time, We will eventually give them what they deserve, for in Our presence, no good deed is ever forgotten (وَ لا نُضِيعُ أَجْرَ الْمُحْسِنِينَ).

But the important point is that We will not be content with only the reward of this world, "and the reward that will reach them in the Hereafter is better and more fitting for those who believed and were God-wary" (وَ لَأَجْرُ الْآخِرَةِ خَيْرٌ لِلَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَ كانُوا يَتَّقُونَ).

Key Points

1- How did Yusuf accept the invitation of the Taghut (tyrant) of the time?

The first thing that draws attention regarding the above verses is:

How did Yusuf, this great Prophet, agree to accept the treasurership or prime ministry of one of the tyrants of the time and cooperate with him?

The answer to this question is actually hidden within the verses themselves. He undertook this office as a "Hafiz" and "Alim" (trustworthy and knowledgeable) person, to protect the public treasury—which belonged to the people—for their benefit and to utilize it in their interests, especially to deliver the rights of the oppressed, which are trampled upon in most societies.

Furthermore, through the science of interpretation—as we mentioned—he was aware that a severe economic crisis was ahead for the Egyptian nation, which, without precise planning and close supervision, could lead to the loss of many lives. Therefore, saving a nation and preserving the lives of innocent humans necessitated that Yusuf use the opportunity he had gained for the benefit of all people, especially the deprived; for in an economic crisis and famine, their lives are endangered first, and they are the first victims of such crises.

In "Fiqh" (jurisprudence), in the discussion of accepting authority on behalf of an oppressor, this topic is covered extensively. It is stated that accepting a post or position from an oppressor is not always forbidden; rather, it sometimes becomes recommended or even obligatory. This occurs when the benefits of accepting it and its religious advantages outweigh the harms resulting from strengthening the regime.

In numerous narrations, we read that the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt علیهم السلام gave such permission to some of their close friends (such as Ali ibn Yaqtin, who was a companion of Imam al-Kadhim علیه السلام and accepted the ministry of the Pharaoh of his time, Harun al-Rashid, with the Imam's permission).

In any case, accepting or rejecting such posts follows the law of "the more important and the important" (al-aham wa al-muhim). Its religious and social benefits and harms must be weighed.
