Often, the person who accepts such a position eventually succeeds in stripping the oppressor of power (as happened in the life of Yusuf according to some narrations) and sometimes becomes a source for subsequent revolutions and uprisings, as he prepares the ground for revolution from within the system (perhaps the "Believer of the Family of Pharaoh" was of this type).

And at the very least, such individuals serve as a stronghold and refuge for the oppressed and deprived, reducing the pressure of the regime upon them. Each of these matters alone can justify accepting such posts.

The famous narration from Imam al-Sadiq علیه السلام regarding such people states: "The expiation for working for the Sultan is fulfilling the needs of the brothers" (كفارة عمل السلطان قضاء حوائج الاخوان). [^1]

However, this subject is one where the boundary between the permissible (halal) and the forbidden (haram) is very narrow. Sometimes, due to a small negligence, a person falls into the trap of useless cooperation with an oppressor and commits one of the greatest sins, while imagining himself occupied with worship and service to the people.

And sometimes, opportunistic individuals use the life of "Yusuf" or "Ali ibn Yaqtin" as an excuse for their own improper actions, while there is no similarity between their work and the work of Yusuf or Ali ibn Yaqtin. [^2]

[^1]: Wasa'il al-Shi'a, Vol. 12, p. 139 (The same meaning is narrated from Imam al-Kadhim علیه السلام regarding Ali ibn Yaqtin in Safinat al-Bihar, Vol. 2, p. 252).

[^2]: In several narrations from Imam Ali ibn Musa al-Rida علیه السلام, we read: That some people who were not familiar with Islamic criteria would sometimes criticize the Imam, asking why, despite all his asceticism and indifference to the world, he had accepted the position of heir-apparent to Ma'mun. The Imam replied: "Is a Prophet superior or the successor of a Prophet?" They replied: "A Prophet is superior." He asked: "Which is superior, a Muslim or a polytheist?" They said: "A Muslim." He said: "The Aziz of Egypt was a polytheist and Yusuf was a Prophet, while Ma'mun is (apparently) a Muslim and I am the successor of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله. Yusuf asked the Aziz of Egypt to place him over the treasuries of Egypt and said, 'I am a guardian and knowledgeable,' whereas I was forced to accept this position." (Wasa'il al-Shi'a, Vol. 12, p. 146).

Here another question arises: How did the tyrannical Sultan of Egypt submit to such a thing, knowing that Yusuf would not walk the path of his self-indulgence, oppression, exploitation, and colonialism, but rather would hinder his injustices?

The answer to this question is not very difficult when considering one point: sometimes social and economic crises are such that they shake the foundations of the rule of autocrats to the core, to the point where they see everything they own at risk. In such cases, to save themselves from destruction, they are even willing to welcome a just, people-oriented government to rescue themselves.

2- The Importance of Economic Issues and Management

Although we never agree with one-dimensional schools of thought that summarize everything in the economic dimension and fail to recognize man and the dimensions of his existence, the special importance of economic issues in the destiny of societies can never be overlooked. The above verses also point to this truth, for Yusuf, out of all positions, put his finger on the treasurership, because he knew that if he brought order to it, a major part of the disorders of the ancient country of Egypt would be resolved, and through economic justice, he could control other organizations.

In Islamic narrations, extraordinary importance is given to this subject. For instance, in a famous Hadith from Ali علیه السلام, economic issues are placed as one of the two main pillars of the material and spiritual life of the people (the sustenance of religion and the world), while the other pillar is considered to be science, knowledge, and awareness.

Although Muslims have so far ignored the importance that Islam has given to this part of individual and social life—and for this reason have fallen behind their enemies in this area—the increasing awakening and awareness seen in the layers of Islamic society gives hope that in the future, they will pursue economic work and activities as a great Islamic act of worship and, with a correct and calculated system, compensate for their backwardness compared to the ruthless enemies of Islam in this regard.

Furthermore, Yusuf's expression "I am a skilled guardian and well-versed" (إِنِّي حَفِيظٌ عَلِيمٌ) is evidence of the importance of management alongside trustworthiness. It shows that purity and trustworthiness alone are not sufficient for accepting a sensitive social post; rather, awareness, expertise, and management are also necessary, as he placed "Alim" (knowledgeable) alongside "Hafiz" (guardian).

We have often seen that the dangers arising from a lack of information and management are no less than the dangers arising from betrayal; in fact, they are sometimes greater and more extensive! With these clear Islamic teachings, we do not know why some Muslims give no importance to the issue of management and awareness, and the maximum extent of their thinking in the conditions for assigning posts is that same issue of trustworthiness and purity—even though the conduct of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and Ali علیه السلام during their rule shows that they gave as much importance to awareness and management as they did to trustworthiness and integrity.

3- Supervision of Consumption

In economic matters, it is not only the subject of "increased production" that is relevant; sometimes "control of consumption" is even more important. For this reason, during his administration, Yusuf tried to strictly control consumption during those seven years of abundance so that he could store a significant portion of agricultural production for the difficult years ahead.

In truth, these two cannot be separated. Increased production is useful when there is more correct control over consumption, and control of consumption will be more useful when accompanied by increased production.

Yusuf’s economic policy in Egypt showed that an authentic and dynamic economy cannot always look only at the present; rather, it must encompass the "future" and even subsequent generations. It is the height of selfishness for us to think only of our interests today and, for example, plunder all existing resources of the earth without any thought for those who come after us and the conditions in which they will live. Are our brothers only those who live with us today? Are those who come in the future not our brothers?

Interestingly, it is understood from some narrations that Yusuf used the years of famine to end class exploitation and the gap between the strata of the Egyptian people. In this way, during the years of abundance, he bought food supplies from the people and stored them in large warehouses prepared for this purpose. When these years ended and the years of famine arrived, in the first year he sold the food supplies for dirhams and dinars, thereby collecting a major part of the money. In the second year, in exchange for ornaments and jewels (except for those who were unable); in the third year, in exchange for livestock; in the fourth year, in exchange for male and female slaves; in the fifth year, in exchange for houses; in the sixth year, in exchange for farms and water; and in the seventh year, in exchange for the Egyptian people themselves. Then he returned all of them (in a just manner) to them, saying his goal was to rescue them from calamity and disorder. [^1]

[^1]: The above Hadith, mentioned in brief and paraphrased, is from Imam Ali ibn Musa al-Rida علیه السلام; refer to Tafsir Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 5, p. 244.

4- Praising Oneself or Introducing Oneself

Undoubtedly, praising oneself is an unpleasant act; however, this is not a universal rule. Sometimes conditions necessitate that a person introduces himself to society so that people may know him and utilize the capital of his existence, rather than him remaining like a hidden and neglected treasure.

In the above verses, we read that when Yusuf proposed the post of treasurer of Egypt, he praised himself with the phrase "Hafiz Alim" (guardian and knowledgeable), because it was necessary for the Sultan of Egypt and the people to know that he possessed the qualities essential for supervising this work.

Therefore, in Tafsir al-Ayyashi, we read from Imam al-Sadiq علیه السلام that in response to the question of whether it is permissible for a person to self-praise and laud himself, he said:

"Yes, when he is forced to do so. Have you not heard the words of Yusuf: 'Set me over the storehouses of the land, for I am a skilled guardian and well-versed,' and the words of the righteous servant (Hud): 'And I am for you a faithful adviser'?" [^1]

From this, it becomes clear that when Ali علیه السلام praises himself in the "Shaqshaqiyya" sermon and some other sermons of Nahj al-Balagha—describing himself as the axis of the millstone of the Caliphate, such that the high-flying bird of thought cannot reach the height of his station, and the flood of sciences and knowledge flows down from the mountain of his existence—all such descriptions are so that the unaware and uninformed people may realize his station and utilize the treasure of his existence for the improvement of society.

[^1]: Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 2, p. 433.

5- Spiritual Rewards are Superior

Although many righteous people receive their material reward in this very world—just as Yusuf received the result of his chastity, patience, piety, and God-wariness in this world, for had he been tainted, he would never have reached such a position—this does not mean that everyone should have such an expectation. If they do not reach material rewards, they should not think they have been wronged, for the primary reward is the one awaiting man in his future life.

Perhaps to remove this mistake and dispel this illusion, the Quran, after mentioning Yusuf's worldly reward, adds: "And the reward of the Hereafter is better for those who believe and are God-wary" (وَ لَأَجْرُ الْآخِرَةِ خَيْرٌ لِلَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَ كانُوا يَتَّقُونَ).

6- Support for Prisoners

Although prison has not always been a place for the righteous—sometimes the innocent and sometimes the guilty have been housed there—in any case, human principles necessitate that human standards be observed toward prisoners, even if they are guilty.

While the modern world may consider itself the innovator of the issue of prisoner support, in the rich history of Islam, we remember the recommendations and orders of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله regarding captives and prisoners from the first days he ruled. We have all heard the recommendation of Ali علیه السلام regarding that criminal prisoner (namely, Abd al-Rahman ibn Muljam al-Muradi, his assassin), where he ordered that he be treated with leniency and even sent him some of his own food, which was milk. Regarding his execution, he said: "Do not strike him more than one blow, for he struck no more than one blow!"

Yusuf, too, while in prison, was considered a kind companion, a compassionate nurse, a sincere friend, and a well-wishing advisor to the prisoners. When he was about to leave the prison, he first drew the world's attention to the condition of prisoners and their support by ordering that it be written over the prison gate:

"These are the graves of the living, the house of sorrows, the testing ground for friends, and the place of reproach for enemies!" [^1]

And with this prayer, he showed his affection for them: "O Allah! Turn the hearts of Your virtuous servants toward them, and do not hide the news from them." [^2] Interestingly, in that same narration, we read:

"For this reason, the people of the prison are the most knowledgeable of people regarding the news in every city"!!

We ourselves experienced this during our time in prison: except in exceptional cases, news reached the prisoners extensively through very mysterious ways that the prison guards never discovered. Sometimes those who had just entered the prison heard news inside that they were unaware of outside. If we were to describe examples of this, we would stray from our objective.

[^1] and [^2]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 2, p. 432.

Ayahs

وَجَآءَ إِخْوَةُ يُوسُفَ فَدَخَلُوا۟ عَلَيْهِ فَعَرَفَهُمْ وَهُمْ لَهُۥ مُنكِرُونَ (٥٨)
[After some years] the brothers of Joseph came and entered his presence. He recognized them, but they did not recognize him. (58)
وَلَمَّا جَهَّزَهُم بِجَهَازِهِمْ قَالَ ٱئْتُونِى بِأَخٍ لَّكُم مِّنْ أَبِيكُمْ ۚ أَلَا تَرَوْنَ أَنِّىٓ أُوفِى ٱلْكَيْلَ وَأَنَا۠ خَيْرُ ٱلْمُنزِلِينَ (٥٩)
When he had furnished them with their provision, he said, ‘Bring me a brother that you have through your father. Do you not see that I give the full measure and that I am the best of hosts? (59)
فَإِن لَّمْ تَأْتُونِى بِهِۦ فَلَا كَيْلَ لَكُمْ عِندِى وَلَا تَقْرَبُونِ (٦٠)
But if you do not bring him to me, then there will be no rations for you with me, and don’t [ever] come near me.’ (60)
قَالُوا۟ سَنُرَٰوِدُ عَنْهُ أَبَاهُ وَإِنَّا لَفَـٰعِلُونَ (٦١)
They said, ‘We will solicit him from his father. [That] we will surely do.’ (61)
وَقَالَ لِفِتْيَـٰنِهِ ٱجْعَلُوا۟ بِضَـٰعَتَهُمْ فِى رِحَالِهِمْ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَعْرِفُونَهَآ إِذَا ٱنقَلَبُوٓا۟ إِلَىٰٓ أَهْلِهِمْ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَرْجِعُونَ (٦٢)
He said to his servants, ‘Put their money [back] into their saddlebags. Maybe they will recognize it when they return to their folks, and maybe they will come back [again].’ (62)

Commentary

Joseph’s New Proposal to the Brothers

Finally, as had been predicted, for seven consecutive years the agricultural state of Egypt was completely satisfactory due to abundant rains and the overflowing of the Nile. Joseph, who oversaw all the treasuries of Egypt and its economic affairs, ordered the construction of small and large storehouses and granaries designed to preserve food from spoiling. He commanded the people to take the amount of produce they needed and sell the remainder to the government; thus, the warehouses and granaries were filled with provisions.

Those seven years of blessing and abundance passed, and famine and drought showed their grim face. The heavens became so stingy toward the earth that neither crops nor palm trees could moisten their lips, and the people fell into hardship regarding provisions. Since they knew the government held vast reserves, they solved their problems through this channel. Joseph, following a specific plan and order coupled with foresight, sold grain to them and met their needs in a just manner.

This drought was not limited to the land of Egypt; it spread to neighboring countries and encompassed the people of Palestine and the land of Canaan, located to the northeast of Egypt. The family of Jacob, living in that land, also faced the problem of food shortage. For this reason, Jacob decided to send his sons to Egypt, with the exception of Benjamin, who remained with his father in place of Joseph.

They set out for this land with a caravan heading to Egypt and, according to some accounts, entered Egypt after eighteen days of travel.

According to historical records, foreigners entering Egypt had to identify themselves so that officials could inform Joseph. When the officials reported the Palestinian caravan, Joseph saw the names of his brothers among those requesting grain. He recognized them and ordered that they be summoned without anyone realizing they were his brothers. As the Quran says: "And the brothers of Joseph came and entered upon him, and he recognized them, but they knew him not" (وَ جاءَ إِخْوَةُ يُوسُفَ فَدَخَلُوا عَلَيْهِ فَعَرَفَهُمْ وَ هُمْ لَهُ مُنْكِرُونَ).

They had every right not to recognize Joseph, for on one hand, thirty to forty years had passed (from the day they threw him into the well until the day they came to Egypt). On the other hand, they never imagined that their brother could have become the Al-Aziz (Mighty Governor) of Egypt. Even if they saw a resemblance to their brother, they would surely have attributed it to coincidence. Beyond all this, Joseph’s style of dress and appearance had changed so much from the past that recognizing him in his new attire—the clothing of the Egyptians—was no easy task. In fact, the possibility of Joseph being alive after that incident seemed very remote to them.

In any case, they purchased the grain they needed and paid its price, which consisted of money, frankincense, shoes, or other goods they had brought with them from Canaan to Egypt.

Joseph treated his brothers with great kindness and affection and opened a conversation with them. The brothers said: "We are ten brothers, sons of Jacob, who is the grandson of Abraham the Friend (Khalil), the great Prophet of God. If you knew our father, you would show even more respect. We have an elderly father who is among the Prophets of Allah, but a deep sorrow has enveloped his entire being!"

Joseph immediately asked: "Why all this sorrow?"

They said: "He had a son whom he loved dearly and who was younger than us in age. One day he came with us to the wilderness for hunting and recreation, and we were negligent of him, and a wolf devoured him! From that day until now, our father has been weeping and grieving for him."

Some commentators have narrated that it was Joseph’s habit not to sell more than one camel-load of grain to any individual. Since Joseph’s brothers were ten, he gave them ten loads of grain. They said: "We have an elderly father and a younger brother who remained at home. Our father cannot travel due to the intensity of his grief, and the younger brother stayed with him for service and companionship. Grant us a portion for those two as well."

Joseph ordered two more loads to be added. Then he turned to them and said: "I see you as intelligent and polite individuals. Since you say your father is very fond of the younger brother, it appears he is an extraordinary child, and I wish to see him on your next trip."

Furthermore, people here have some suspicions regarding you because you are from a foreign country. To remove these suspicions, bring your younger brother as a sign with you on your next journey.

Here, the Quran says: "And when he had furnished them with their provisions, he said, 'Bring me a brother of yours from your father'" (وَ لَمَّا جَهَّزَهُمْ بِجَهازِهِمْ قالَ ائْتُونِي بِأَخٍ لَكُمْ مِنْ أَبِيكُمْ).

Then he added: "Do you not see that I give full measure and that I am the best of hosts?" (أَ لا تَرَوْنَ أَنِّي أُوفِي الْكَيْلَ وَ أَنَا خَيْرُ الْمُنْزِلِينَ).

Following this encouragement and expression of affection, he threatened them with these words: "But if you bring him not to me, then there shall be no measure [of grain] for you with me, nor shall you even come near me" (فَإِنْ لَمْ تَأْتُونِي بِهِ فَلا كَيْلَ لَكُمْ عِنْدِي وَ لا تَقْرَبُونِ).

Joseph wanted to bring Benjamin to him by any means necessary; sometimes he approached through endearment and sometimes through threat. Additionally, it is clear from these expressions that the buying and selling of grain in Egypt was not by weight but by measure (volume), and it is also clear that Joseph hosted his brothers and other guests in the most excellent manner, being a hospitable host in every sense of the word.

The brothers replied to him: "We shall petition his father for him, and we shall certainly do it" (قالُوا سَنُراوِدُ عَنْهُ أَباهُ وَ إِنَّا لَفاعِلُونَ). The expression "we shall certainly do it" (إِنَّا لَفاعِلُونَ) shows they were certain they could influence their father and gain his consent, which is why they gave such a decisive promise to the Al-Aziz of Egypt. And so it should be; if they were able to take Joseph away from his father through persistence and pleading, how could they not separate Benjamin from him?

At this point, to further draw their affections toward himself and to give them sufficient confidence, Joseph "said to his servants, 'Put their money (merchandise) into their saddlebags so that they may recognize it when they go back to their family, and perhaps they will return [to Egypt]'" (وَ قالَ لِفِتْيانِهِ اجْعَلُوا بِضاعَتَهُمْ فِي رِحالِهِمْ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَعْرِفُونَها إِذَا انْقَلَبُوا إِلى‌ أَهْلِهِمْ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَرْجِعُونَ).

Key Points

1. Why did Joseph not identify himself to his brothers?

The first question that arises regarding the above verses is why Joseph did not introduce himself to his brothers so they could recognize him sooner and return to their father, relieving him of the agonizing grief of Joseph’s absence.

This question can be framed more broadly: when the brothers came to Joseph, at least eight years had passed since his release from prison, as he had completed the seven years of abundance during which he was busy storing food for the years of famine. In the eighth year, when the famine began, or thereafter, the brothers came to Egypt to obtain grain. Was it not necessary during these eight years to send a messenger to Canaan to inform his father of his condition and free him from that endless sorrow?

Many commentators, such as Tabarsi in Majma' al-Bayan, Allamah Tabataba'i in Al-Mizan, and Qurtubi in Al-Jami' li-Ahkam al-Quran, have addressed this question. The best answer seems to be that Joseph did not have such permission from the Lord. The story of Joseph’s separation, among other aspects, was a scene of trial and a field of testing for Jacob, and the period of this trial had to reach its end by the command of the Lord. Before that, Joseph was not authorized to give news.

Furthermore, if Joseph had immediately identified himself to his brothers, it might have caused undesirable reactions. For instance, they might have been so overcome by the terror of the situation that they would never return to him, fearing that Joseph might take revenge for the past.

2. Why did Joseph return the money to his brothers?

Why did Joseph order that the money (or goods) the brothers had paid for the grain be placed back in their saddlebags?

Multiple answers have been given to this question; for example, Fakhr al-Razi in his commentary mentions ten answers, some of which are unsuitable. However, the verses themselves provide the answer: "so that they may recognize it when they go back to their family, and perhaps they will return." Joseph’s goal was for them to see the money among their loads upon returning home and realize the nobility and generosity of the Al-Aziz of Egypt (Joseph) even more, which would serve as a reason for them to return to him and even bring their younger brother with peace of mind. Additionally, their father Jacob, seeing this situation, would have more trust in them regarding sending Benjamin to Egypt.

3. How did Joseph give from the public treasury (Bayt al-Mal) to his brothers?

Another question that arises here is how Joseph could give public funds to his brothers for free.

This can be answered in two ways: First, there was a right for the underprivileged (mustad'afin) in the Egyptian public treasury (as there always is), and national borders cannot interfere with this right. Therefore, Joseph utilized this right for his brothers, who were underprivileged at that time, just as he did for other underprivileged people. Second, in the sensitive position he held, Joseph personally had entitlements, and at the very least, his right was to provide for himself and his needy family—such as his father and brothers—at a basic subsistence level. Thus, he used his own right in this gift and bestowal.

Ayahs

فَلَمَّا رَجَعُوٓا۟ إِلَىٰٓ أَبِيهِمْ قَالُوا۟ يَـٰٓأَبَانَا مُنِعَ مِنَّا ٱلْكَيْلُ فَأَرْسِلْ مَعَنَآ أَخَانَا نَكْتَلْ وَإِنَّا لَهُۥ لَحَـٰفِظُونَ (٦٣)
So when they returned to their father, they said, ‘Father, the measure has been withheld from us, so let our brother go with us so that we may obtain the measure, and we will indeed take [good] care of him.’ (63)
قَالَ هَلْ ءَامَنُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ إِلَّا كَمَآ أَمِنتُكُمْ عَلَىٰٓ أَخِيهِ مِن قَبْلُ ۖ فَٱللَّهُ خَيْرٌ حَـٰفِظًا ۖ وَهُوَ أَرْحَمُ ٱلرَّٰحِمِينَ (٦٤)
He said, ‘Should I not trust you with him just as I trusted you with his brother before? Yet Allah is the best of protectors, and He is the most merciful of merciful ones.’ (64)
وَلَمَّا فَتَحُوا۟ مَتَـٰعَهُمْ وَجَدُوا۟ بِضَـٰعَتَهُمْ رُدَّتْ إِلَيْهِمْ ۖ قَالُوا۟ يَـٰٓأَبَانَا مَا نَبْغِى ۖ هَـٰذِهِۦ بِضَـٰعَتُنَا رُدَّتْ إِلَيْنَا ۖ وَنَمِيرُ أَهْلَنَا وَنَحْفَظُ أَخَانَا وَنَزْدَادُ كَيْلَ بَعِيرٍ ۖ ذَٰلِكَ كَيْلٌ يَسِيرٌ (٦٥)
And when they opened their baggage, they found their money restored to them. They said, ‘Father, what [more] do we want?! This is our money, restored to us! We will get provisions for our family and take care of our brother, and add another camel-load of rations. These are meagre rations.’ (65)
قَالَ لَنْ أُرْسِلَهُۥ مَعَكُمْ حَتَّىٰ تُؤْتُونِ مَوْثِقًا مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ لَتَأْتُنَّنِى بِهِۦٓ إِلَّآ أَن يُحَاطَ بِكُمْ ۖ فَلَمَّآ ءَاتَوْهُ مَوْثِقَهُمْ قَالَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ مَا نَقُولُ وَكِيلٌ (٦٦)
He said, ‘I will not let him go with you until you give me a [solemn] pledge by Allah that you will surely bring him back to me, unless you are made to perish.’ When they had given him their [solemn] pledge, he said, ‘Allah is witness over what we say.’ (66)

Commentary

Finally, the Father’s Consent Was Won

Joseph’s brothers returned to Canaan with full loads and great joy, yet they were concerned about the future. They knew that if their father did not agree to send their youngest brother (Benjamin), the Al-Aziz of Egypt would not receive them again and would grant them no further rations.

Thus, the Quran states: "So when they returned to their father, they said:

'O our father! No more measure [of grain] will be given to us [in the future]'" (فَلَمَّا رَجَعُوا إِلى‌ أَبِيهِمْ قالُوا يا أَبانا مُنِعَ مِنَّا الْكَيْلُ‌).

"Now that this is the case, send our brother with us so that we may obtain our measure" (فَأَرْسِلْ مَعَنا أَخانا نَكْتَلْ‌) [1] "and rest assured that we will indeed guard him" (وَ إِنَّا لَهُ لَحافِظُونَ‌).

The father, who had never forgotten the memory of Joseph, became distressed and anxious upon hearing this. Turning to them, "He said: 'Should I entrust him to you otherwise than I entrusted his brother Joseph to you before?'" (قالَ هَلْ آمَنُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ إِلَّا كَما أَمِنْتُكُمْ عَلى‌ أَخِيهِ مِنْ قَبْلُ‌).

[^1]: "نكتل" (Naktal) was originally "نكتال" (Naktaal), derived from the root "kayl" (measure), meaning to receive something by measure. Conversely, "kaala" means to give or pay out by measure.

That is to say: with such a dark past that can never be forgotten, how do you expect me to trust your proposal once more and entrust another beloved child to you—especially on a long journey to a foreign land?! He then added: "In any case, Allah is the best Guardian, and He is the most Merciful of the merciful" (فَاللَّهُ خَيْرٌ حافِظاً وَ هُوَ أَرْحَمُ الرَّاحِمِينَ‌).

This sentence may imply that: "It is difficult for me to send Benjamin with you people of bad record; and if I do send him, it is out of reliance on Allah’s protection and His being the Most Merciful, not out of reliance on you!"

Therefore, the above sentence does not indicate a definitive acceptance of their proposal, but rather a conditional discussion. As becomes clear from the upcoming verses, Jacob (peace be upon him) had not yet accepted their proposal; he only did so after taking a solemn oath and covenant and following other events that transpired.

Furthermore, it might be a reference to Joseph; for at that moment, he remembered Joseph, and he already knew he was alive. (We will read in future verses that he was certain of Joseph’s life). Thus, he prayed for his protection: "O Allah, wherever he is, keep him safe!"

Then, "when they opened their baggage, they found to their great surprise that all they had paid as the price for the grain to the Al-Aziz of Egypt had been returned to them and was inside their bags!" (وَ لَمَّا فَتَحُوا مَتاعَهُمْ وَجَدُوا بِضاعَتَهُمْ رُدَّتْ إِلَيْهِمْ‌). Finding this to be definitive proof for their claims, they came to their father and "said: 'O our father! What more can we desire? Look, all our goods

have been returned to us'" (قالُوا يا أَبانا ما نَبْغِي هذِهِ بِضاعَتُنا رُدَّتْ إِلَيْنا) [1]. Could there be any greater nobility than this—that the ruler of a foreign land, during such a famine and drought, gives us food supplies and returns our money as well?

And to do so in a way that we wouldn't even notice, so as not to feel ashamed—what could be better than this?! "O father! There is no more room for delay; send our brother with us. We will bring food supplies for our family" (وَ نَمِيرُ أَهْلَنا) [2] "and we will strive to protect our brother" (وَ نَحْفَظُ أَخانا).

"And we will add another camel-load [of grain] because of him" (وَ نَزْدادُ كَيْلَ بَعِيرٍ).

"And this [extra load] is a simple and easy matter for the Al-Aziz of Egypt, that noble and generous man we saw" (ذلِكَ كَيْلٌ يَسِيرٌ) [3].

Despite all this, Jacob was still not satisfied with sending his son Benjamin with them. On the other hand, their persistence, accompanied by clear logic, compelled him to yield to the proposal. Finally, he saw the solution in giving a conditional consent to sending his son. Thus, he said to them:

[^1]: The phrase "ما نَبْغِي" (Ma nabghi) could be "interrogative," meaning: "What more do we want beyond this?" Or it could be "negative," meaning: "We do not intend to lie," or "We do not want any more money from you; this is enough."

[^2]: "Namiru" is from the root "mirah," meaning to bring food and provisions.

[^3]: The phrase "ذلِكَ كَيْلٌ يَسِيرٌ" (That is an easy measure), in addition to what was mentioned in the text, could also mean that the brothers intended to say: "What we brought was a small measure; if our younger brother comes with us, we can obtain a much larger quantity of grain."

"I will never send him with you until you give me a solemn pledge in the name of Allah—something that provides me with assurance and trust—that you will bring him back to me, unless you are overwhelmed [by death or other factors beyond your control]" (قالَ لَنْ أُرْسِلَهُ مَعَكُمْ حَتَّى تُؤْتُونِ مَوْثِقاً مِنَ اللَّهِ لَتَأْتُنَّنِي بِهِ إِلَّا أَنْ يُحاطَ بِكُمْ‌). The "solemn pledge in the name of Allah" (مَوْثِقاً مِنَ اللَّهِ) refers to a covenant and oath sworn by the name of God.

The phrase "إِلَّا أَنْ يُحاطَ بِكُمْ‌" (unless you are surrounded/overwhelmed) originally means "unless events surround you," i.e., you are defeated by circumstances. This phrase may be an allusion to death or other calamities that bring a person to their knees and strip them of their power [1]. The mention of this exception is a sign of the outstanding wisdom of the Prophet Jacob (peace be upon him); despite his intense love for Benjamin, he did not impose an impossible task (taklif ma la yutaq) upon his other sons. He said, "I demand my son back from you unless events occur that are beyond your power, in which case no sin lies upon you."

Naturally, if some of them were caught in an accident and lost their power, the rest were still duty-bound to return the father’s trust to him. Therefore, Jacob says, "unless all of you are overwhelmed by events."

In any case, Joseph’s brothers accepted their father’s proposal, "and when they had given him their solemn pledge, Jacob said: 'Allah is Witness, Watcher, and Guardian

[^1]: This expression appears in several places in the Holy Quran meaning destruction or annihilation, such as: "and they thought they were overwhelmed" (Jonah: 22) and "so his fruits were encircled [by ruin]" (Al-Kahf: 42). However, it is clear that in the verse under discussion, it does not specifically mean destruction, but rather any excuse that strips a person of their power.

over what we say'" (فَلَمَّا آتَوْهُ مَوْثِقَهُمْ قالَ اللَّهُ عَلى‌ ما نَقُولُ وَكِيلٌ‌).

Key Points

1- How Jacob agreed to entrust Benjamin to the brothers

The first question that comes to mind regarding the above verses is: how did Jacob agree to entrust Benjamin to them, considering the brothers were individuals with a bad record due to their behavior toward Joseph? Furthermore, we know they did not only harbor malice and envy toward Joseph, but they held the same feelings—albeit perhaps to a lesser degree—toward Benjamin, as we read in the opening verses of the Surah: "When they said: 'Joseph and his brother are more beloved to our father than we are, even though we are a strong group...'"

However, paying attention to this point clarifies the answer: thirty to forty years had passed since the incident with Joseph. Joseph’s young brothers had reached middle age and had naturally become more mature. Moreover, they had deeply felt the undesirable consequences of their plot against Joseph within the family environment and within their own restless consciences. Experience had shown them that the loss of Joseph had not only failed to secure their father’s affection but had created a new distance. Above all, the issue was one of survival—the matter of procuring food during a famine for a large family—not a mere recreational outing like the one they proposed for Joseph. All these factors combined led Jacob to yield to his sons' proposal, provided they made a divine covenant with him to bring their brother Benjamin back safely.

2- Was a mere oath and covenant sufficient?

Another question that arises here is: was a mere oath

and a divine covenant sufficient for him to hand Benjamin over to them?

The answer is that certainly, the oath and covenant alone were not enough. However, the evidence and circumstances indicated that this time, a reality was being discussed, not a conspiracy, deception, or lie. Therefore, the covenant and oath were, so to speak, for "extra security" and further emphasis. This is much like what we see in our own time, where political figures such as presidents and members of parliament take an oath of loyalty to perform their duties after careful scrutiny has been applied to their selection.

Ayahs

وَقَالَ يَـٰبَنِىَّ لَا تَدْخُلُوا۟ مِنۢ بَابٍ وَٰحِدٍ وَٱدْخُلُوا۟ مِنْ أَبْوَٰبٍ مُّتَفَرِّقَةٍ ۖ وَمَآ أُغْنِى عَنكُم مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ مِن شَىْءٍ ۖ إِنِ ٱلْحُكْمُ إِلَّا لِلَّهِ ۖ عَلَيْهِ تَوَكَّلْتُ ۖ وَعَلَيْهِ فَلْيَتَوَكَّلِ ٱلْمُتَوَكِّلُونَ (٦٧)
And he said, ‘My sons, do not enter by one gate, but enter by separate gates, though I cannot avail you anything against Allah. Sovereignty belongs only to Allah. In Him I have put my trust; and in Him let all the trusting put their trust.’ (67)
وَلَمَّا دَخَلُوا۟ مِنْ حَيْثُ أَمَرَهُمْ أَبُوهُم مَّا كَانَ يُغْنِى عَنْهُم مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ مِن شَىْءٍ إِلَّا حَاجَةً فِى نَفْسِ يَعْقُوبَ قَضَىٰهَا ۚ وَإِنَّهُۥ لَذُو عِلْمٍ لِّمَا عَلَّمْنَـٰهُ وَلَـٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ ٱلنَّاسِ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ (٦٨)
When they entered whence their father had bidden them, it did not avail them anything against Allah, but only fulfilled a wish in Jacob’s heart. Indeed, he had the knowledge of what We had taught him, but most people do not know. (68)

Commentary

Finally, after obtaining their father's consent, Joseph's brothers took their younger brother with them and prepared to set out for Egypt for the second time. At this point, their father gave them a piece of advice and an instruction: "He said: O my sons! Do not enter from one gate, but enter from different gates" (وَ قالَ يا بَنِيَّ لا تَدْخُلُوا مِنْ بابٍ واحِدٍ وَ ادْخُلُوا مِنْ أَبْوابٍ مُتَفَرِّقَةٍ).

He added: "I cannot avail you anything against [the decree of] Allah" (وَ ما أُغْنِي عَنْكُمْ مِنَ اللَّهِ مِنْ شَيْ‌ءٍ).

However, there are a series of unfortunate events and incidents that are avoidable and for which no definitive Divine decree has been issued; my goal is that these should be averted from you, and this is possible.

And in the end, he said: "Judgment belongs only to Allah" (إِنِ الْحُكْمُ إِلَّا لِلَّهِ‌).

"In Him I have put my trust" (عَلَيْهِ تَوَكَّلْتُ‌).

And "In Him let all those who trust put their trust, seek His help, and leave their affairs to Him" (وَ عَلَيْهِ فَلْيَتَوَكَّلِ الْمُتَوَكِّلُونَ‌).

Undoubtedly, the capital of Egypt at that time, like any other city, had walls, towers, and fortifications with multiple gates. However, the reason why Jacob عليه السلام advised his sons not to enter through a single gate, but rather to divide into groups and have each group enter through a different gate, is not mentioned in the above verse. A group of commentators have said:

The reason for this instruction was that Joseph's brothers were blessed with sufficient beauty (even if they were not Joseph, they were, after all, Joseph's brothers!) and possessed tall, handsome statures. The father was concerned that a group of eleven people, whose appearances showed they had come to Egypt from another country, would attract people's attention. He did not want them to be afflicted by the "evil eye" in this way.

Following this interpretation, a detailed discussion has taken place among commentators regarding the effect of the evil eye, citing evidence from narrations and history. God willing, we will discuss this under the verse: وَ إِنْ يَكادُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا لَيُزْلِقُونَكَ بِأَبْصارِهِمْ (Surah Al-Qalam, Verse 51), where we will prove that part of this subject is true and can be justified scientifically through a specific magnetic fluid that emanates from the eye, even though the common folk have mixed it with a great deal of superstition.

Another reason mentioned for this instruction by Jacob عليه السلام is that their collective entry through one gate of Egypt and their movement as a group—with their attractive appearances and large builds—might have incited the envy of the envious. They might have been slandered before the government apparatus and viewed with suspicion as a foreign group intending to commit sabotage. Therefore, the father ordered them to enter through different gates so as not to attract attention.

Some commentators [^1] have also offered a more metaphorical interpretation for the above verse: that Jacob wanted to give his sons an important social instruction as a parting gift, which is that they should not seek what they have lost through only one door, but should enter through every possible door. This is because it often happens that a person chooses only one path to reach a goal, and when they hit a dead end, they become disappointed and give up. However, if one pays attention to the fact that lost things usually do not have only one path and begins searching through various ways, they will often succeed.

The brothers set out, and after traversing the long distance between Canaan and Egypt, they entered the land of Egypt. "And when they entered from where their father had commanded them, it could not avail them anything against [the decree of] Allah" (وَ لَمَّا دَخَلُوا مِنْ حَيْثُ أَمَرَهُمْ أَبُوهُمْ ما كانَ يُغْنِي عَنْهُمْ مِنَ اللَّهِ مِنْ شَيْ‌ءٍ).

Rather, its only benefit was "that it was a need in Jacob's soul which he [thereby] satisfied" (إِلَّا حاجَةً فِي نَفْسِ يَعْقُوبَ قَضاها).

This indicates that its only effect was to soothe the father's mind and bring peace to his heart. Since he was far from all his children and thought of them and Joseph day and night, fearing the harm of accidents and the envy of the envious and ill-wishers, he was comforted simply by the assurance that they were following his instructions.

Then the Quran praises and describes Jacob with this sentence: "And indeed, he was a possessor of knowledge because of what We had taught him, but most of the people do not know" (وَ إِنَّهُ لَذُو عِلْمٍ لِما عَلَّمْناهُ وَ لكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ النَّاسِ لا يَعْلَمُونَ‌).

This points to the fact that many people become so lost in the world of causes that they forget Allah. They imagine, for example, that the evil eye is an unavoidable effect of certain eyes, and for this reason, they forget Allah and trust in Him, clinging instead to this or that. But Jacob was not like this; he knew that nothing happens unless Allah wills it. Therefore, his primary trust and reliance were upon Allah, and then he would seek the world of causes. At the same time, he knew that behind these causes lies the Pure Essence of the "Causer of Causes," just as the Quran says in Surah Al-Baqarah, Verse 102, regarding the sorcerers of Babylon: وَ ما هُمْ بِضارِّينَ بِهِ مِنْ أَحَدٍ إِلَّا بِإِذْنِ اللَّهِ (But they could not harm anyone through it except by Allah's permission). This indicates that above all these is the Will of Allah; one must set their heart upon Him and seek help from Him.

[^1]: The noble scholar and orator, the late Ishraqi (may his soul be sanctified).

Ayahs

وَلَمَّا دَخَلُوا۟ عَلَىٰ يُوسُفَ ءَاوَىٰٓ إِلَيْهِ أَخَاهُ ۖ قَالَ إِنِّىٓ أَنَا۠ أَخُوكَ فَلَا تَبْتَئِسْ بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ (٦٩)
When they entered into the presence of Joseph, he set his brother close to himself, and said, ‘Indeed I am your brother, so do not sorrow for what they used to do.’ (69)
فَلَمَّا جَهَّزَهُم بِجَهَازِهِمْ جَعَلَ ٱلسِّقَايَةَ فِى رَحْلِ أَخِيهِ ثُمَّ أَذَّنَ مُؤَذِّنٌ أَيَّتُهَا ٱلْعِيرُ إِنَّكُمْ لَسَـٰرِقُونَ (٧٠)
When he had furnished them with their provision, he put the drinking-cup into his brother’s saddlebag. Then a herald shouted: ‘O [men of the] caravan! You are indeed thieves!’ (70)
قَالُوا۟ وَأَقْبَلُوا۟ عَلَيْهِم مَّاذَا تَفْقِدُونَ (٧١)
They said, as they turned towards them, ‘What are you missing?’ (71)
قَالُوا۟ نَفْقِدُ صُوَاعَ ٱلْمَلِكِ وَلِمَن جَآءَ بِهِۦ حِمْلُ بَعِيرٍ وَأَنَا۠ بِهِۦ زَعِيمٌ (٧٢)
They said, ‘We miss the king’s goblet.’ ‘Whoever brings it shall have a camel-load [of grain],’ [said the steward], ‘I will guarantee that.’ (72)
قَالُوا۟ تَٱللَّهِ لَقَدْ عَلِمْتُم مَّا جِئْنَا لِنُفْسِدَ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَمَا كُنَّا سَـٰرِقِينَ (٧٣)
They said, ‘By Allah! You certainly know that we did not come to make trouble in this country, and we are not thieves.’ (73)
قَالُوا۟ فَمَا جَزَٰٓؤُهُۥٓ إِن كُنتُمْ كَـٰذِبِينَ (٧٤)
They said, ‘What shall be its requital if you [prove to] be lying?’ (74)
قَالُوا۟ جَزَٰٓؤُهُۥ مَن وُجِدَ فِى رَحْلِهِۦ فَهُوَ جَزَٰٓؤُهُۥ ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ نَجْزِى ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (٧٥)
They said, ‘The requital for it shall be that he in whose saddlebag it is found shall give himself over as its requital. Thus do we requite the wrongdoers.’ (75)
فَبَدَأَ بِأَوْعِيَتِهِمْ قَبْلَ وِعَآءِ أَخِيهِ ثُمَّ ٱسْتَخْرَجَهَا مِن وِعَآءِ أَخِيهِ ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ كِدْنَا لِيُوسُفَ ۖ مَا كَانَ لِيَأْخُذَ أَخَاهُ فِى دِينِ ٱلْمَلِكِ إِلَّآ أَن يَشَآءَ ٱللَّهُ ۚ نَرْفَعُ دَرَجَـٰتٍ مَّن نَّشَآءُ ۗ وَفَوْقَ كُلِّ ذِى عِلْمٍ عَلِيمٌ (٧٦)
Then he began with their sacks, before [opening] his brother’s sack. Then he took it out from his brother’s sack. Thus did We devise for Joseph’s sake. He could not have held his brother under the king’s law unless Allah willed [otherwise]. We raise in rank whomever We please, and above every man of knowledge is One who knows best. (76)

Commentary

A Plan to Keep the Brother

Finally, the brothers entered upon Joseph, and they informed him that they had carried out his command. They explained that although their father was initially unwilling to send the younger brother with them, they had persuaded him through persistence, so that Joseph would know they were faithful to their word and covenant.

Joseph received them with full honor and hospitality and invited them to his banquet. He ordered that they sit in pairs at the dining tables or trays. They did so, but Benjamin, who was left alone, began to weep and said: "If my brother Joseph were alive, he would have seated me with him at one table, for we were from the same father and mother." Joseph turned to them and said: "It seems your younger brother has been left alone? To relieve his loneliness, I shall seat him with myself at my table!" Then he ordered that a bedroom be prepared for every two people, and again Benjamin was left alone. Joseph said: "Send him to me." At this moment, Joseph gave his brother a place with him, but he saw that he was very distressed and anxious, constantly remembering his lost brother, Joseph. At this point, Joseph’s cup of patience overflowed, and he unveiled the truth, as the Quran says: "When they entered upon Joseph, he took his brother to himself and said: 'Indeed, I am your brother [Joseph]; so do not despair over what they used to do.'"

(وَلَمَّا دَخَلُوا عَلَىٰ يُوسُفَ آوَىٰ إِلَيْهِ أَخَاهُ ۖ قَالَ إِنِّي أَنَا أَخُوكَ فَلَا تَبْتَئِسْ بِمَا كَانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ)

"La tabta'is" (لَا تَبْتَئِسْ) is derived from the root "bu's" (بؤس), which originally means harm and severity, and here it means "do not be grieved or saddened." The "actions of the brothers" that distressed Benjamin refer to the unkindness they showed toward him and Joseph, and the plots they devised to exclude them from the family. [Joseph's message was:] Now that you see their actions did not end in my ruin but were instead a means for my elevation and advancement, do not let any grief or sorrow from that direction enter your heart.

At this time, according to some narrations, Joseph said to his brother Benjamin: "Would you like to stay with me?" He replied: "Yes, but my brothers will never agree, for they have promised our father and sworn an oath that they will return me at any cost." Joseph said: "Do not worry; I will devise a plan so that they will be forced to leave you with me." "Then, when he had furnished them with their supplies,"

he ordered the special, precious goblet to be placed inside his brother Benjamin's saddlebag (since he was providing a load of grain for each of them). (فَلَمَّا جَهَّزَهُم بِجَهَازِهِمْ جَعَلَ السِّقَايَةَ فِي رَحْلِ أَخِيهِ).

Of course, this was done in secret, and perhaps only one of the officials was aware of it. At this moment, the food measurement officials noticed that there was no sign of the special and precious goblet, even though it had been in their hands previously. Therefore, just as the caravan was ready to depart, someone shouted: "O people of the caravan, indeed you are thieves!" (ثُمَّ أَذَّنَ مُؤَذِّنٌ أَيَّتُهَا الْعِيرُ إِنَّكُمْ لَسَارِقُونَ).

When Joseph's brothers heard this sentence, they were deeply shaken and terrified, for such a possibility had never crossed their minds—that after all this honor and hospitality, they would be accused of theft!

Therefore, "they turned toward them and said: 'What is it that you have lost?'" (قَالُوا وَأَقْبَلُوا عَلَيْهِم مَّاذَا تَفْقِدُونَ)

"They said: 'We have lost the King's goblet, and we are suspicious of you.'" (قَالُوا نَفْقِدُ صُوَاعَ الْمَلِكِ). And since the goblet was precious and favored by the King, "for him who brings it shall be a camel-load [of grain] as a reward." (وَلِمَن جَاءَ بِهِ حِمْلُ بَعِيرٍ).

Then the speaker of these words said for further emphasis: "And I personally guarantee this reward." (وَأَنَا بِهِ زَعِيمٌ).

The brothers, who were deeply concerned and flustered by hearing this, and did not know what was happening, turned to them and "said: 'By Allah, you certainly know that we did not come to cause corruption in the land, and we have never been thieves.'" (قَالُوا تَاللَّهِ لَقَدْ عَلِمْتُمْ مَا جِئْنَا لِنُفْسِدَ فِي الْأَرْضِ وَمَا كُنَّا سَارِقِينَ).

Their statement "you yourselves know that we are not people of corruption and theft" perhaps refers to the fact that [the Egyptians] had a good record of them; for on the previous occasion, the price they had paid was placed back in their bags, and they had returned to them and declared they were ready to give it all back. Therefore, how could people who return from a distant country to fulfill their debt commit theft?

Furthermore, it is said that upon entering Egypt, they had muzzled their camels so they would not damage anyone's crops or property. [Their logic was:] We who take such care that even our animals do not harm anyone, how could we commit such a loathsome act?!

At this point, the officials turned to them and "said: 'Then what shall be its recompense if you are liars?'" (قَالُوا فَمَا جَزَاؤُهُ إِن كُنتُمْ كَاذِبِينَ).

And "they replied: 'Its recompense is that he in whose bag it is found—he [himself] shall be the recompense for it.'" (قَالُوا جَزَاؤُهُ مَن وُجِدَ فِي رَحْلِهِ فَهُوَ جَزَاؤُهُ). "Yes, thus do we punish the wrongdoers." (كَذَٰلِكَ نَجْزِي الظَّالِمِينَ).

At this moment, Joseph ordered that their bags be opened and inspected one by one. However, so that Joseph's original plan would not be revealed, "he began with their bags before the bag of his brother [Benjamin], and then he extracted it from his brother's bag." (فَبَدَأَ بِأَوْعِيَتِهِمْ قَبْلَ وِعَاءِ أَخِيهِ ثُمَّ اسْتَخْرَجَهَا مِن وِعَاءِ أَخِيهِ).

As soon as the goblet was found in Benjamin's bag, the brothers' mouths hung open in astonishment; it was as if a mountain of grief and sorrow had descended upon them, and they found themselves in a strange dead end.

On one hand, their brother had apparently committed such a theft, which was a source of disgrace for them. On the other hand, it jeopardized their position with the Al-Aziz (the Governor) of Egypt, making it impossible to seek his support in the future. Above all, what would they say to their father? How would he believe that the brothers were not at fault in this matter?

Some commentators have written that at this moment, the brothers turned to Benjamin and said: "O heedless one! You have disgraced us and blackened our faces. What is this wrong act you have done? (You had mercy neither on yourself, nor on us, nor on the family of Jacob, which is a family of Prophethood). Tell us, when did you take this goblet and put it in your bag?"

Benjamin, who knew the inner reality of the matter, replied calmly: "The one who put your payments back in your bags did this!" But the incident was so distressing for the brothers that they did not understand what he was saying [1]. Then the Quran adds: "Thus did We plan for Joseph" (so that he could keep his brother with him in a way that the other brothers could not resist). (كَذَٰلِكَ كِدْنَا لِيُوسُفَ).

The important point here is that if Joseph had wanted to deal with his brother Benjamin according to the laws of Egypt, he would have had to have him beaten and cast into prison. Besides causing suffering to his brother, his goal—which was to keep his brother with him—would not have been achieved. Therefore, he first obtained a confession from the brothers: "If you commit theft, what is the punishment among you?" They replied according to their own custom: "In our environment, the custom is that the thief himself is taken [as a slave] in exchange for the theft he committed and is made to work." Joseph dealt with them according to this very program, for one of the ways to punish a culprit is to punish him according to his own law and custom.

For this reason, the Quran says: "Joseph could not have taken his brother according to the law of the King [of Egypt]" and kept him with himself (مَا كَانَ لِيَأْخُذَ أَخَاهُ فِي دِينِ الْمَلِكِ).

Then, as an exception, He says: "Except if Allah willed" (إِلَّا أَن يَشَاءَ اللَّهُ).

This indicates that what Joseph did—treating the brothers according to their own custom—was by Divine command. It was a plan to protect the brother, to complete the trial of his father Jacob, and to test the other brothers! At the end, He adds: "We raise in degrees whom We will" (نَرْفَعُ دَرَجَاتٍ مَّن نَّشَاءُ).

The degrees of those who are worthy and who, like Joseph, emerge safely from the crucible of trials.

"And in any case, above every possessor of knowledge is one more knowing" (meaning Allah) (وَفَوْقَ كُلِّ ذِي عِلْمٍ عَلِيمٌ).

And it was He who had inspired Joseph with the design of this plan.

Key Points

The above verses raise many questions that must be answered one by one:

1- Why did Joseph not introduce himself to the brothers sooner to relieve his father of the agonizing grief of separation?

The answer to this question, as previously mentioned, was to complete the program of testing the father and the brothers. In other words, this act was not out of whim or desire, but according to a Divine command that sought to test Jacob's resilience in the face of losing a second son, thereby implementing the final stage of his perfection. It was also to test the brothers: now that their brother was caught in such a fate, what would they do regarding the covenant they had made with their father to protect him?

2- How could an innocent person be accused of theft?

Was it permissible to accuse an innocent person of theft—an accusation whose ill effects would more or less affect the rest of the brothers?

The answer to this can also be found in the fact that this matter was done with the agreement of Benjamin himself, for Joseph had already introduced himself to him. Benjamin knew this plan was devised to keep him there. As for the brothers, no formal charge was brought against them [individually]; it only created anxiety and distress, which was permissible in the context of an important trial.

3- What is the meaning of attributing theft to everyone?

Was the attribution of theft, especially in a general and collective manner with the phrase "Indeed you are thieves" (إِنَّكُمْ لَسَارِقُونَ), not a lie? What was the justification for this lie and accusation?

The answer to this question is clarified by the following analysis:

Firstly: It is not certain who the speakers of these words were. We only read in the Quran "They said" (قالوا). It is possible that the speakers were a group of Joseph's officials who, when they did not find the special goblet, became certain that one of the Canaanite caravanners had stolen it. It is common that if something is stolen from within a structured group and the actual thief is not known, the speaker addresses the whole group and says, "You did this," meaning "one of you" or "some of you."

Secondly: The primary subject of the statement, Benjamin, was satisfied with this attribution because while this plan apparently accused him of theft, in reality, it was a prelude to him staying with his brother Joseph.

The fact that all of them fell under suspicion was a fleeting matter that was resolved as soon as the bags of Joseph's brothers were inspected, and the primary party (Benjamin) was identified.

Some have also said that the "theft" attributed to them here referred to the past—the brothers' "stealing" of Joseph from his father Jacob. However, this would only apply if the attribution was made by Joseph himself, as he was aware of the history. Perhaps the subsequent phrase hints at this, as Joseph's officials did not say "You stole the King's goblet," but rather:

(نَفْقِدُ صُوَاعَ الْمَلِكِ): "We are missing the King's goblet" (though the first answer seems more correct).

4- What was the punishment for theft at that time?

It can be inferred from the above verses that the punishment for theft differed between the Egyptians and the people of Canaan. Among Joseph's brothers, and likely the people of Canaan, the punishment for this act was the enslavement (permanent or temporary) of the thief to the victim of the theft [1]. However, this punishment was not customary among the Egyptians; rather, they punished thieves through other means, such as beating and imprisonment.

In any case, this sentence does not serve as proof that taking slaves was the punishment for a thief in every Divine religion; it may have been a common custom among a group of people at that time. In the history of slavery, we also read that among ancient peoples, debtors were taken as slaves when they were unable to pay their debts.

5- The "Siqayah" or the "Suwa'"

In the above verses, the object is sometimes referred to as "Suwa'" (measure) and sometimes as "Siqayah" (drinking vessel). There is no contradiction between the two, as it appears this vessel was originally the King's drinking cup. However, when grain became expensive, scarce, and rationed in the land of Egypt, to demonstrate its importance and to ensure people exercised the utmost care in conservation, they used the King's special drinking vessel as a measure.

Commentators have many details regarding the specifics of this vessel; some say it was made of silver, some say gold, and some add that it was encrusted with jewels. Some unreliable narrations also hint at such things, but none have clear evidence.

What is certain is that it was a vessel from which the King of Egypt once drank, and it was subsequently converted into a measure.

It is also obvious that all the needs of a country cannot be measured with a single such vessel. Perhaps this act was symbolic, intended to show the scarcity and importance of grain during those specific years so that people would be extremely frugal in its consumption.

Additionally, since this measure was in Joseph's possession at that time, it meant that if they were to take the thief as a slave, he would have to become the slave of the owner of the measure—namely Joseph himself—and remain with him. This was exactly what Joseph had planned for.

[^1]: Tabarsi in Majma' al-Bayan has narrated that the custom among a group of people at that time was to take the thief as a slave for one year. He also narrated that the family of Jacob would take the thief as a slave in proportion to his theft (so that he would work for that amount).

Ayahs

۞ قَالُوٓا۟ إِن يَسْرِقْ فَقَدْ سَرَقَ أَخٌ لَّهُۥ مِن قَبْلُ ۚ فَأَسَرَّهَا يُوسُفُ فِى نَفْسِهِۦ وَلَمْ يُبْدِهَا لَهُمْ ۚ قَالَ أَنتُمْ شَرٌّ مَّكَانًا ۖ وَٱللَّهُ أَعْلَمُ بِمَا تَصِفُونَ (٧٧)
They said, ‘If he has stolen [there is no wonder]; a brother of his had stolen before.’ Thereupon Joseph kept the matter to himself and he did not disclose it to them. He said, ‘You are in a worse state! And Allah knows best what you allege.’ (77)
قَالُوا۟ يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلْعَزِيزُ إِنَّ لَهُۥٓ أَبًا شَيْخًا كَبِيرًا فَخُذْ أَحَدَنَا مَكَانَهُۥٓ ۖ إِنَّا نَرَىٰكَ مِنَ ٱلْمُحْسِنِينَ (٧٨)
They said, ‘O emir! Indeed, he has a father, a very old man; so take one of us in his place. Indeed we see that you are a virtuous man.’ (78)
قَالَ مَعَاذَ ٱللَّهِ أَن نَّأْخُذَ إِلَّا مَن وَجَدْنَا مَتَـٰعَنَا عِندَهُۥٓ إِنَّآ إِذًا لَّظَـٰلِمُونَ (٧٩)
He said, ‘God forbid that we should detain anyone except him with whom we found our wares, for then we would indeed be wrongdoers.’ (79)

Commentary

Why was the brothers' sacrifice not accepted?

The brothers finally believed that their brother, Benjamin, had committed a foul and ominous theft, which had completely ruined their reputation before the Aziz of Egypt. Therefore, to exonerate themselves, they said: "If this boy steals, it is no wonder, for his brother (Joseph) also committed such an act before" (قالُوا إِنْ يَسْرِقْ فَقَدْ سَرَقَ أَخٌ لَهُ مِنْ قَبْلُ‌). They claimed that both were from the same mother and father, and their account was separate from theirs, as they were from a different mother!

In this way, they wanted to draw a dividing line between themselves and Benjamin and link his fate to that of his brother, Joseph! Joseph was deeply pained by hearing these words and "kept it secret within his heart, and did not reveal it to them" (فَأَسَرَّها يُوسُفُ فِي نَفْسِهِ وَ لَمْ يُبْدِها لَهُمْ‌).

For He knew that with these words, they had committed a great slander, yet He did not engage in answering them. He only said to them in a veiled manner: "You are in a worse position than the one you attribute this to"—or—you are the worst of people in my eyes in terms of status and rank (قالَ أَنْتُمْ شَرٌّ مَكاناً).

Then He added: "And Allah knows best what you describe" (وَ اللَّهُ أَعْلَمُ بِما تَصِفُونَ‌).

It is true that Joseph's brothers cast an unjust accusation against their brother Joseph in the hope of exonerating themselves in these critical moments, but such an act ultimately requires a pretext or excuse to make such an attribution. For this reason, commentators have researched this matter and cited three narrations from ancient histories in this regard:

First: After the death of his mother, Joseph lived with his paternal aunt, who was deeply fond of him. When he grew up and Jacob عليه السلام wanted to take him back from his aunt, she devised a plan. She tied a special belt or shawl that had remained as a legacy from Isaac عليه السلام in their family around Joseph's waist and claimed that he had intended to steal it from her. According to their law and tradition, she kept Joseph with her in exchange for that special belt and shawl.

Second: One of Joseph's maternal relatives had an idol which Joseph took, broke, and threw onto the road; therefore, they accused him of theft, while none of these were actually theft.

Third: Sometimes he would take some food from the table and give it to the poor and needy. For this reason, the fault-finding brothers used this as a pretext to accuse him of theft, while none of these were sins. Is it correct to accuse someone of theft if another person puts a garment on them without them knowing it belongs to someone else? And is there any sin in taking an idol and breaking it? Furthermore, what is the harm in a person taking something from his father's table—knowing for certain that he would be pleased—and giving it to the poor?!

When the brothers saw that their younger brother Benjamin had to remain with the Aziz of Egypt according to the law they themselves had accepted, and on the other hand, they had made a covenant with their father to exert their maximum effort in protecting and returning Benjamin, they turned to Joseph—who was still unknown to them—"and said: O Aziz of Egypt! O noble ruler! He has a father, an aged old man, who cannot endure the separation from him. We separated him from his father upon your insistence, and he took a firm covenant from us to return him at any cost. Come, show nobility and take one of us in his place" (قالُوا يا أَيُّهَا الْعَزِيزُ إِنَّ لَهُ أَباً شَيْخاً كَبِيراً فَخُذْ أَحَدَنا مَكانَهُ‌).

"For we see you as one of the doers of good," and this is not the first time you have shown us kindness; come and complete your kindness with this act (إِنَّا نَراكَ مِنَ الْمُحْسِنِينَ‌).

Joseph strongly rejected this proposal and "said: Allah forbid that we should take anyone except him with whom we found our property" (قالَ مَعاذَ اللَّهِ أَنْ نَأْخُذَ إِلَّا مَنْ وَجَدْنا مَتاعَنا عِنْدَهُ‌). Have you ever heard of a fair person punishing an innocent person for the crime of another? If we were to do such a thing, we would surely be among the wrongdoers (إِنَّا إِذاً لَظالِمُونَ‌).

It is noteworthy that in this speech, Joseph does not attribute theft to his brother in any way; rather, He refers to him as the one "with whom we found our property." This is evidence that He was precisely careful never to speak untruthfully in His life.

Ayahs

فَلَمَّا ٱسْتَيْـَٔسُوا۟ مِنْهُ خَلَصُوا۟ نَجِيًّا ۖ قَالَ كَبِيرُهُمْ أَلَمْ تَعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّ أَبَاكُمْ قَدْ أَخَذَ عَلَيْكُم مَّوْثِقًا مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ وَمِن قَبْلُ مَا فَرَّطتُمْ فِى يُوسُفَ ۖ فَلَنْ أَبْرَحَ ٱلْأَرْضَ حَتَّىٰ يَأْذَنَ لِىٓ أَبِىٓ أَوْ يَحْكُمَ ٱللَّهُ لِى ۖ وَهُوَ خَيْرُ ٱلْحَـٰكِمِينَ (٨٠)
When they had despaired of [moving] him, they withdrew to confer privately. The eldest of them said, ‘Don’t you know that your father has taken a [solemn] pledge from you by Allah, and earlier you have neglected your duty in regard to Joseph? So I will never leave this land until my father permits me, or Allah passes a judgement for me, and He is the best of judges. (80)
ٱرْجِعُوٓا۟ إِلَىٰٓ أَبِيكُمْ فَقُولُوا۟ يَـٰٓأَبَانَآ إِنَّ ٱبْنَكَ سَرَقَ وَمَا شَهِدْنَآ إِلَّا بِمَا عَلِمْنَا وَمَا كُنَّا لِلْغَيْبِ حَـٰفِظِينَ (٨١)
Go back to your father, and say, ‘‘Father! Your son has indeed committed theft, and we testified only to what we knew, and we could not have forestalled the unseen. (81)
وَسْـَٔلِ ٱلْقَرْيَةَ ٱلَّتِى كُنَّا فِيهَا وَٱلْعِيرَ ٱلَّتِىٓ أَقْبَلْنَا فِيهَا ۖ وَإِنَّا لَصَـٰدِقُونَ (٨٢)
Ask [the people of] the town we were in, and the caravan with which we came. We indeed speak the truth.’’ ’ (82)

Commentary

Did the Disgraced Brothers Return to Their Father?

The brothers exerted their final efforts and attempts to save Benjamin, but they found all paths closed to them. On one hand, the circumstances had been arranged in such a way that exonerating their brother seemed ostensibly impossible; on the other hand, the proposal to accept another person in his place was rejected by the Al-Aziz. Consequently, they became despondent and decided to return to Canaan and relate the incident to their father. The Quran states:

"So when they despaired of him—or of saving their brother—they went apart to confer privately" (فَلَمَّا اسْتَيْأَسُوا مِنْهُ خَلَصُوا نَجِيًّا).

"Khalasu" (خلصوا) means "they became pure," which is an allusion to separating themselves from others and forming a private meeting. "Najiyya" (نجيا) is derived from the root "munajat," originally from "najwah," meaning elevated land. Since elevated lands are separated from their surroundings, and secret meetings and whispered conversations are separated from those around them, it is called "najwa" (therefore, "najwa" includes any kind of confidential talk, whether it be whispering in the ear or a secret meeting).

The phrase "Khalasu najiyya," as many commentators have noted, is among the most eloquent and beautiful Quranic expressions, conveying in two words a wealth of meaning that would otherwise require several sentences to express.

In any case, "the eldest of them said in that private meeting: 'Do you not know that your father has taken a divine covenant from you that you would return Benjamin at any cost?'" (قالَ كَبِيرُهُمْ أَ لَمْ تَعْلَمُوا أَنَّ أَباكُمْ قَدْ أَخَذَ عَلَيْكُمْ مَوْثِقاً مِنَ اللَّهِ).

And "you are the same ones who previously fell short regarding Joseph" and tarnished your reputation with your father (وَ مِنْ قَبْلُ ما فَرَّطْتُمْ فِي يُوسُفَ) [1].

[1]: "Farrattum" (فرطتم) is from the root "tafrit," originally from "furut" (rhyming with shurut), meaning to precede. When it enters the form of Taf'il, it means falling short in preceding (negligence), whereas in the form of If'al (ifrat), it means excess and overstepping in preceding.

"Since this is the case, I will not move from my place (or from the land of Egypt)—and so to speak, I will take sanctuary here—until my father permits me, or Allah decrees for me, and He is the best of judges" (فَلَنْ أَبْرَحَ الْأَرْضَ حَتَّى يَأْذَنَ لِي أَبِي أَوْ يَحْكُمَ اللَّهُ لِي وَ هُوَ خَيْرُ الْحاكِمِينَ).

What is meant by this "decree" is either the decree of death—meaning I will not move from here until I die—or a way out that Allah may provide, or a justified excuse that would be definitively accepted by the father.

Then the eldest brother instructed the other brothers: "Return to your father and say: 'O our father! Indeed, your son has committed theft!'" (ارْجِعُوا إِلى‌ أَبِيكُمْ فَقُولُوا يا أَبانا إِنَّ ابْنَكَ سَرَقَ).

"And this testimony we give is only to the extent of what we know"—meaning only what we saw, that the King's measuring cup was removed from our brother's luggage, which indicated he had committed theft; however, the inner reality of the matter is with God (وَ ما شَهِدْنا إِلَّا بِما عَلِمْنَا).

"And we were not guardians of the unseen" (وَ ما كُنَّا لِلْغَيْبِ حافِظِينَ). There is also another possibility in the interpretation of this verse: that the brothers meant to tell their father that if they had testified and pledged to take the brother and bring him back, it was because they were unaware of his inner intentions and did not know the unseen—that his affair would eventually end up like this.

Then, to remove any suspicion from their father and to reassure him that the course of events was exactly as described, neither more nor less, they said: "For further investigation, ask the city in which we were" (وَ سْئَلِ الْقَرْيَةَ الَّتِي كُنَّا فِيها) [1] "and also the caravan with which we arrived, for there are naturally people from the land of Canaan and those whom you recognize among them; you can ask them the truth of the situation" (وَ الْعِيرَ الَّتِي أَقْبَلْنا فِيها) [2]. In any case, "be certain that we are truthful in our speech and say nothing but the truth" (وَ إِنَّا لَصادِقُونَ).

From the totality of this speech, it is understood that the issue of Benjamin's theft in Egypt was a public matter; a caravan from Canaan had come to that land, and from among them, one person intended to take the King's cup, but the King's officials arrived in time, seized the cup, and detained him. Perhaps the brothers saying "ask the land of Egypt" is an allusion to the fact that the matter became so famous that even the walls and doors know of it!

Key Points

1- Who was the eldest brother?

Some have said his name was Reuben (Rubil), some considered him to be "Simeon" (Sham'un), and others "Judah" (Yahuda). There is also discussion among commentators whether "eldest" refers to age or wisdom, but the apparent meaning of the verse is the eldest in terms of age.

[1]: "Qaryah" in the Arabic language does not mean "village," but rather refers to settlements and cities in general; here it refers to Egypt.

[2]: "Ir" (عير)—as Raghib stated in Al-Mufradat—refers to a group or company that takes camels and beasts of burden with them to carry food supplies; the entire group is called "Ir." Therefore, questioning them is entirely possible as it includes the people, and there is no need for an implied word (taqdir). Some commentators have said "Ir" only includes the beasts, in which case the verse would require an implied word, just as with "Qaryah" (i.e., "ask the people of the city/caravan").

2- Judging based on circumstantial evidence

It is indirectly inferred from this verse that a judge can act upon definitive circumstantial evidence (qara'in qat'iyyah), even if there is no confession or witnesses. In the incident of Joseph's brothers, there were neither witnesses nor a confession; only the discovery of the King's cup in Benjamin's luggage was considered proof of his guilt. This was especially so considering that each of them personally filled their own luggage, or at least was present during the filling, and if there were locks or ties, the keys were in their own possession. Furthermore, no one believed there was a plot involved, and the travelers from Canaan (Joseph's brothers) had no enemies in that city who would want to conspire against them.

The combination of these factors led to the conclusion, upon seeing the King's cup in Benjamin's luggage, that he had personally committed such an act.

This subject, which the modern world relies upon in its judgments, requires further investigation from the perspective of Islamic jurisprudence, as it is extremely influential in contemporary judicial discussions; the place for this discussion is in the Kitab al-Qada (Book of Judgment).

3- It appears from the above verses

That Joseph's brothers were very different from one another in terms of temperament. The eldest brother was strictly committed to his covenant and pledge, while the other brothers, as soon as they saw that their discussions with the Al-Aziz led nowhere, considered themselves excused and ceased further effort. Of course, the eldest brother was right, because by taking sanctuary in the city of Egypt, especially near the court of the Al-Aziz, there was hope that he might show grace and not punish a stranger—at the cost of grieving his brothers and elderly father—for a single cup that was eventually recovered. Therefore, because of this possibility, he remained in Egypt and sent the brothers to their father to explain the situation and seek further instructions.

Ayahs

قَالَ بَلْ سَوَّلَتْ لَكُمْ أَنفُسُكُمْ أَمْرًا ۖ فَصَبْرٌ جَمِيلٌ ۖ عَسَى ٱللَّهُ أَن يَأْتِيَنِى بِهِمْ جَمِيعًا ۚ إِنَّهُۥ هُوَ ٱلْعَلِيمُ ٱلْحَكِيمُ (٨٣)
He said, ‘No, your souls have made a matter seem decorous to you. Yet patience is graceful. Maybe Allah will bring them all [back] to me. Indeed He is the All-knowing, the All-wise.’ (83)
وَتَوَلَّىٰ عَنْهُمْ وَقَالَ يَـٰٓأَسَفَىٰ عَلَىٰ يُوسُفَ وَٱبْيَضَّتْ عَيْنَاهُ مِنَ ٱلْحُزْنِ فَهُوَ كَظِيمٌ (٨٤)
He turned away from them and said, ‘Alas for Joseph!’ His eyes had turned white with grief, and he choked with suppressed agony. (84)
قَالُوا۟ تَٱللَّهِ تَفْتَؤُا۟ تَذْكُرُ يُوسُفَ حَتَّىٰ تَكُونَ حَرَضًا أَوْ تَكُونَ مِنَ ٱلْهَـٰلِكِينَ (٨٥)
They said, ‘By Allah! You will go on remembering Joseph until you wreck your health or perish.’ (85)
قَالَ إِنَّمَآ أَشْكُوا۟ بَثِّى وَحُزْنِىٓ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ وَأَعْلَمُ مِنَ ٱللَّهِ مَا لَا تَعْلَمُونَ (٨٦)
He said, ‘I complain of my anguish and grief only to Allah. I know from Allah what you do not know.’ (86)

Commentary

I Know of Favors from Allah That You Do Not Know!

The brothers departed from Egypt, leaving both the eldest and the youngest brother behind. They returned to Canaan in a state of distress and misery and rushed to their father. When the father observed the signs of grief and sorrow on their faces upon their return from this journey—unlike the previous one—he realized they were bearing unpleasant news, especially since there was no sign of Benjamin or the eldest brother among them. When the brothers recounted the events exactly as they had happened, Jacob (Ya'qub) became agitated. Turning to them, he said: "Your inner selves have enticed you to something and made it seem fair in your eyes!" (قَالَ بَلْ سَوَّلَتْ لَكُمْ أَنْفُسُكُمْ أَمْراً).

In fact, he spoke the exact same sentence to them that he had uttered after the incident of Joseph (Yusuf), when they presented that false narrative.

Here, a question arises: Did Jacob suspect them solely because of their past misconduct, becoming certain they were lying and that a conspiracy was afoot? Such a thing seems unlikely not only for a Prophet like Jacob but even for ordinary people—to definitively accuse someone based solely on one past misdeed, especially when the other party has brought witnesses and the path to investigation is not closed.

Or was the purpose of this sentence to express other points, such as:

  1. Why did you surrender to the idea that he had stolen just by seeing the king’s measuring cup in your brother’s bag, when this alone cannot be logical proof?
  2. Why did you tell the 'Aziz of Egypt that the punishment for a thief is to be taken as a slave, when this is not a Divine law but rather an incorrect custom among the people of Canaan? (This is assuming we do not consider this law to be part of Jacob’s Sharia, contrary to what some commentators have said).
  3. Why did you surrender so quickly to this event and not show resistance like your eldest brother, despite having given me a solemn Divine pledge? [^1]

Then Jacob turned inward and said: I shall not lose the reins of patience; "So, beautiful patience [is my course]" (فَصَبْرٌ جَمِيلٌ) [^2]. "I hope that Allah will bring them all back to me (Joseph, Benjamin, and my eldest son)" (عَسَى اللَّهُ أَنْ يَأْتِيَنِي بِهِمْ جَمِيعاً).

For I know that "He is All-Knowing of what is in the hearts and aware of all events that have passed and are passing; furthermore, He is All-Wise and does nothing without purpose." (إِنَّهُ هُوَ الْعَلِيمُ الْحَكِيمُ).

At this moment, grief and sorrow overwhelmed Jacob’s entire being. The empty place of Benjamin—the very son who was his source of consolation—reminded him of his beloved Joseph. He remembered the times when that upright, faithful, intelligent, and beautiful son was in his arms, and how inhaling his scent provided the father with a fresh lease on life at every moment. But today, not only is there no trace of him, but his successor, Benjamin, has also met a painful and uncertain fate similar to his. "At this point, he turned away from them and said: 'Alas, my grief for Joseph!'" (وَ تَوَلَّى عَنْهُمْ وَ قَالَ يَا أَسَفَى عَلَى يُوسُفَ).

The brothers, who felt ashamed before their father regarding the incident of Benjamin, fell into deep thought upon hearing Joseph’s name, and the sweat of shame appeared on their brows.

This doubled sorrow caused a flood of tears to flow involuntarily from Jacob’s eyes, to the extent that "his eyes turned white and he became blind from grief" (وَ ابْيَضَّتْ عَيْنَاهُ مِنَ الْحُزْنِ).

Yet, despite this, he tried to control himself, suppress his anger, and say nothing contrary to the pleasure of the Truth (Allah). "He was a man of great endurance and one who suppressed his grief" (فَهُوَ كَظِيمٌ).

The apparent meaning of the verse is that Jacob had not been blind until that time; rather, this doubled grief and the continuous weeping and shedding of tears took away his sight. As we have previously mentioned, this was an involuntary matter that does not conflict with "beautiful patience." [^1]

The brothers, who were deeply troubled by the sum of these events, felt their consciences tormented by the story of Joseph on one hand, saw themselves on the verge of a new trial regarding Benjamin on the other, and found their father’s doubled anxiety heavy and burdensome. With distress and impatience, they said to their father: "By Allah, you will keep remembering Joseph until you become terminally ill or you perish!" (قَالُوا تَاللَّهِ تَفْتَؤُا تَذْكُرُ يُوسُفَ حَتَّى تَكُونَ حَرَضاً أَوْ تَكُونَ مِنَ الْهَالِكِينَ) [^2].

But the Elder of Canaan, that enlightened Prophet, replied to them: "I did not bring my complaint to you that you should speak thus; I take my grief and sorrow to Allah and complain to Him" (قَالَ إِنَّمَا أَشْكُوا بَثِّي وَ حُزْنِي إِلَى اللَّهِ) [^1]. "And I know from my God favors, kindnesses, and things that you do not know" (وَ أَعْلَمُ مِنَ اللَّهِ مَا لا تَعْلَمُونَ).

[^1]: The possibility suggested by some that this sentence refers to the original incident of Joseph is unlikely, as the issue of separating Joseph from his parents is not discussed at all in the verses above. [^2]: Regarding "beautiful patience" (Sabrun Jamil), refer to the commentary on verse 18 of this same Surah. [^1]: For further explanation, refer to the commentary on the [earlier] verse of this Surah. [^2]: Harad (on the pattern of marad) means something corrupt and distressing; here it means ill, frail, thin, and on the verge of death. [^1]: Bath means scattering, and something that cannot be concealed; here it means manifest sorrow and visible distraction of the mind.

Ayahs

يَـٰبَنِىَّ ٱذْهَبُوا۟ فَتَحَسَّسُوا۟ مِن يُوسُفَ وَأَخِيهِ وَلَا تَا۟يْـَٔسُوا۟ مِن رَّوْحِ ٱللَّهِ ۖ إِنَّهُۥ لَا يَا۟يْـَٔسُ مِن رَّوْحِ ٱللَّهِ إِلَّا ٱلْقَوْمُ ٱلْكَـٰفِرُونَ (٨٧)
‘Go, my sons, and look for Joseph and his brother, and do not despair of Allah’s mercy. Indeed no one despairs of Allah’s mercy except the faithless lot.’ (87)
فَلَمَّا دَخَلُوا۟ عَلَيْهِ قَالُوا۟ يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلْعَزِيزُ مَسَّنَا وَأَهْلَنَا ٱلضُّرُّ وَجِئْنَا بِبِضَـٰعَةٍ مُّزْجَىٰةٍ فَأَوْفِ لَنَا ٱلْكَيْلَ وَتَصَدَّقْ عَلَيْنَآ ۖ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَجْزِى ٱلْمُتَصَدِّقِينَ (٨٨)
Then, when they entered into his presence, they said, ‘O emir! Distress has befallen us and our family, and we have brought [just] a meagre sum. Yet grant us the full measure, and be charitable to us! Indeed Allah rewards the charitable.’ (88)
قَالَ هَلْ عَلِمْتُم مَّا فَعَلْتُم بِيُوسُفَ وَأَخِيهِ إِذْ أَنتُمْ جَـٰهِلُونَ (٨٩)
He said, ‘Have you realized what you did to Joseph and his brother, when you were senseless?’ (89)
قَالُوٓا۟ أَءِنَّكَ لَأَنتَ يُوسُفُ ۖ قَالَ أَنَا۠ يُوسُفُ وَهَـٰذَآ أَخِى ۖ قَدْ مَنَّ ٱللَّهُ عَلَيْنَآ ۖ إِنَّهُۥ مَن يَتَّقِ وَيَصْبِرْ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يُضِيعُ أَجْرَ ٱلْمُحْسِنِينَ (٩٠)
