They said, ‘Are you really Joseph?!’ He said, ‘I am Joseph, and this is my brother. Certainly Allah has shown us favour. Indeed if one is Godwary and patient Allah does not waste the reward of the virtuous.’ (90)
قَالُوا۟ تَٱللَّهِ لَقَدْ ءَاثَرَكَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَيْنَا وَإِن كُنَّا لَخَـٰطِـِٔينَ (٩١)
They said, ‘By Allah, Allah has certainly preferred you over us, and we have indeed been erring.’ (91)
قَالَ لَا تَثْرِيبَ عَلَيْكُمُ ٱلْيَوْمَ ۖ يَغْفِرُ ٱللَّهُ لَكُمْ ۖ وَهُوَ أَرْحَمُ ٱلرَّٰحِمِينَ (٩٢)
He said, ‘There shall be no reproach on you today. Allah will forgive you, and He is the most merciful of the merciful. (92)
ٱذْهَبُوا۟ بِقَمِيصِى هَـٰذَا فَأَلْقُوهُ عَلَىٰ وَجْهِ أَبِى يَأْتِ بَصِيرًا وَأْتُونِى بِأَهْلِكُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ (٩٣)
Take this shirt of mine, and cast it upon my father’s face; he will regain his sight, and bring me all your folks.’ (93)

Commentary

Strive and Do Not Despair, for Despair is a Sign of Disbelief!

Famine was raging in Egypt and its surroundings, including Canaan. Food supplies were completely exhausted. Once again, Ya'qub (Jacob) commanded his sons to set out for Egypt to secure provisions. However, this time, at the forefront of his requests, he placed the search for Yusuf (Joseph) and his brother Benjamin, saying: "O my sons, go and find out about Yusuf and his brother" (يَا بَنِيَّ اذْهَبُوا فَتَحَسَّسُوا مِنْ يُوسُفَ وَأَخِيهِ).

Since the sons were almost certain that Yusuf was no longer alive and were surprised by their father's recommendation and emphasis, Ya'qub reminded them: "Never despair of Allah's mercy," for His power is above all problems and hardships (وَلَا تَيْأَسُوا مِنْ رَوْحِ اللَّهِ).

"For none despairs of Allah's mercy except the disbelieving people who are unaware of His power" (إِنَّهُ لَا يَيْأَسُ مِنْ رَوْحِ اللَّهِ إِلَّا الْقَوْمُ الْكَافِرُونَ).

"Tahassus" (تحسس) is derived from the root "Hiss" (حس), meaning to seek something through the senses. There is discussion among commentators and linguists regarding its difference from "Tajassus" (تجسس): It is narrated from Ibn Abbas that "Tahassus" is used for seeking good matters, while "Tajassus" is for seeking evil matters. Others have said that Tahassus is the effort to hear the stories of individuals and peoples, while Tajassus is the effort to seek out faults.

Some consider both to have the same meaning, but in light of the Hadith that says: "Do not perform Tajassus and do not perform Tahassus" (لا تجسسوا و لا تحسسوا), it becomes clear that these two are different. Ibn Abbas's view on the distinction between the two seems appropriate for the meaning of the verses under discussion. If we see that the Hadith prohibits both, it may indicate that one should not pry into people's affairs, whether for good or for evil.

"Rawh" (روح) means mercy, comfort, relief, and the opening of a matter.

Al-Raghib says in Al-Mufradat: "Rawh" (with the vowel fatha) and "Ruh" (with the vowel damma) both originally have the same meaning, referring to "soul" and "breath." Subsequently, "Rawh" came to mean mercy and relief (because whenever problems are resolved, a new soul and life are felt by the human, and they breathe freely).

In any case, the sons of Ya'qub packed their belongings and set out for Egypt. This was the third time they entered this eventful land. In this journey, unlike previous ones, a sense of shame tormented their souls, for their reputation in Egypt and with the Al-Aziz had been severely damaged; they had become ill-famed, and perhaps some recognized them as the "group of thieves from Canaan." On the other hand, they carried no significant goods to exchange for wheat and other food supplies. The loss of the second brother, Benjamin, and their father's extreme distress added to their problems; indeed, the knife had reached the bone. The only thing among this heap of problems and soul-crushing distress that brought them solace was their father's final sentence: "Do not despair of Allah's mercy, for every difficulty is easy for Him."

"They entered upon Yusuf, and at this moment, with utmost distress, they turned to him and said: 'O Aziz! Adversity and famine have touched us and our family'" (فَلَمَّا دَخَلُوا عَلَيْهِ قَالُوا يَا أَيُّهَا الْعَزِيزُ مَسَّنَا وَأَهْلَنَا الضُّرُّ).

"And we have brought only a scant and meager sum of goods" (وَجِئْنَا بِبِضَاعَةٍ مُزْجَاةٍ) [1]. Yet, despite this, we rely on your generosity and nobility, "and we expect that you fulfill for us the full measure" (فَأَوْفِ لَنَا الْكَيْلَ).

"And be charitable to us and bestow a favor upon us" (وَتَصَدَّقْ عَلَيْنَا).

And do not take your reward from us, but rather from your God, for "Indeed, Allah rewards the charitable and the generous" (إِنَّ اللَّهَ يَجْزِي الْمُتَصَدِّقِينَ).

It is interesting that Yusuf's brothers, despite their father's emphasis to search for Yusuf and his brother—with food supplies being secondary—did not pay much attention to that instruction. First, they requested food from the Aziz of Egypt. Perhaps this was because they had little hope of finding Yusuf, or because they thought it better to present themselves in the natural guise of food buyers, keeping the request to release their brother in the background to have a greater impact on the Aziz.

[1]: "Bida'ah" (بضاعت) is from the root "Bad'" (بضع), meaning a piece of meat cut from the body; it is also applied to a portion of wealth set aside as the price for a commodity. "Muzjah" (مزجاة) is from the root "Izja'" (ازجاء), meaning to drive away or push back. Since a small and worthless price is pushed away by the receiver, it is called Muzjah.

Some have said: The meaning of "be charitable to us" (تَصَدَّقْ عَلَيْنَا) was the release of the brother; otherwise, regarding food supplies, their intention was not to receive goods without compensation such that it would be called Sadaqah (charity).

We also read in narrations that the brothers carried a letter from their father to the Aziz of Egypt. In that letter, Ya'qub, while praising the justice, fairness, and kindness of the Aziz toward his family, and then introducing himself and his household of prophethood, described his distress over the loss of his son Yusuf and his other son Benjamin, as well as the hardships resulting from the drought.

At the end of the letter, he requested the release of Benjamin and emphasized that "we are a family in whom theft and the like have never existed and never will."

When the brothers handed their father's letter to the Aziz, he took the letter, kissed it, placed it upon his eyes, and wept so much that teardrops fell upon his shirt [1]. (This very act plunged the brothers into wonder and thought: what affection does the Aziz of Egypt have for their father Ya'qub that his letter creates such emotion in him? Perhaps it was at this point that a spark struck their hearts: could he himself be Yusuf? Likewise, perhaps this letter made Yusuf so restless that he could no longer remain hidden behind the mask and veil of the Aziz of Egypt, and soon, as we shall see, he introduced himself as that very brother to his brothers).

At this moment, when the period of trial had ended and Yusuf appeared deeply restless and distressed, he began his introduction from here. He turned to his brothers and "said: Do you know what you did to Yusuf and his brother when you were ignorant?" (قَالَ هَلْ عَلِمْتُمْ مَا فَعَلْتُمْ بِيُوسُفَ وَأَخِيهِ إِذْ أَنْتُمْ جَاهِلُونَ). Observe Yusuf's nobility: first, he mentions their sin in a general way, saying "what you did" (ما فعلتم), and second, he shows them the way to apologize by saying these acts were due to ignorance, and that period of ignorance has passed, and now you are wise and understanding!

Furthermore, it becomes clear from this statement that in the past, they did not only inflict that calamity upon Yusuf, but the other brother, Benjamin, was also not safe from their harm during that period, and they had caused distress for him as well. Perhaps Benjamin, during the time he stayed with Yusuf in Egypt, had described some of their injustices to his brother.

In some narrations, we read that Yusuf, after saying this sentence, ended his speech with a smile so that they would not become too distressed or imagine that the Aziz of Egypt was seeking revenge. This smile caused Yusuf's beautiful teeth to be fully revealed to the brothers. When they looked closely, they saw an amazing resemblance to the teeth of their brother Yusuf [1]!

All these factors combined: on one hand, they see the Aziz of Egypt speaking of Yusuf and the calamities the brothers inflicted upon him—which no one knew of except them and Yusuf. On the other hand, Ya'qub's letter moves him so much as if he has the closest relationship with him.

[1]: Majma' al-Bayan, under the verse.

And thirdly, the more they scrutinized his appearance and face, the more they saw his resemblance to their brother Yusuf. Yet, at the same time, they could not believe that Yusuf was sitting upon the throne of the Aziz of Egypt. Where was he, and where was this place?! Therefore, in a tone mixed with doubt, "they said: Are you indeed Yusuf?"

(قَالُوا أَإِنَّكَ لَأَنْتَ يُوسُفُ). At this point, extremely sensitive moments passed for the brothers. They did not know exactly what the Aziz of Egypt would say in response to their question! Would he truly pull back the curtain and introduce himself, or would he call them madmen for bringing up something so ridiculous?

The moments passed quickly, and an agonizing anticipation weighed heavily on the brothers' hearts. But Yusuf did not let this time last long; suddenly, he pulled the veil from the face of truth. "He said: Yes, I am Yusuf! And this is my brother Benjamin!" (قَالَ أَنَا يُوسُفُ وَهَذَا أَخِي).

However, to give thanks for Allah's blessing—who had bestowed all these favors upon him—and to simultaneously give a great lesson to his brothers, he added: "Allah has indeed been gracious to us. Indeed, whoever fears Allah and is patient, then surely Allah does not waste the reward of the doers of good" (قَدْ مَنَّ اللَّهُ عَلَيْنَا إِنَّهُ مَنْ يَتَّقِ وَيَصْبِرْ فَإِنَّ اللَّهَ لَا يُضِيعُ أَجْرَ الْمُحْسِنِينَ).

No one knows what happened in these sensitive moments, or what excitement and clamor these brothers raised after decades of not recognizing one another—how they embraced each other and how tears of joy flowed. Yet, despite this, the brothers, who felt deeply ashamed, could not look directly at Yusuf's face.

They were waiting to see if their great sin was forgivable and could be overlooked or not. Therefore, they turned to their brother and said:

"By Allah, Allah has certainly preferred you over us" and granted you virtue in terms of knowledge, forbearance, wisdom, and authority (قَالُوا تَاللَّهِ لَقَدْ آثَرَكَ اللَّهُ عَلَيْنَا) [1], "even though we were indeed sinners and wrongdoers" (وَإِنْ كُنَّا لَخَاطِئِينَ) [2].

But Yusuf, who was not willing for this state of shame to continue for his brothers—especially at the time of his victory—or for the thought to possibly cross their minds that Yusuf might seek revenge here, immediately gave them security and peace of mind with this sentence: "He said: No blame shall be upon you today" (قَالَ لَا تَثْرِيبَ عَلَيْكُمُ الْيَوْمَ) [3]. Let your minds be at ease, your consciences be at rest, and do not let grief or sorrow from the past enter your hearts. Then, to remind them that not only has his right been forgiven, but the Divine right is also forgivable in this regard with this regret and repentance, "he added: Allah will forgive you, for He is the Most Merciful of the merciful" (يَغْفِرُ اللَّهُ لَكُمْ وَهُوَ أَرْحَمُ الرَّاحِمِينَ).

This is evidence of Yusuf's ultimate nobility; he not only waived his own right and was unwilling to permit even the slightest reproach or blame—let alone punishment—against his brothers, but he also assured them regarding Allah's right that Allah is Forgiving and Merciful. He even argued for this statement by saying that He is the Most Merciful of the merciful.

At this point, another grief weighed heavily on the brothers' hearts: that their father had become blind due to the separation from his children, and the continuation of this state was a soul-crushing pain for the whole family. Moreover, it was a constant proof and witness of their crime. To solve this great problem, Yusuf said:

"Take this shirt of mine and cast it over my father's face; he will regain his sight" (اذْهَبُوا بِقَمِيصِي هَذَا فَأَلْقُوهُ عَلَى وَجْهِ أَبِي يَأْتِ بَصِيراً).

"And then come to me with all your family" (وَأْتُونِي بِأَهْلِكُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ).

Key Points

1- Who Should Carry Yusuf's Shirt?

In some narrations, it is mentioned that Yusuf said: "The one who carries my healing shirt to my father must be the same one who brought the blood-stained shirt to him, so that just as he made the father sad, this time he may make him happy and joyful!" Therefore, this task was entrusted to "Judah," for he said: "I was the one who took the bloody shirt to my father and said your son was eaten by a wolf." This shows that Yusuf, despite all the troubles he had, did not neglect the details and subtleties of moral issues [1].

2- Yusuf's Nobility

In some other narrations, it is mentioned that Yusuf's brothers were constantly ashamed after this event. They sent one of them to him, saying: "You seat us at your table every morning and evening, and we feel embarrassed before you because we committed such audacity." To ensure they did not feel the slightest sense of shame, but rather felt their presence at his table as a service to him, Yusuf gave a very interesting answer. He said: "The people of Egypt until now looked at me with the eye of a purchased slave, and said to one another: 'Glory be to Him who brought a slave sold for twenty dirhams to such a status!'"

"But now that you have come and the record of my life has been opened to these people, they understand I was not a slave; I am from the house of prophethood and from the children of Ibrahim the Friend (Khalil), and this is a source of pride and honor for me!" [2]

3- The Gratitude for Victory

The above verses teach us this important moral lesson and Islamic instruction in the clearest way: at the time of victory over an enemy, do not be vengeful or malicious.

Yusuf's brothers had struck the hardest blows against Yusuf and brought him to the brink of death; if Allah's grace had not included him, escape would not have been possible. They not only tormented Yusuf but also severely tortured his father.

[1]: Majma' al-Bayan, under the verses discussed. [2]: Tafsir Fakhr Razi, Vol. 18, p. 206.

But now they all stand weak and wretched before him, and all power is in his hands. Yet, from between Yusuf's words, it is clearly felt that he not only held no grudge in his heart, but it pained him that the brothers might remember the past, become upset, and feel ashamed! For this reason, he exerts the utmost effort to drive this feeling out of their souls. Even beyond this, he wants to make them understand that "your coming to Egypt is a source of pride for me, as it is a means for my greater recognition in this land—that I am from the house of the Message, not a Canaanite slave sold for a few dirhams." He wants them to feel that they are not only not in debt, but are actually owed something!

It is noteworthy that when the Prophet of Islam ﷺ was in a similar situation during the Conquest of Mecca and triumphed over bloodthirsty enemies—namely the leaders of polytheism and idolatry—according to Ibn Abbas, he came to the side of the Kaaba and took hold of the door handle while the opponents had taken refuge in the Kaaba, waiting to see what command the Prophet of Islam ﷺ would issue regarding them.

Here, the Prophet ﷺ said: "Praise be to Allah, who fulfilled His promise, helped His servant, and defeated the confederates alone" (الحمد لله الذي صدق وعده و نصر عبده و هزم الاحزاب وحده). Then he turned to the people and said:

"What do you think, O assembly of Quraysh? They said: Good, a noble brother, and the son of a noble brother, and you have gained power! He said: And I say as my brother Yusuf said: 'No blame shall be upon you today!'" (ما ذا تظنون يا معشر قريش قالوا خيرا، اخ كريم، و ابن اخ كريم و قد قدرت! قال و انا اقول كما قال اخى يوسف لا تَثْرِيبَ عَلَيْكُمُ الْيَوْمَ).

"What do you suppose, O people of Quraysh, that I shall decree concerning you? They replied: We expect nothing from you but good and kindness; you are a noble and generous brother and the son of our noble brother, and now power is in your hands. The Prophet said: And I say concerning you the same as my brother Yusuf said concerning his brothers at the time of victory: 'No blame shall be upon you today!'—today is not a day of reproach, blame, or rebuke!" Umar says: "At this moment, the sweat of shame flowed from my face, for upon entering Mecca, I had said to them: 'Today is the day we shall take revenge on you.' When the Prophet ﷺ spoke this sentence, I became ashamed of my own words" [1]. In Islamic narrations, we also repeatedly read that: "The Zakat (alms) of victory is forgiveness and pardon."

Ali عليه السلام says: "When you gain power over your enemy, make forgiveness for him the gratitude for the power over him" (اذا قدرت على عدوك فاجعل العفو عنه شكرا للقدرة عليه) [2].

[1]: Tafsir Qurtubi, Vol. 5, p. 3487. [2]: Nahj al-Balagha, Short Sayings, No. 11.

Ayahs

وَلَمَّا فَصَلَتِ ٱلْعِيرُ قَالَ أَبُوهُمْ إِنِّى لَأَجِدُ رِيحَ يُوسُفَ ۖ لَوْلَآ أَن تُفَنِّدُونِ (٩٤)
As the caravan set off, their father said, ‘I sense the scent of Joseph, if you will not consider me a dotard.’ (94)
قَالُوا۟ تَٱللَّهِ إِنَّكَ لَفِى ضَلَـٰلِكَ ٱلْقَدِيمِ (٩٥)
They said, ‘By God, you persist in your inveterate error.’ (95)
فَلَمَّآ أَن جَآءَ ٱلْبَشِيرُ أَلْقَىٰهُ عَلَىٰ وَجْهِهِۦ فَٱرْتَدَّ بَصِيرًا ۖ قَالَ أَلَمْ أَقُل لَّكُمْ إِنِّىٓ أَعْلَمُ مِنَ ٱللَّهِ مَا لَا تَعْلَمُونَ (٩٦)
When the bearer of good news arrived, he cast it on his face, and he regained his sight. He said, ‘Did I not tell you, ‘‘I know from Allah what you do not know?’’ ’ (96)
قَالُوا۟ يَـٰٓأَبَانَا ٱسْتَغْفِرْ لَنَا ذُنُوبَنَآ إِنَّا كُنَّا خَـٰطِـِٔينَ (٩٧)
They said, ‘Father! Plead [with Allah] for forgiveness of our sins! We have indeed been erring.’ (97)
قَالَ سَوْفَ أَسْتَغْفِرُ لَكُمْ رَبِّىٓ ۖ إِنَّهُۥ هُوَ ٱلْغَفُورُ ٱلرَّحِيمُ (٩٨)
He said, ‘I shall plead with my Lord to forgive you; indeed He is the All-forgiving, the All-merciful.’ (98)

Commentary

Finally, God’s Grace Accomplished Its Task

The sons of Ya’qub (Jacob), overflowing with a joy they could hardly contain, took Yusuf’s (Joseph’s) shirt and departed from Egypt with the caravan. While these brothers were experiencing one of the sweetest moments of their lives, back in the land of Sham and Canaan, in the house of the aged Ya’qub, the dust of sorrow, grief, and mourning still sat upon every face.

A depressed, mourning, and grief-stricken family was passing through painful moments.

However, simultaneously with the caravan’s departure from Egypt, an event suddenly occurred in the house of Ya’qub that plunged everyone into shock and amazement. Ya’qub stirred and, with complete certainty and hope, called out: "If you do not begin to speak ill of me and do not attribute to me senility, ignorance, or falsehood, I tell you: I smell the scent of my dear Yusuf." I feel that the era of grief and affliction will soon end, and the time of union and victory arrives. The household of Ya’qub shall cast off the garments of mourning and shroud themselves in the robes of joy and delight—though I do not think you will believe these words (وَ لَمَّا فَصَلَتِ الْعِيرُ قالَ أَبُوهُمْ إِنِّي لَأَجِدُ رِيحَ يُوسُفَ لَوْ لا أَنْ تُفَنِّدُونِ‌) [1]. From the word "fasalat" (departed), it is understood that Ya’qub experienced this sensation the very moment the caravan moved out of Egypt.

Those around Ya’qub, who were presumably his grandchildren, the wives of his sons, and the like, turned to him with utter amazement and audacity, saying with certainty: "By Allah, you are indeed in your old wandering!" (قالُوا تَاللَّهِ إِنَّكَ لَفِي ضَلالِكَ الْقَدِيمِ‌). What wandering is greater than this? Many years have passed since Yusuf’s death, yet you still think he is alive, and now you say you smell Yusuf’s scent from Egypt?!

Where is Egypt and where is Sham and Canaan?! Is this not proof that you are constantly immersed in a world of fantasies and perceive your imaginations as reality? What strange thing is this you say?! But this "wandering" is nothing new; previously you also told your sons: "Go to Egypt and search for my Yusuf!"

From this, it becomes clear that by "dalalat" (wandering/error), they did not mean error in creed, but rather error in judging matters related to Yusuf. Regardless, these expressions show with what harshness and boldness they treated this great, aged, and enlightened Prophet. In one instance they said: "Our father is in manifest error," and here they said: "You are in your old wandering."

They were unaware of the purity of heart and the inner light of the Old Man of Canaan; they considered his heart as dark as their own and did not imagine that future events from far and near could be reflected in the mirror of his heart.

Several days and nights passed, and Ya’qub remained in anticipation—a soul-searing anticipation in whose depths waves of joy, peace, and certainty surged, while those around him remained indifferent to such matters, essentially considering the saga of Yusuf closed forever.

After several days and nights—and it is unknown how they passed for Ya’qub—one day a cry arose: "Come! The caravan of Canaan has arrived from Egypt." Unlike before, the sons of Ya’qub entered the city happy and laughing, rushing to their father’s house. Before everyone else, the "bashir" (the bearer of glad tidings of union and the carrier of Yusuf’s shirt) came to the aged Ya’qub and cast the shirt upon his face. Ya’qub, whose dim eyes lacked the power to see the shirt, felt only this much: that a familiar scent reached the nostrils of his soul. In a golden, joyous moment, he felt every atom of his being light up. Heaven and earth seemed to smile; the breeze of mercy blew, swirling the dust of sorrow and carrying it away. The very doors and walls seemed to shout with joy, and Ya’qub smiled with them. A strange excitement overwhelmed the old man. Suddenly he felt his eyes brighten; he could see everywhere, and the world with its beauties stood once more before his eyes, just as the Quran says: "So when the bearer of glad tidings came, he cast it (the shirt) upon his face, and he regained his sight!" (فَلَمَّا أَنْ جاءَ الْبَشِيرُ أَلْقاهُ عَلى‌ وَجْهِهِ فَارْتَدَّ بَصِيراً).

The brothers and those around them shed tears of longing and joy, and Ya’qub said to them in a decisive tone: "Did I not say to you that I know from Allah that which you do not know?" (قالَ أَ لَمْ أَقُلْ لَكُمْ إِنِّي أَعْلَمُ مِنَ اللَّهِ ما لا تَعْلَمُونَ‌).

This astonishing miracle plunged the brothers deep into thought. For a moment, they reflected on their dark past—a past filled with errors, sins, mistakes, and narrow-mindedness. But how good it is when a person realizes their mistake and immediately thinks of reform and compensation, just as the sons of Ya’qub did. They reached out to their father and "said: O our father! Ask forgiveness for our sins for us" (قالُوا يا أَبانَا اسْتَغْفِرْ لَنا ذُنُوبَنا). "For indeed we were sinners" (إِنَّا كُنَّا خاطِئِينَ‌).

The noble old man, who possessed a soul as vast and capacious as an ocean, without reproaching or blaming them, "promised them: I will soon ask forgiveness for you from my Lord" (قالَ سَوْفَ أَسْتَغْفِرُ لَكُمْ رَبِّي‌). And I hope He accepts your repentance and overlooks your sins, "for He is the Forgiving, the Merciful" (إِنَّهُ هُوَ الْغَفُورُ الرَّحِيمُ‌).

Key Points

1- How did Ya’qub sense the scent of Yusuf’s shirt?

This is a question raised by many commentators, usually categorized as a miracle and a supernatural event for Ya’qub or Yusuf. However, given that the Quran remains silent on this aspect and does not explicitly label it as a miracle or otherwise, a scientific explanation can also be found.

Today, the issue of "telepathy"—the transfer of thought from distant points—is an established scientific matter that occurs between individuals who have a close bond or possess extraordinary spiritual power.

Perhaps many of us have encountered this in our daily lives: a mother or brother suddenly feels an extraordinary, unexplained distress, and shortly after, news arrives that a tragic event has happened to their child or sibling in a distant location.

Scientists explain this type of sensation through telepathy and the transfer of thought from afar.

In the story of Ya’qub, it is possible that his exceptionally strong bond with Yusuf and the greatness of his soul caused him to absorb the sensation felt by the brothers carrying Yusuf’s shirt from that great distance into his own mind.

Of course, it is also entirely possible that this matter relates to the vast scope of the knowledge of the Prophets.

In some narrations, there is an interesting reference to the issue of thought transfer. Someone asked Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام: "Sometimes I become sorrowful without any calamity reaching me or any unpleasant event occurring, to the extent that my family and friends see it in my face." He replied: "Yes, Allah created the believers from a single heavenly essence and breathed into them from His Spirit; therefore, believers are brothers to one another. When a calamity reaches one of these brothers in one of the cities, it affects the others." [1] Some narrations also suggest that this shirt was not an ordinary one, but a heavenly shirt inherited from Ibrahim (Abraham) the Friend of God within Ya’qub’s family, and someone with a heavenly sense of smell like Ya’qub could sense the scent of this heavenly shirt from afar. [2]

2- The Variation in the States of the Prophets

There is another famous objection here, reflected in Persian poetry, where someone said to Ya’qub: From Egypt you smelled the scent of his shirt, Why did you not see him in the well of Canaan?

How is it possible for this great Prophet to sense the scent of Yusuf’s shirt from such a distance—which some record as eighty leagues and others as a ten-day journey—yet remain unaware of events happening right next to him in the land of Canaan when Yusuf was thrown into the well?

The answer to this question is not very complex, considering what we have previously stated regarding the "Knowledge of the Unseen" (Ghayb) and the limits of the knowledge of Prophets and Imams. Their knowledge of unseen matters is dependent on the knowledge and will of the Lord; where Allah wills, they know, even if it concerns the closest points in the world.

In this regard, they can be likened to travelers passing through a desert on a dark, gloomy night under a cloud-covered sky. For a moment, lightning flashes in the sky, illuminating the depths of the desert, and everything becomes clear before the eyes of these travelers. But a moment later, it is extinguished, and darkness covers everything such that nothing can be seen.

Perhaps the Hadith narrated from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام regarding the Imam’s knowledge also points to this meaning, where he says:

"Allah has placed between Himself and the Imam a pillar of light through which Allah looks at the Imam and the Imam looks toward Him. When he wishes to know something, he looks into that light and knows it." [1] Sa’di’s famous poem following the aforementioned verse also refers to this explanation and such narrations: He said: Our state is like the flashing lightning, One moment manifest, the next hidden. At times we sit upon the highest throne, At times we see not even the back of our own feet. (The word "jahan" here means "leaping," and "barq-e jahan" means the leaping lightning of the sky).

Given this reality, it is no surprise that one day, by Divine Will to test Ya’qub, he remained unaware of the events of Canaan happening near him, and another day, when the era of affliction and testing ended, he sensed the scent of the shirt from Egypt.

3- How did Ya’qub regain his sight?

Some commentators have suggested that Ya’qub had not lost his eyesight entirely but that his eyes had become weak, and upon the arrival of the precursors of union, such an upheaval and excitement overcame him that he returned to his original state. However, the apparent meaning of the Quranic verses indicates that he had become completely blind and even his eyes had turned white. Therefore, the return of his sight occurred through a miracle. The Quran says: "He regained his sight" (فَارْتَدَّ بَصِيراً).

4- The Promise of Seeking Forgiveness

In the verses above, we read that Yusuf, in response to his brothers' expression of remorse, said: "May Allah forgive you" (يَغْفِرُ اللَّهُ لَكُمْ). But Ya’qub, when they confessed their sin to him, expressed remorse, and requested him to seek forgiveness, said: "I will soon ask forgiveness for you." As stated in narrations, his goal was to delay fulfilling this request until the dawn of Friday night, which is a more suitable time for the answering of prayers and the acceptance of repentance. [1] Now the question arises: why did Yusuf answer them definitively, while the father deferred it to the future?

This difference might be because Yusuf was speaking of the possibility of forgiveness and that this sin is forgivable, whereas Ya’qub was discussing its actualization and what must be done for this forgiveness to be realized (reflect on this).

5- Tawassul (Intercession) is Permissible

From the verses above, it is understood that requesting another to seek forgiveness is not only not inconsistent with Monotheism (Tawhid) but is a way to reach the grace of the Lord. Otherwise, how could the Prophet Ya’qub accept his sons' request to seek forgiveness for them and respond positively to their tawassul?

This shows that seeking the intercession of the friends of Allah (Awliya) is generally a permissible matter, and those who deem it forbidden and contrary to the principle of Monotheism are either unaware of the Quranic texts or are blinded by misguided prejudices.

6- The End of the Dark Night...

The great lesson these verses teach us is that no matter how hard and painful problems and events may be, no matter how limited and inadequate apparent causes and means become, and no matter how much victory and relief are delayed—none of these can prevent hope in the grace of the Lord. The same God who brightens a blind eye with a shirt, transfers the scent of a shirt from a great distance, and returns a lost loved one after many years, also places a balm on hearts wounded by separation and heals agonizing pains.

Yes, within this history and narrative lies this great lesson of Monotheism and the recognition of God: that nothing is difficult or complicated before the Will of Allah.

[^1]: (From page 1): Tafannudun is from the root fand (rhyming with namad), meaning weakness of thought and senility; some have considered it to mean lying. Originally it means corruption. Therefore, the phrase "law la tufannidun" means: "If you do not call me senile and of corrupt intellect..." [^1]: (From page 3): Usul al-Kafi, Vol. 2, p. 133. [^2]: (From page 3): For more information on these narrations, refer to Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 2, p. 464. [^1]: (From page 4): Sharh Nahj al-Balagha by Khoei, Vol. 5, p. 200. [^1]: (From page 5): In Tafsir al-Qurtubi, we read that his goal was to seek forgiveness for them on a Friday night that coincided with the day of Ashura! (Tafsir al-Qurtubi, Vol. 6, p. 3491).

Ayahs

فَلَمَّا دَخَلُوا۟ عَلَىٰ يُوسُفَ ءَاوَىٰٓ إِلَيْهِ أَبَوَيْهِ وَقَالَ ٱدْخُلُوا۟ مِصْرَ إِن شَآءَ ٱللَّهُ ءَامِنِينَ (٩٩)
When they entered into the presence of Joseph, he set his parents close to himself, and said, ‘Welcome to Egypt, in safety, God willing!’ (99)
وَرَفَعَ أَبَوَيْهِ عَلَى ٱلْعَرْشِ وَخَرُّوا۟ لَهُۥ سُجَّدًا ۖ وَقَالَ يَـٰٓأَبَتِ هَـٰذَا تَأْوِيلُ رُءْيَـٰىَ مِن قَبْلُ قَدْ جَعَلَهَا رَبِّى حَقًّا ۖ وَقَدْ أَحْسَنَ بِىٓ إِذْ أَخْرَجَنِى مِنَ ٱلسِّجْنِ وَجَآءَ بِكُم مِّنَ ٱلْبَدْوِ مِنۢ بَعْدِ أَن نَّزَغَ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ بَيْنِى وَبَيْنَ إِخْوَتِىٓ ۚ إِنَّ رَبِّى لَطِيفٌ لِّمَا يَشَآءُ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ هُوَ ٱلْعَلِيمُ ٱلْحَكِيمُ (١٠٠)
And he seated his parents high upon the throne, and they fell down prostrate before him. He said, ‘Father! This is the fulfillment of my dream of long ago, which my Lord has made come true. He was certainly gracious to me when He brought me out of the prison and brought you over from the desert after that Satan had incited ill feeling between me and my brothers. Indeed my Lord is all-attentive in bringing about what He wishes. Indeed He is the All-knowing, the All-wise.’ (100)
۞ رَبِّ قَدْ ءَاتَيْتَنِى مِنَ ٱلْمُلْكِ وَعَلَّمْتَنِى مِن تَأْوِيلِ ٱلْأَحَادِيثِ ۚ فَاطِرَ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ أَنتَ وَلِىِّۦ فِى ٱلدُّنْيَا وَٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ ۖ تَوَفَّنِى مُسْلِمًا وَأَلْحِقْنِى بِٱلصَّـٰلِحِينَ (١٠١)
‘My Lord! You have granted me a share in the kingdom, and taught me the interpretation of dreams. Originator of the heavens and earth! You are my guardian in this world and the Hereafter! Let my death be in submission [to You], and unite me with the Righteous.’ (101)

Commentary

The Final Outcome of the Affairs of Joseph, Jacob, and the Brothers

Tafsir Nemouneh; Vol. 10; p. 78

With the arrival of the caravan carrying the greatest glad tidings from Egypt to Canaan and the restoration of the elderly Jacob’s sight, a great commotion arose in Canaan. The family that had not removed the garments of grief and sorrow for years was now drowned in joy and delight; they never concealed their satisfaction with all these Divine blessings.

Now, according to Joseph’s recommendation, this family had to move toward Egypt. Preparations for the journey were made in every respect. They mounted Jacob upon a beast of burden while his lips were busy with the remembrance and praise of Allah. The love of reunion had granted him such strength and vigor that it was as if he had become young again! This journey, unlike the previous trips of the brothers which were coupled with fear and anxiety, was void of any apprehension. Even if the journey itself held any hardship, such pain was insignificant compared to what awaited them at their destination: The longing for the Kaaba makes me run with such haste, That the desert thorns feel like silk beneath my feet!

They traveled slowly through the nights and days, for the yearning for reunion made every minute feel like a day or a year. But whatever it was, it passed, and the settlements of Egypt appeared from afar—Egypt with its lush green fields, sky-reaching trees, and beautiful buildings.

However, the Quran, as is its perpetual custom, omits these preliminary details—which can be understood with a little reflection—and at this stage says:

"When they entered into the presence of Joseph, he took his parents into his arms" (فَلَمَّا دَخَلُوا عَلى‌ يُوسُفَ آوى‌ إِلَيْهِ أَبَوَيْهِ‌).

The word "آوى" (āwā), as Raghib says in the book Al-Mufradat, originally means the joining of one thing to another. Joseph joining his parents to himself is a metaphor for embracing them.

Finally, the sweetest moment of Jacob’s life was realized. In this meeting and reunion, which occurred after years of separation, moments passed for Jacob and Joseph that none but Allah knows—what emotions they felt in those sweet moments, what tears of joy they shed, and what loving sighs they uttered.

Then Joseph "said to all: 'Enter Egypt, if Allah wills, in complete security'" for Egypt had become safe and peaceful under Joseph’s rule (وَ قالَ ادْخُلُوا مِصْرَ إِنْ شاءَ اللَّهُ آمِنِينَ‌).

From this sentence, it is understood that Joseph had come out to the city gates to welcome his parents. Perhaps from the phrase "entered into the presence of Joseph," which pertains to the area outside the gates, it can be inferred that he had ordered tents to be pitched there to provide initial hospitality for his parents and brothers.

When they entered Joseph’s palace, "he seated his parents upon the throne" (وَ رَفَعَ أَبَوَيْهِ عَلَى الْعَرْشِ‌).

The greatness of this Divine blessing and the depth of this gift and grace from the Lord so affected the brothers and the parents that "they all fell down before him in prostration" (وَ خَرُّوا لَهُ سُجَّداً).

At this moment, Joseph turned to his father "and said: 'O my father! This is the interpretation of my dream of old, which I saw when I was no more than a young child'" (وَ قالَ يا أَبَتِ هذا تَأْوِيلُ رُءْيايَ مِنْ قَبْلُ‌).

Is it not true that I saw in my dream the sun, the moon, and eleven stars prostrating before me?

See how, just as you predicted, "my Lord has made it a reality" (قَدْ جَعَلَها رَبِّي حَقًّا).

"And my Lord was indeed kind to me when He brought me out of the prison" (وَ قَدْ أَحْسَنَ بِي إِذْ أَخْرَجَنِي مِنَ السِّجْنِ‌).

It is interesting that regarding the hardships of his life, he only speaks of the Egyptian prison, but for the sake of his brothers, he said nothing of the well in Canaan! Then he added: "How kind Allah has been to me by bringing you from that desert of Canaan to here, after Satan had sown discord between me and my brothers" (وَ جاءَ بِكُمْ مِنَ الْبَدْوِ مِنْ بَعْدِ أَنْ نَزَغَ الشَّيْطانُ بَيْنِي وَ بَيْنَ إِخْوَتِي‌).

Here again, he shows another example of his magnanimity and nobility. Without saying who was at fault, he only mentions in a general way that Satan interfered in this matter and became the cause of corruption, because he did not want to complain about his brothers' past mistakes.

Describing the land of Canaan as a "desert" (badw) is also interesting and highlights the difference in civilization between Egypt and Canaan.

Finally, he says all these blessings are from Allah, "for my Lord is the Source of grace to whom He wills"; He directs the affairs of His servants and makes their difficulties easy (إِنَّ رَبِّي لَطِيفٌ لِما يَشاءُ).

He knows who is in need and who is worthy, "for He is the All-Knowing, the All-Wise" (إِنَّهُ هُوَ الْعَلِيمُ الْحَكِيمُ‌).

Then he turned toward the threshold of the True Master of the Kingdom and the Perpetual Benefactor, saying by way of gratitude and supplication: "My Lord! You have indeed bestowed upon me a portion of a vast kingdom" (رَبِّ قَدْ آتَيْتَنِي مِنَ الْمُلْكِ‌).

"And You have taught me the interpretation of dreams" (وَ عَلَّمْتَنِي مِنْ تَأْوِيلِ الْأَحادِيثِ‌). And what a transformation this seemingly simple knowledge brought about in my life and the lives of a great many of Your servants! How blessed is knowledge! "You are the One who originated and created the heavens and the earth" (فاطِرَ السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضِ‌).

And for this reason, everything is humble and submissive before Your power.

O Lord! "You are my Guardian, Helper, Director, and Protector in this world and the Hereafter" (أَنْتَ وَلِيِّي فِي الدُّنْيا وَ الْآخِرَةِ).

"Cause me to die as a Muslim, submissive to Your command" (تَوَفَّنِي مُسْلِماً) "and join me with the righteous" (وَ أَلْحِقْنِي بِالصَّالِحِينَ‌).

That is to say: I do not ask You for the permanence of the kingdom or the survival of my rule and material life, for these are all fleeting and only have an enchanting outward appearance. Rather, I ask You that my end and the conclusion of my affair be good, and that I die with faith and submission in Your way and for You, and be placed in the ranks of the righteous, the worthy, and Your sincere friends. These are what matter to me.

Key Points

1- Is Prostration to Other Than Allah Permissible?

As we stated in the first volume regarding the prostration of the angels to Adam (page 127), prostration in the sense of worship and adoration is exclusive to Allah. Worship is not permissible for anyone else in any religion. The "Oneness of Worship," which is a significant part of the issue of Monotheism (Tawhid) to which all prophets invited, means exactly this.

Therefore, neither Joseph, who was a Prophet of Allah, would allow them to prostrate to and worship him, nor would a great Prophet like Jacob undertake such an act, nor would the Quran mention it as a worthy or at least permissible deed.

Consequently, the said prostration was either for Allah (a prostration of thanks)—the same God who gave all these blessings and great status to Joseph and removed the hardships and troubles of Jacob’s family. In this case, while it was for Allah, because it was performed due to the greatness of Joseph’s blessing, it was also considered a gesture of honor and respect for him. From this perspective, the pronoun in "for him" (lahu), which certainly refers back to Joseph, would be perfectly consistent with this meaning.

Or, the meaning of prostration is its broad sense, namely humility and modesty, because prostration does not always carry its well-known technical meaning. Rather, it has sometimes been used to mean any type of humility. Thus, some commentators have said that the greeting and humility common in those days was bowing and honoring, and the "prostration" in the above verse refers to this.

However, considering the phrase "fell down" (kharru), which implies falling to the ground, it appears that their prostration was not merely bowing or lowering the head.

Some other great commentators have said that the prostration of Jacob, the brothers, and their mother was for Allah, but Joseph was the Qibla (direction), just like the Kaaba. Therefore, in Arabic expressions, it is sometimes said: "So-and-so prayed to the Qibla" [1].

[1]: Tafsir al-Mizan and Tafsir Fakhr al-Razi under the verse in question.

However, the first meaning seems closer to the truth, especially since we read in several narrations narrated from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt علیهم السلام: "Their prostration was for Allah" or "Worship for Allah" [1]. In some other hadiths, we read: "It was obedience to Allah and a greeting to Joseph" [2]. Just as in the story of Adam, the prostration was for that Great God who created such a marvelous creation; while being an act of worship to Allah, it was a proof of respect and the greatness of Adam’s status! This is exactly like when a person performs a very important and worthy task, and we prostrate to the God who created such a servant; the prostration is both for Allah and out of respect for this person.

2- The Whispers of Satan

The phrase "Satan had sown discord between me and my brothers"—considering that "نزغ" (nazgh) means entering into an affair with the intent of corruption and causing mischief—is proof that Satanic whispers always play an important role in such events. However, as we have said before, these whispers alone cannot achieve anything; the final decider is the human being himself. Rather, it is he who opens the doors of his heart to Satan and allows him entry. Therefore, no point contrary to the principle of free will can be derived from the above verse.

Furthermore, Joseph, with his nobility, high-mindedness, and magnanimity, did not want to make his brothers—who were already sufficiently ashamed—even more ashamed in this event. Thus, he made no mention of the final decider and only brought up the whispers of Satan, which were the secondary factor.

[1] and [2]: Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 2, pp. 467 and 468.

3- Security is a Great Blessing from Allah

From among all the gifts and blessings of Egypt, Joseph pointed to the issue of "security" and said to his parents and brothers: "Enter Egypt, for if Allah wills, you will be in security." This shows that the blessing of security is the root of all blessings. Truly it is so, for whenever security is lost, other welfare issues and material and spiritual blessings are also endangered. In an insecure environment, neither obedience to Allah is possible, nor a life coupled with dignity and peace of mind, nor effort, striving, and Jihad for the advancement of social goals.

This sentence may also implicitly point to the fact that Joseph wants to say: "The land of Egypt under my rule is not that land of the Pharaohs of yesterday; those autocracies, crimes, exploitations, suppressions, and tortures have all vanished. It is an environment of complete peace and security."

4- The Importance of the Status of Knowledge

Once again, at the end of his work, Joseph emphasizes the issue of the knowledge of dream interpretation. Alongside that great and undisputed kingdom, he places this seemingly simple knowledge, which signifies the greatest emphasis on the importance and impact of knowledge and science, even if it is a simple branch of knowledge. He says: "My Lord! You have indeed bestowed upon me a portion of the kingdom and taught me the interpretation of dreams."

5- The End of the Story

Throughout his life, a person may undergo many changes, but certainly, the final pages of his life are the most destiny-shaping, for the book of life ends with them, and the final judgment depends on them. Therefore, faithful and vigilant people always ask Allah that these pages of their lives be luminous and brilliant. Joseph also asks Allah for this here, saying: "Cause me to die as a Muslim and join me with the righteous."

The meaning of this statement is not a request for death from Allah, as Ibn Abbas imagined when he said: "No prophet asked for death from Allah except Joseph, who, when all the means of his government were gathered, the love and affection for the Lord flared up in his soul and he desired to meet the Lord." Rather, Joseph’s request was only a request for a condition and state; that is, he requested that at the time of death, he possess faith and Islam, just as Abraham and Jacob also gave this recommendation to their children, saying: "So do not die except as Muslims" (Al-Baqarah: 132).

Many commentators have also chosen this meaning.

6- Did Joseph’s Mother Come to Egypt?

From the apparent meaning of the above verses, it is clearly understood that Joseph’s mother was alive at that time and came to Egypt with her husband and children, and prostrated in gratitude for this blessing. However, some commentators insist that his mother, Rachel, had passed away and it was Joseph’s maternal aunt who came to Egypt and was considered in place of his mother.

But in the Book of Genesis of the Torah, Chapter 35, Verse 18, we read that Rachel passed away after Benjamin was born. In some narrations quoted from "Wahb ibn Munabbih" and "Ka'b al-Ahbar," this same meaning is mentioned, which seems to have been taken from the Torah.

In any case, we cannot ignore the apparent meaning of the Quranic verses, which say Joseph’s mother was alive that day, without definitive evidence, nor can we justify or reinterpret it otherwise.

7- Not Recounting the Past to the Father

In a narration from Imam al-Sadiq علیه السلام, we read that when Jacob met Joseph, he said to him: "My son, I wish to know exactly what the brothers did to you."

Joseph asked his father to overlook this matter, but Jacob adjured him to explain.

Joseph described a portion of the event to his father until he said: "The brothers took me and sat me on the edge of the well and commanded me: 'Take off your shirt!' I said to them: 'I adjure you by the respect of my father Jacob not to take the shirt off my body and not to make me naked.' One of them drew a knife he had with him and shouted: 'Take off your shirt!'" Upon hearing this sentence, Jacob could not bear it; he let out a cry and fainted. When he regained consciousness, he asked his son to continue his words, but Joseph said: "I adjure you by the God of Abraham, Ishmael, and Isaac to excuse me from this." When Jacob heard this, he desisted [1]. This shows that Joseph had no desire whatsoever to renew the bitter past in his own mind or his father’s, even though Jacob’s curiosity would not leave him at peace.

[1]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 5, p. 265.

Ayahs

ذَٰلِكَ مِنْ أَنۢبَآءِ ٱلْغَيْبِ نُوحِيهِ إِلَيْكَ ۖ وَمَا كُنتَ لَدَيْهِمْ إِذْ أَجْمَعُوٓا۟ أَمْرَهُمْ وَهُمْ يَمْكُرُونَ (١٠٢)
These are accounts from the Unseen, which We reveal to you, and you were not with them when they conspired together and schemed. (102)
وَمَآ أَكْثَرُ ٱلنَّاسِ وَلَوْ حَرَصْتَ بِمُؤْمِنِينَ (١٠٣)
Yet most people will not have faith, however eager you should be. (103)
وَمَا تَسْـَٔلُهُمْ عَلَيْهِ مِنْ أَجْرٍ ۚ إِنْ هُوَ إِلَّا ذِكْرٌ لِّلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (١٠٤)
You do not ask them any reward for it: it is just a reminder for all the nations. (104)
وَكَأَيِّن مِّنْ ءَايَةٍ فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ يَمُرُّونَ عَلَيْهَا وَهُمْ عَنْهَا مُعْرِضُونَ (١٠٥)
How many a sign there is in the heavens and the earth that they pass by while they are disregardful of it! (105)
وَمَا يُؤْمِنُ أَكْثَرُهُم بِٱللَّهِ إِلَّا وَهُم مُّشْرِكُونَ (١٠٦)
And most of them do not believe in Allah without ascribing partners to Him. (106)
أَفَأَمِنُوٓا۟ أَن تَأْتِيَهُمْ غَـٰشِيَةٌ مِّنْ عَذَابِ ٱللَّهِ أَوْ تَأْتِيَهُمُ ٱلسَّاعَةُ بَغْتَةً وَهُمْ لَا يَشْعُرُونَ (١٠٧)
Do they feel secure from being overtaken by a blanket punishment from Allah, or being overtaken by the Hour, suddenly, while they are unaware? (107)

Commentary

These Claimants are Mostly Polytheists!

With the conclusion of the story of Joseph, filled with all those lessons and instructive examples, and its precious and fruitful results—all free from any exaggeration or historical myths—the Quran turns its address to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and says: "This is of the news of the unseen which We reveal to you" (ذلِكَ مِنْ أَنْباءِ الْغَيْبِ نُوحِيهِ إِلَيْكَ).

"You were not with them when they put together their plan and while they were plotting" (وَ ما كُنْتَ لَدَيْهِمْ إِذْ أَجْمَعُوا أَمْرَهُمْ وَ هُمْ يَمْكُرُونَ).

Only Allah knows these intricacies, or someone who was present there; and since you were not present, it is only divine revelation that has placed such news at your disposal.

From here it becomes clear that although the story of Joseph is mentioned in the Torah and people in the environment of Hijaz likely had some information about it, the entire event—precisely and with all its subtleties and details, even what transpired in private gatherings, without any additions and free from all myths—was never known.

Nevertheless, upon seeing all these signs of revelation and hearing these divine exhortations, people ought to believe and return from the path of error. But, O Prophet, "Most of the people, however much you may desire it, will not believe" (وَ ما أَكْثَرُ النَّاسِ وَ لَوْ حَرَصْتَ بِمُؤْمِنِينَ).

The use of the term "حرص" (intense desire/greed) indicates the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله deep longing and eagerness for the people's faith. But to what avail? His insistence and eagerness alone were not enough; the readiness of the ground is also a condition. When the sons of Jacob, who were raised in the house of revelation and prophethood, became so entangled in the storms of whims and desires that they sought to destroy their own brother, how can one expect all people to overcome the demon of whim and the giant of lust and all at once turn completely toward Allah?

This sentence also serves as a kind of comfort and consolation for the Prophet, so that He never becomes exhausted or despondent by the people's insistence on disbelief and sin, nor becomes weary of the fewness of fellow travelers on this path. As we read in other verses of the Quran: "Then perhaps you would kill yourself through grief over them, if they do not believe in this message" (Al-Kahf: 6).

Then it adds: In reality, they have no excuse or pretext for rejecting Your call, because in addition to the signs of truth being clear within it, "You do not ask them for any reward for it" (وَ ما تَسْئَلُهُمْ عَلَيْهِ مِنْ أَجْرٍ) that they might use as an excuse for opposition. "It is but a reminder for the worlds" (إِنْ هُوَ إِلَّا ذِكْرٌ لِلْعالَمِينَ)—a general and universal call, and a spread table for the elite and the commonality, and all of humanity!

In reality, they have gone astray because they do not have open, seeing eyes or hearing ears. For this reason, "How many a sign there is in the heavens and the earth which they pass by, yet they turn away from it" (وَ كأَيِّنْ مِنْ آيَةٍ فِي السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضِ يَمُرُّونَ عَلَيْها وَ هُمْ عَنْها مُعْرِضُونَ).

The very events they witness every day with their own eyes: the sun rising from the eastern horizon in the morning, scattering its golden rays over mountains, valleys, deserts, and seas, and setting in the western horizon in the evening, as the heavy black curtain of night falls everywhere.

The secrets of this wondrous system, this rising and setting, this clamor of life in plants, birds, insects, and humans, the murmur of brooks, the whisper of the breeze, and all these amazing designs upon the doors and walls of existence, are so manifest that anyone who does not reflect upon them and their Creator is like a mere painting on a wall! There are many small and seemingly insignificant matters that we always pass by with indifference, but suddenly a profound scientist appears who, after months or years of study, discovers such wondrous secrets within them that the world's mouth stays open in amazement.

Fundamentally, the important thing is for us to know that in this universe, nothing is simple or insignificant, for all are the handiwork and creation of a God whose knowledge is infinite and whose wisdom is boundless. Simple and insignificant are those who consider the world insignificant and trivial.

Therefore, do not be surprised if they do not believe in the verses of the Quran revealed to you, for they have not believed in the signs of creation and nature that surround them from every side!

In the next verse, it adds that even those who do believe, the faith of most of them is not pure, "but is mixed with polytheism" (وَ ما يُؤْمِنُ أَكْثَرُهُمْ بِاللَّهِ إِلَّا وَ هُمْ مُشْرِكُونَ).

They might imagine themselves to be pure believers, but streaks of polytheism (shirk) are often present in their thoughts, words, and deeds.

Faith is not merely that a person believes in the existence of God; rather, a pure monotheist is one in whose heart and soul there is no object of worship in any form other than Allah. His speech is for Allah, his actions are for Allah, and every deed is performed for Him. He recognizes no law other than Allah's law, does not place the yoke of servitude to anyone else around his neck, and accepts divine commands with heart and soul, whether they align with his desires or not. At the crossroads of "God" and "whim," he always prioritizes God. This is pure faith, free from any polytheism: polytheism in belief, polytheism in speech, and polytheism in action.

Truly, if we were to make a precise calculation in this regard, true and pure monotheists are very few! For this reason, we read in Islamic narrations that Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام said:

"Polytheism is more hidden than the crawling of an ant." [1]

Or we read:

"The thing I fear most for you is the lesser polytheism. They said: 'What is the lesser polytheism, O Messenger of Allah?' He said: 'Riya (showing off). Allah Almighty will say on the Day of Resurrection when people come with their deeds: Go to those for whom you were showing off in the world, and see if you find any reward with them!'" [1]

It is narrated from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام regarding the commentary of the above verse that he said:

"It refers to polytheism in obedience and not polytheism in worship; and the sins that people commit are polytheism in obedience, for in them they obey Satan and thus associate a partner with Allah in obedience to someone else." [2]

In some other narrations, we read that it refers to "polytheism in blessings," meaning that a blessing from Allah reaches a person and he says, "This blessing reached me because of so-and-so; if it weren't for him, I would have died!" or "My life would have been ruined and I would have been miserable." [3] Here, he has considered someone other than Allah as a partner to Allah in granting sustenance and blessings.

In short, polytheism in the above verse does not mean formal disbelief, denial of God, or idol worship (as narrated from Imam Ali ibn Musa al-Ridha عليه السلام who said: "It is a polytheism that does not reach the level of disbelief"). However, polytheism in the broad sense of the word includes all of these.

In the final verse under discussion, it warns those who have not believed, who pass by the clear divine signs unawares, and who are polytheists in their actions: "Do they feel secure that a covering of Allah's punishment should not come upon them suddenly and without warning—an all-encompassing punishment that covers them all?"

[1]: Safinat al-Bihar, Vol. 1, p. 697. [2]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 2, p. 475; Usul al-Kafi, Vol. 2, p. 292. [3]: Ibid, p. 476.

(أَ فَأَمِنُوا أَنْ تَأْتِيَهُمْ غاشِيَةٌ مِنْ عَذابِ اللَّهِ).

"Or that the Hour should come upon them suddenly, and the great divine court be established to settle their accounts, while they are unaware and heedless?" (أَوْ تَأْتِيَهُمُ السَّاعَةُ بَغْتَةً وَ هُمْ لا يَشْعُرُونَ).

"غاشية" (Ghashiyah) means "coverer" or "covering," and among other things, it refers to the large cloth placed over a horse's saddle to cover it. Here, it refers to a calamity and punishment that encompasses all evildoers. [1]

"الساعة" (The Hour) refers to the Day of Resurrection, as it appears with this meaning in many other verses of the Quran. However, there is also the possibility that "the Hour" is a metaphor for terrifying events, as Quranic verses repeatedly state that the beginning of the Resurrection is accompanied by a series of extraordinary and terrifying events, such as earthquakes, storms, and lightning bolts; or it might refer to the hour of death. But the first interpretation seems closer.

[1]: The feminine form of "غاشية" is because it is an adjective for the word "عقوبة" (punishment) which is implied.

Ayahs

قُلْ هَـٰذِهِۦ سَبِيلِىٓ أَدْعُوٓا۟ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ ۚ عَلَىٰ بَصِيرَةٍ أَنَا۠ وَمَنِ ٱتَّبَعَنِى ۖ وَسُبْحَـٰنَ ٱللَّهِ وَمَآ أَنَا۠ مِنَ ٱلْمُشْرِكِينَ (١٠٨)
Say, ‘This is my way. I summon to Allah with insight—I and he who follows me. Immaculate is Allah, and I am not one of the polytheists.’ (108)
وَمَآ أَرْسَلْنَا مِن قَبْلِكَ إِلَّا رِجَالًا نُّوحِىٓ إِلَيْهِم مِّنْ أَهْلِ ٱلْقُرَىٰٓ ۗ أَفَلَمْ يَسِيرُوا۟ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ فَيَنظُرُوا۟ كَيْفَ كَانَ عَـٰقِبَةُ ٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِهِمْ ۗ وَلَدَارُ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ خَيْرٌ لِّلَّذِينَ ٱتَّقَوْا۟ ۗ أَفَلَا تَعْقِلُونَ (١٠٩)
We did not send [any apostles] before you except as men from among the people of the towns, to whom We revealed. Have they not travelled over the land so that they may observe how was the fate of those who were before them? And the abode of the Hereafter is surely better for those who are Godwary. Do you not exercise your reason? (109)
حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا ٱسْتَيْـَٔسَ ٱلرُّسُلُ وَظَنُّوٓا۟ أَنَّهُمْ قَدْ كُذِبُوا۟ جَآءَهُمْ نَصْرُنَا فَنُجِّىَ مَن نَّشَآءُ ۖ وَلَا يُرَدُّ بَأْسُنَا عَنِ ٱلْقَوْمِ ٱلْمُجْرِمِينَ (١١٠)
When the apostles lost hope and they thought that they had been told lies, Our help came to them, and We delivered whomever We wished, and Our punishment will not be averted from the guilty lot. (110)
لَقَدْ كَانَ فِى قَصَصِهِمْ عِبْرَةٌ لِّأُو۟لِى ٱلْأَلْبَـٰبِ ۗ مَا كَانَ حَدِيثًا يُفْتَرَىٰ وَلَـٰكِن تَصْدِيقَ ٱلَّذِى بَيْنَ يَدَيْهِ وَتَفْصِيلَ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ وَهُدًى وَرَحْمَةً لِّقَوْمٍ يُؤْمِنُونَ (١١١)
There is certainly a moral in their accounts for those who possess intellect. This [Quran] is not a fabricated discourse; rather, it is a confirmation of what was [revealed] before it, and an elaboration of all things, and a guidance and mercy for a people who have faith. (111)

Commentary

The Most Living Lessons of Admonition

In the first verse under discussion, the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله) is commanded to define his creed, method, and path. It says: "Say, 'This is my way; I invite to Allah (the One and Only God)'" (قُلْ هذِهِ سَبِيلِي أَدْعُوا إِلَى اللَّهِ).

Then it adds: "I do not travel this path without knowledge or out of blind imitation; rather, with insight and awareness, I and those who follow me invite all the people of the world to this way" (عَلى‌ بَصِيرَةٍ أَنَا وَ مَنِ اتَّبَعَنِي).

This sentence indicates that every Muslim who follows the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) is, in turn, an inviter toward the Truth and must call others to the path of Allah through both word and deed.

It also shows that a "leader" must possess sufficient insight, vision, and awareness; otherwise, his invitation will not truly be toward the Truth.

Then, for emphasis, it says: "Allah—that is, the One to whom I invite—is Pure and Exalted above any defect, deficiency, likeness, or partner" (وَ سُبْحانَ اللَّهِ).

Again, for further emphasis, it says: "I am not of the polytheists," and I shall never ascribe any partner or peer to Him (وَ ما أَنَا مِنَ الْمُشْرِكِينَ).

In fact, it is among the duties of a true leader to explicitly declare his programs and goals, ensuring that both he and his followers adhere to a single, defined, and clear path, rather than having their objectives and methods shrouded in a halo of ambiguity or each going their separate way.

Fundamentally, one of the ways to distinguish true leaders from false ones is that the former speak with clarity and their path is luminous, whereas the latter always resort to ambiguous and multi-faceted speech to cover up their true actions.

The placement of this verse following the verses of Yusuf is an indication that my (the Prophet's) way and tradition are not separate from the way and tradition of Yusuf, the great Prophet of God. He too, even in the corner of a prison, invited toward "Allah, the One, the Irresistible," and considered anything other than Him to be meaningless names inherited through the imitation of one ignorant person by another. Yes, my method and the method of all the Prophets is the same.

Since a constant objection of misguided and ignorant nations against the Prophets was: "Why are they human? Why was this duty not placed upon the shoulders of an angel?" and naturally, the people of the Age of Ignorance held the same objection against the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله) regarding his great invitation, the Holy Quran once again answers this objection: "We sent not before you [any messengers] except men to whom We revealed, from among the people of the townships" (وَ ما أَرْسَلْنا مِنْ قَبْلِكَ إِلَّا رِجالًا نُوحِي إِلَيْهِمْ مِنْ أَهْلِ الْقُرى).

They also lived in these very cities and settlements like other humans, moved among the people, and were well aware of their pains, needs, and problems.

The expression "مِنْ أَهْلِ الْقُرى" (from the people of the townships)—considering that "Qaryah" in the Arabic language refers to any city or inhabited settlement as opposed to "Badw" which refers to the desert—might implicitly suggest that the Divine Prophets never rose from among nomadic desert-dwellers (as some commentators have explicitly stated). This is because desert nomads are usually afflicted with ignorance and harshness, and have less awareness of the issues of life and spiritual or material needs.

It is true that in the land of Hijaz, there were many nomadic Arabs, but the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله) rose from Mecca, which was a relatively large city at that time. It is also true that the land of Canaan was of little significance compared to the land of Egypt where Yusuf ruled—which is why Yusuf referred to it as "Badw" (the desert)—but we know that the Prophet Ya'qub (عليه السلام) and his children were never nomads or desert-dwellers; rather, they lived in the small town of Canaan.

Then it adds that so they may know what the end of their opposition to your invitation—which is an invitation to Monotheism—will be, they should go and look at the remains of those before them: "Have they not traveled through the land and observed how was the end of those before them?" (أَ فَلَمْ يَسِيرُوا فِي الْأَرْضِ فَيَنْظُرُوا كَيْفَ كانَ عاقِبَةُ الَّذِينَ مِنْ قَبْلِهِمْ).

This "traveling through the land," observing the ruins of the predecessors, and the destruction of palaces and settlements crushed under the strikes of Divine punishment, provides them with the best lesson—a living, perceptible lesson that is tangible for everyone! At the end of the verse, He says: "And the home of the Hereafter is surely better for those who fear [Allah]" (وَ لَدارُ الْآخِرَةِ خَيْرٌ لِلَّذِينَ اتَّقَوْا).

"Do you not then reason and employ your thought and intellect?" (أَ فَلا تَعْقِلُونَ).

For this world is a transient abode mixed with all kinds of calamities, sorrows, and pains, while that world is an eternal home free from any suffering or discomfort.

In the next verse, it points to one of the most sensitive and critical moments in the lives of the Prophets, saying: "The Divine Prophets persisted in their path of inviting toward the Truth, and the misguided and rebellious nations continued their opposition until the Prophets despaired of them and thought that even the small group of believers had lied to them, and that they were completely alone in the path of their mission. At this moment, when their hope was cut off from everywhere, Our help and victory arrived, and We saved whom We willed and deemed worthy" (حَتَّى إِذَا اسْتَيْأَسَ الرُّسُلُ وَ ظَنُّوا أَنَّهُمْ قَدْ كُذِبُوا جاءَهُمْ نَصْرُنا فَنُجِّيَ مَنْ نَشاءُ) 1. And at the end of the verse, He says: "And Our punishment and retribution is not averted from the guilty and criminal people" (وَ لا يُرَدُّ بَأْسُنا عَنِ الْقَوْمِ الْمُجْرِمِينَ).

This is a Divine Sunnah (law): that after criminals persist in their ways, close all doors of guidance upon themselves, and in short, after the proof is completed, Divine punishments come for them, and no power is capable of repelling it.

Regarding the interpretation of the above verse and which group the phrase "ظَنُّوا أَنَّهُمْ قَدْ كُذِبُوا" (they thought they were lied to) describes, there is discussion among commentators.

What we mentioned above is the interpretation chosen by many great scholars of Tafsir, the summary of which is: the situation of the Prophets reached a point where they thought all people without exception would deny them, and even the group of believers who expressed faith were not steadfast in their conviction.

Another possibility given in the interpretation of the verse is that the subject of "ظَنُّوا" (they thought) is the believers. That is, the problems and crises were so intense that the believers thought: "Could it be that the promise of help and victory given by the Prophets was false?" Such suspicion and the resulting instability are not far-fetched for individuals who have recently embraced faith.

Some have also mentioned a third interpretation, summarized as follows: "The Prophets were undoubtedly human. When they found themselves in the most turbulent situations—where the knife reached the bone, all doors seemed closed, no way of relief appeared, the strikes of the storms of events continuously crushed them, and the cries of the believers whose patience had overflowed rang in their ears—yes, in this state, for a fleeting moment dictated by human nature, the thought involuntarily entered their minds: 'Could it be that the promise of victory will turn out to be false?' or 'Perhaps the promise of victory was conditional upon circumstances that have not been met.' However, they quickly overcame this thought, cast it away from their minds, the light of hope shone in their hearts, and following that, the harbingers of victory appeared."

Evidence for this interpretation is taken from verse 214 of Surah Al-Baqarah: "...until the Messenger and those who believed with him said, 'When is the help of Allah?'" Meaning: previous nations were so squeezed by poverty and hardship and were so shaken that their Prophet and those who believed with him cried out, "Where is Allah's help?" But they were answered: "Unquestionably, the help of Allah is near" (أَلا إِنَّ نَصْرُ اللَّهِ قَرِيبٌ).

However, a group of commentators, such as Tabarsi in Majma' al-Bayan and Fakhr Razi in Tafsir al-Kabir, after mentioning this possibility, considered it unlikely, as even this much is far from the station of the Prophets. In any case, the first interpretation is the most correct.

The final verse of this Surah contains a very comprehensive content, which summarizes all the discussions that passed in this Surah: "In the stories of Yusuf and his brothers, and the past Prophets and the believing and unbelieving nations, there are great lessons of admonition for all people of understanding" (لَقَدْ كانَ فِي قَصَصِهِمْ عِبْرَةٌ لِأُولِي الْأَلْبابِ). It is a mirror in which they can see the factors of victory and defeat, success and failure, happiness and misery, honor and humiliation—in short, whatever is valuable in human life and whatever is worthless. It is a mirror reflecting the essence of all the experiences of past nations and great leaders, a mirror whose observation extends the short lifespan of any human to the length of the lifespan of all humanity!

But only the Ulul-Albab (people of understanding) and those with intellect and thought have the ability to observe these patterns of admonition upon the surface of this wondrous mirror.

Following that, it adds: "What has been told was not a fabricated tale or an imaginary and false story" (ما كانَ حَدِيثاً يُفْتَرى).

These verses revealed to you, which have pulled back the curtain from the true history of the predecessors, are not the product of your own mind and thought, "but rather a great heavenly revelation, which also confirms and testifies to the original books of the previous Prophets" (وَ لكِنْ تَصْدِيقَ الَّذِي بَيْنَ يَدَيْهِ).

Furthermore, "everything that man needs and that plays a role in his happiness and perfection has been mentioned in these verses" (وَ تَفْصِيلَ كُلِّ شَيْ‌ءٍ).

And for this reason, it is "a source of guidance for seekers and a source of mercy for all those who believe" (وَ هُدىً وَ رَحْمَةً لِقَوْمٍ يُؤْمِنُونَ).

The above verse seemingly wants to point to this important fact: that fabricated stories, beautiful and enchanting as they may be, are many, and there have always been many interesting imaginary legends among all nations. Let no one imagine that the story of Yusuf or the stories of other Prophets mentioned in the Quran are of this kind.

The important point is that these admonitory and soul-stirring stories are all the very essence of reality, and there is not the slightest deviation from reality or external objectivity in them. For this reason, their impact is extraordinarily great.

For we know that no matter how interestingly and movingly imaginary legends are constructed, their impact is negligible compared to a true story because:

Firstly, when the listener or reader reaches the most exciting moments of the story and is about to be moved, suddenly the realization flashes in his mind that this is nothing more than a fantasy and an imagination! Secondly, these stories actually reflect the mind of their designer; it is he who embodies the essence of his thoughts and desires in the face and actions of the story's hero. Therefore, it is nothing beyond the thought of a human being, and this differs greatly from an objective reality and cannot be more than the preaching and advice of its speaker. But the real history of humans is not like this; it is fruitful, blessed, and path-clearing from every aspect.

End of Surah Yusuf

O Lord! Grant us a seeing eye, a hearing ear, and a knowing heart, so that we may find in the stories of the predecessors the ways of our own salvation from the problems in which we are currently immersed. O Allah! Give us a keen vision to see the end of the lives of nations who, after victory, were afflicted with the most painful defeats due to discord and division, and let us not travel the path they took. O God! Grant us such a pure intention that we may step upon the head of the demon of the self, such knowledge that we do not become arrogant with victory, and such selflessness that if another can perform a task better than us, we entrust it to him. For if You grant us these, we can overcome all problems and keep the lamp of Islam and the Quran bright and alive in the world.
